The critical engineering concept is the **Mechanical Advantage (MA)** of the push-rim-to-wheel system:

$$
MA = \frac{D_{pushrim}}{D_{wheel}} = \frac{\text{Push-rim diameter}}{\text{Driven wheel diameter}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **smaller push-rim relative to the wheel** (e.g., 0.85 ratio) gives **more mechanical advantage** (easier to push, but slower). A **larger push-rim** (e.g., 0.95 ratio) gives **less mechanical advantage** (harder to push, but faster). This is a **classic KTU design-tradeoff question**.

### 2.3 Rolling Resistance — The Energy Thief

Rolling resistance is the force that opposes motion when a wheel rolls on a surface. For a wheelchair on a flat surface:

$$
F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot W
$$

Where:
- $F_{rr}$ = rolling resistance force (N)
- $\mu_{rr}$ = coefficient of rolling resistance (dimensionless)
- $W$ = weight on the wheel (N)

> Typical values: $\mu_{rr} \approx 0.015$ for **pneumatic tires on smooth floor**; $\mu_{rr} \approx 0.04$ for **solid tires on carpet**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical Advantage (Push-rim) | $MA = D_{rim} / D_{wheel}$ | Ease of propulsion |
| Rolling Resistance | $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot W$ | Energy loss to deformation/friction |
| Slope Climbing Force | $F_{slope} = W \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | Force needed to climb incline $\theta$ |
| Total Propulsion Force | $F_{total} = F_{rr} + F_{slope} + F_{acc}$ | Sum of all resistive forces |
| Static Stability Angle | $\tan(\phi) = T / H$ | $\phi$ = tip angle, T = track width, H = center of mass height |
| Push Frequency | $f_{push} = v / (2\pi r_{eff})$ | Strokes per second for velocity $v$ |
| Energy per Push | $E_{push} = F_{push} \cdot d_{contact}$ | Energy delivered per stroke |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NEVER** use the pipe symbol `\|` inside markdown table cells. The above table uses LaTeX vertical bars only inside math mode where they render correctly.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Manual wheelchair design directly impacts:
1. **Shoulder injury prevention** — high push frequency and high push force cause rotator cuff tears in long-term users.
2. **Pressure ulcer prevention** — proper cushion selection reduces hospital readmissions (a **$26 billion** annual cost globally).
3. **Independence and dignity** — the right wheelchair can be the difference between community participation and social isolation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mechanical Advantage of Push-Rim Propulsion

**Problem Statement:** A manual wheelchair has a drive wheel diameter of **610 mm (24 inches)** and a push-rim diameter of **530 mm**. Calculate the mechanical advantage of the propulsion system.

**Step 1:** State the given data.
- Drive wheel diameter: $D_{wheel} = 610$ mm
- Push-rim diameter: $D_{rim} = 530$ mm

**Step 2:** Write the formula for mechanical advantage.

$$
MA = \frac{D_{rim}}{D_{wheel}}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values into the formula.

$$
MA = \frac{530 \text{ mm}}{610 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 4:** Perform the division.

$$
MA = 0.8689 \approx 0.87
$$

**Step 5:** Interpret the result.

> Since **MA < 1**, the system provides a **mechanical disadvantage** in terms of force — meaning the user must apply **more force on the push-rim** than the linear force at the wheel contact patch. However, this trade-off yields **greater linear velocity** per unit angular displacement of the push-rim, which is desirable for active users.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Rolling Resistance on a Flat Surface

**Problem Statement:** A manual wheelchair with a total loaded weight of **800 N** (user + chair) is being pushed on a smooth indoor floor. If **$\mu_{rr} = 0.015$** for pneumatic tires on a smooth surface, calculate the rolling resistance force.

**Step 1:** Identify the parameters.
- Total weight: $W = 800$ N
- Coefficient of rolling resistance: $\mu_{rr} = 0.015$

**Step 2:** State the formula.

$$
F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot W
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values.

$$
F_{rr} = 0.015 \times 800 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the result.

$$
F_{rr} = 12 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5:** Interpret the result.

> A force of just **12 N** is required to overcome rolling resistance on a smooth floor. This is roughly equivalent to **lifting a 1.2 kg mass** — a useful reference for KTU estimation problems.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Slope Climbing Force Requirement

**Problem Statement:** The same wheelchair (W = 800 N) must climb a ramp of **5 degrees**. Calculate the additional force component required to overcome the gravitational slope force (ignoring rolling resistance).

**Step 1:** State the formula for slope force.

$$
F_{slope} = W \cdot \sin(\theta)
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the parameters.

$$
F_{slope} = 800 \text{ N} \times \sin(5^\circ)
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the sine function.

$$
\sin(5^\circ) = 0.0872
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply.

$$
F_{slope} = 800 \times 0.0872 = 69.7 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5:** Interpret the result.

> The user must apply **~70 N** of additional force to climb a 5° ramp. For reference, **ADA (Americans with Disabilities Act) standards** require public ramps to be no steeper than **4.8° (1:12 slope)**, which corresponds to $\sin(4.8^\circ) = 0.0837$ and $F_{slope} \approx 67$ N. This is why **ADA-compliant ramps** are critical for manual wheelchair users.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Total Propulsion Force on an Incline

**Step 1:** State the total force equation.

$$
F_{total} = F_{rr} + F_{slope} + F_{acc}
$$

**Step 2:** For constant velocity on a 5° incline (so $F_{acc} = 0$):

$$
F_{total} = 12 \text{ N} + 69.7 \text{ N} + 0
$$

**Step 3:** Sum the values.

$$
F_{total} = 81.7 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpret the result.

> The user must exert **~82 N** continuously to maintain motion up a 5° slope. **Long-term manual wheelchair users** typically generate peak push forces of **200–300 N**, so this is well within the user's capacity, but **fatigue accumulates** over time.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Wheelchair Selection Decision Tree

| User Characteristic | Recommended Wheelchair Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Elderly, short-term, no customization** | Standard depot folding | Cost-effective, low customization |
| **Active adult, long-term use, self-propels** | Ultralight rigid | Energy efficiency, low shoulder injury risk |
| **Spinal cord injury, complete paralysis** | Ultralight rigid + ROHO cushion | Pressure relief + propulsion efficiency |
| **Cerebral palsy, poor trunk control** | Tilt-in-space + postural supports | Postural management over mobility |
| **Hemiplegia (one-sided paralysis)** | One-arm-drive wheelchair | Single-side lever propulsion |
| **Obese user (BMI > 35)** | Bariatric reinforced | Load capacity ≥ 150 kg |
| **Pediatric with growth potential** | Growth-adjustable chair | Frame extends as child grows |
| **Amputee (above-knee)** | Rear-wheel axle set back | Improves stability (compensates for lost leg mass) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Manual Wheelchair Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Upper Limb Input] --> B[Push-rim Grip and Contact]
    B --> C[Angular Force on Push-rim]
    C --> D[Axle Hub Force Transfer]
    D --> E[Drive Wheel Rotation]
    E --> F[Ground Contact Friction]
    F --> G[Linear Forward Propulsion]

    H[Frame Subsystem] --> I[Chassis Support]
    I --> J[Postural Stability]
    J --> K[Energy Transfer Efficiency]

    L[Caster Wheel Subsystem] --> M[Steering and Turning]
    M --> N[Maneuverability]

    O[Seat Cushion Subsystem] --> P[Pressure Redistribution]
    P --> Q[Decubitus Ulcer Prevention]

    G --> R[User Mobility Output]
    K --> R
    N --> R
    Q --> R
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Wheelchair Prescription Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Patient Assessment] --> A1{Ambulatory Status}
    A1 -->|Non-ambulatory| B1{Duration of Need}
    A1 -->|Short-term| S1[Standard Depot Wheelchair]

    B1 -->|Less than 3 months| S1
    B1 -->|More than 3 months| C1{User Activity Level}

    C1 -->|Sedentary| S2[Lightweight Folding]
    C1 -->|Active| D1{User Postural Control}

    D1 -->|Good trunk control| S3[Ultralight Rigid]
    D1 -->|Poor trunk control| S4[Tilt-in-Space Reclining]

    S3 --> E1{User Weight Class}
    E1 -->|Standard| F1[Aluminum or Titanium Frame]
    E1 -->|Bariatric| F2[Reinforced Steel Frame]

    F1 --> End[Final Prescription with Measurements]
    F2 --> End
    S2 --> End
    S4 --> End
    S1 --> End
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Manual Wheelchair Component Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FrameSystem[Frame Subsystem]
        F1[Cross-brace Folding or Rigid Weldment]
        F2[Material Selection Steel Aluminum Titanium Carbon]
    end

    subgraph WheelSystem[Wheel and Propulsion Subsystem]
        W1[Drive Wheel 24 inch Standard]
        W2[Push-rim Diameter 0.85 to 0.95 of Wheel]
        W3[Caster Wheel 5 to 8 inch]
        W4[Tire Type Pneumatic or Solid Polyurethane]
    end

    subgraph PostureSystem[Postural Support Subsystem]
        P1[Seat Sling or Rigid]
        P2[Backrest Low or High]
        P3[Cushion Foam Gel Air Honeycomb]
    end

    subgraph AccessorySystem[Accessories Subsystem]
        A1[Armrest Desk Full Flip-back]
        A2[Footrest Swing-away Elevating]
        A3[Wheel Locks and Anti-tip Devices]
    end

    FrameSystem --> WheelSystem
    FrameSystem --> PostureSystem
    FrameSystem --> AccessorySystem
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Propulsion Biomechanics Chain

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Hand
    participant R as Push-rim
    participant A as Axle Hub
    participant W as Drive Wheel
    participant G as Ground

    U->>R: Apply tangential force F
    R->>R: Generate torque T = F times radius
    R->>A: Transfer torque to axle
    A->>W: Rotate wheel at angular velocity omega
    W->>G: Apply vertical load and traction
    G->>W: Generate reaction force
    W->>U: Propel chair forward at velocity v
    Note over U,G: Energy chain from muscular to mechanical to kinetic
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a manual wheelchair. List any four components of a manual wheelchair with their function.**

**Model Answer:**

A manual wheelchair is a manually-propelled mobility device used by individuals with ambulatory limitations, providing postural support and mobility. It is typically operated via push-rims or attendant-propulsion handles and conforms to ISO 7176 standards.

**Four key components:**
1. **Frame** — provides structural support, supports user weight, and defines the wheelchair's geometry.
2. **Drive wheels (rear wheels)** — enable forward propulsion via push-rim contact with the user's hands.
3. **Caster wheels (front wheels)** — provide steering, balance, and directional control.
4. **Seat cushion** — redistributes pressure to prevent pressure ulcers (decubitus).

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a folding wheelchair and a rigid-frame wheelchair.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Folding Wheelchair | Rigid-Frame Wheelchair |
|---|---|---|
| **Frame Design** | Cross-brace X-frame, collapsible | One-piece welded, non-collapsible |
| **Portability** | High (folds for car transport) | Low (requires disassembly) |
| **Energy Transfer** | Lower (frame flex loses energy) | Higher (rigid frame = direct energy transfer) |
| **Weight** | Generally heavier (12–18 kg) | Generally lighter (8–12 kg) |
| **Propulsion Efficiency** | Lower | Higher |
| **Maintenance** | More moving parts, more maintenance | Fewer parts, lower maintenance |
| **Best For** | Occasional users, transport needs | Active long-term users |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often **omit the clinical justification** when discussing wheelchair selection. Marks are awarded for **linking engineering specifications to user needs** (e.g., why a tilt-in-space is prescribed for poor trunk control). Always provide the **biomechanical or clinical rationale** behind every prescription decision.

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the different categories of manual wheelchairs based on user needs, frame material, and weight. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the biomechanics of push-rim propulsion in a manual wheelchair. With a suitable diagram, derive the relationship between push-rim diameter, drive wheel diameter, and mechanical advantage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Manual wheelchairs can be categorized along three principal axes:

**1. Based on User Need and Duration:**
- **Standard/Depot wheelchairs** — institutional use, short-term, weight > 15 kg, low cost.
- **Lightweight wheelchairs** — intermediate users, weight 12–15 kg, partial customization.
- **Ultralight wheelchairs** — long-term active users, weight < 12 kg, fully customized (e.g., Quickie, TiLite).
- **Bariatric wheelchairs** — users > 100 kg, reinforced frame, wider seat.
- **Pediatric wheelchairs** — growth-adjustable frames, smaller dimensions.
- **Sports wheelchairs** — designed for basketball, racing, rugby; aggressive camber angles (up to 20°).
- **Reclining/Tilt-in-Space** — for users needing postural support, pressure relief, or with hypotension.

**2. Based on Frame Material:**
- **Steel** — heaviest, cheapest, durable, used in depot chairs.
- **Aluminum alloy** — lightweight, mid-cost, common in lightweight and ultralight chairs.
- **Titanium** — light, corrosion-resistant, vibration-damping, premium cost.
- **Carbon-fiber composite** — lightest, stiffest, highest cost, used in elite sports chairs.

**3. Based on Frame Configuration:**
- **Folding** — cross-brace X-frame, portable, lower energy transfer.
- **Rigid** — fixed-frame, better energy transfer, lower long-term shoulder injury risk.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Biomechanics of Push-Rim Propulsion:**

The push-rim is a smaller-diameter ring mounted concentrically on the drive wheel. The user grips the push-rim and applies a tangential force, generating a torque. This torque is transferred through the axle hub to the drive wheel, which rolls on the ground via frictional contact, producing linear forward motion.

**Forces involved:**
- **Hand contact force** ($F_h$) applied tangentially to the push-rim.
- **Torque on push-rim**: $T_{rim} = F_h \times r_{rim}$
- **Same torque transferred to drive wheel**: $T_{wheel} = F_h \times r_{rim}$
- **Linear force at wheel contact patch**: $F_{wheel} = T_{wheel} / r_{wheel} = (F_h \times r_{rim}) / r_{wheel}$

**Derivation of Mechanical Advantage:**

The mechanical advantage is defined as the ratio of the output force (at the wheel contact patch) to the input force (at the push-rim):

$$
MA = \frac{F_{wheel}}{F_h} = \frac{F_h \cdot r_{rim}/r_{wheel}}{F_h} = \frac{r_{rim}}{r_{wheel}} = \frac{D_{rim}}{D_{wheel}}
$$

**Result:** $MA = D_{rim} / D_{wheel}$

**Interpretation:**
- If $MA = 1$ → push-rim and wheel are the same diameter (no mechanical advantage).
- If $MA < 1$ → push-rim is smaller than wheel → **mechanical disadvantage** for force, but **mechanical advantage for speed** (each push travels farther). This is the typical configuration for active users.
- If $MA > 1$ → push-rim is larger than wheel → **mechanical advantage for force** (easier to push), but **slower**. Used in geriatric or weak-user chairs.

**Diagram:** (Refer to SECTION 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram for visual representation of the propulsion chain.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the key measurements required for proper wheelchair fitting (seat width, seat depth, seat height, back height, armrest height, and footrest length). Explain the clinical significance of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manual wheelchair with loaded weight 900 N is pushed up a 6° incline. The coefficient of rolling resistance is 0.02. Calculate: (i) Rolling resistance force, (ii) Slope climbing force, (iii) Total propulsion force required at constant velocity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Proper wheelchair fitting is critical to **prevent secondary complications** such as pressure ulcers, postural deformities, shoulder injuries, and reduced propulsion efficiency. The following measurements are essential:

**1. Seat Width:**
- Measured as the **broadest point of the user's hips** + 2–3 cm clearance.
- **Too narrow:** causes pressure on greater trochanters, skin breakdown.
- **Too wide:** poor postural support, reduced propulsion efficiency (arms must reach outward).

**2. Seat Depth:**
- Measured from the **posterior buttock to the popliteal fossa** − 5 cm (to avoid pressure behind the knee).
- **Too long:** pressure behind the knees, popliteal vessel compression.
- **Too short:** insufficient thigh support, weight concentrated on ischial tuberosities → pressure ulcer risk.

**3. Seat Height (Seat-to-Floor):**
- Determined by the **user's leg length + desired foot-propulsion clearance**.
- Too low → shoulder strain during propulsion.
- Too high → feet cannot reach the floor for foot propulsion.

**4. Back Height:**
- Low-back (below scapulae) for active users with good trunk control.
- High-back (above scapulae) for users with poor trunk control.
- **Clinical significance:** back height affects upper-limb range of motion during propulsion.

**5. Armrest Height:**
- Measured with the user seated, **elbow flexed at 90°**, allowing 2.5 cm clearance between forearm and armrest.
- **Too high:** shoulder elevation → shoulder pain.
- **Too low:** forward lean → poor posture, reduced trunk stability.

**6. Footrest Length:**
- Measured with the user's thigh parallel to the seat pan; foot should rest fully on footplate.
- **Too long:** leg elevation → hamstring tightness.
- **Too short:** excessive thigh pressure → ischial pressure concentration.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Total loaded weight: $W = 900$ N
- Incline angle: $\theta = 6°$
- Coefficient of rolling resistance: $\mu_{rr} = 0.02$
- Constant velocity: $a = 0$, so $F_{acc} = 0$

**(i) Rolling Resistance Force:**

$$
F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot W = 0.02 \times 900 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{rr} = 18 \text{ N}
$$

**(ii) Slope Climbing Force:**

$$
F_{slope} = W \cdot \sin(\theta) = 900 \text{ N} \times \sin(6°)
$$

$$
\sin(6°) = 0.1045
$$

$$
F_{slope} = 900 \times 0.1045 = 94.05 \text{ N}
$$

**(iii) Total Propulsion Force at Constant Velocity:**

$$
F_{total} = F_{rr} + F_{slope} + F_{acc}
$$

$$
F_{total} = 18 \text{ N} + 94.05 \text{ N} + 0
$$

$$
F_{total} = 112.05 \text{ N}
$$

**Result:** The user must exert a continuous force of **~112 N** to maintain constant velocity up a 6° incline. This is within typical manual wheelchair user capacity but contributes to **upper-limb fatigue** over prolonged use, supporting the case for powered wheelchairs on steep inclines.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- ✅ **Definition:** A manual wheelchair is a human-powered mobility device per **ISO 7176** standards.
- ✅ **Frame types:** Folding (portable, less efficient) vs. Rigid (efficient, less portable).
- ✅ **Frame materials:** Steel (heavy, cheap) → Aluminum (mid) → Titanium (light, vibration-damping) → Carbon-fiber (premium).
- ✅ **Wheel sizes:** Drive wheel = **24 inch** (610 mm) standard; Caster = **5–8 inch**.
- ✅ **Push-rim MA:** $MA = D_{rim}/D_{wheel}$; typically **0.85–0.95** (MA < 1, speed-favored).
- ✅ **Rolling resistance:** $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot W$; use $\mu_{rr} \approx 0.015$ for pneumatic on smooth floor.
- ✅ **Slope force:** $F_{slope} = W \cdot \sin(\theta)$.
- ✅ **ADA standard:** Maximum public ramp slope = **1:12 = 4.8°**.
- ✅ **Fitting measurements:** Seat width, depth, height, backrest height, armrest height, footrest length — all critical for pressure ulcer and postural deformity prevention.
- ✅ **Cushion types:** Foam, Gel, Air (ROHO), Honeycomb (Stimulite) — used for pressure redistribution.
- ✅ **Clinical linkage:** Always justify wheelchair selection with **user activity level, postural control, weight, and duration of need**.
- ✅ **Common exam trap:** Students forget to **convert degrees to radians** OR **forget to add the rolling resistance** to the slope force. Total force is **the sum** of all resistive components.
- ✅ **Standards to memorize:** **ISO 7176** (international), **RESNA** (North American), **ANSI/RESNA WC** (US testing standards), **IS 14614** (Indian Standard).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Power Wheelchairs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT753 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Electric Power Wheelchairs # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **Electric Power Wheelchair (EPW)**, also termed a **Powered Wheelchair (PWC)** or **Motorized Wheelchair**, is a wheeled mobility device propelled by one or more electric motors, powered by a rechargeable battery system, and controlled by the user through an electronic input interface (typically a joystick, sip-and-puff, head array, or switch array). EPWs are prescribed assistive technologies classified under **ISO 9999 — Class 12 22 06** and are engineered to provide independent, self-directed mobility for individuals with severe mobility impairments who cannot self-propel a manual wheelchair. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEBMT753 – Module 3):** > Electric Power Wheelchairs are explicitly listed as a core sub-topic under *Mobility Aids and Assistive Devices*. Students must be able to differentiate drive configurations (front, mid, rear), describe the electromechanical subsystems, and explain performance parameters such as range, speed, and grade-climbing ability. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of an Electric Power Wheelchair as a **"mini electric car shrunk to human scale"** — but with one major difference: the steering is done by varying the speeds of the left and right drive wheels independently, much like a **tank** (skid-steer principle). Just as a Toyota Prius has an accelerator pedal, brake, and steering wheel, an EPW has a **joystick**, **controller electronics**, **batteries**, and **drive motors** — but the "steering" is achieved by sending more power to one wheel than the other, causing the chair to turn in that direction. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Definition (Board-Exam Ready):** > An Electric Power Wheelchair is a battery-powered mobility device that uses differential-speed control of two or more motor-driven wheels to achieve both **locomotion** and **steering** through a single user-operated input device. ## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Parameters The following standard specifications are universally used to classify EPWs in both clinical prescription and KTU board examinations: - **Maximum Speed:** Typically **6 km/h** (standard) up to **15 km/h** (high-performance outdoor) - **Range per Charge:** **15–40 km** depending on battery, terrain, and user weight - **Battery Voltage:** **12 V, 24 V, or 36 V** deep-cycle lead-acid / gel / **Li-ion** systems - **Motor Power:** **2 × 150 W to 2 × 450 W** permanent-magnet DC or brushless DC motors - **User Weight Capacity:** Standard **113 kg (250 lbs)**, bariatric models up to **270 kg** - **Drive Wheel Configurations:** Front-Wheel Drive (FWD), Mid-Wheel Drive (MWD), Rear-Wheel Drive (RWD) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architectural Breakdown of an EPW An Electric Power Wheelchair is composed of **five major subsystems**, each engineered for a specific role: ### 2.1.1 Power Subsystem - **Battery Pack:** The energy reservoir. Two **12 V, 35–75 Ah** deep-cycle batteries connected in series produce a nominal **24 V** DC bus. - **Battery Charger:** Off-board or on-board **constant-voltage / constant-current (CV/CC)** charger. - **Circuit Breaker / Fuse:** A **50 A** automatic reset breaker protects the motor bus from short-circuit faults. ### 2.1.2 Drive Subsystem - **Drive Motors:** Two independent **permanent-magnet DC (PMDC)** or **brushless DC (BLDC)** gearmotors, one per drive wheel. - **Gear Reduction:** A **planetary or worm gearhead** (ratio 10:1 to 30:1) multiplies motor torque and reduces output speed. - **Drive Wheels:** Pneumatic or solid tyres mounted directly to the motor output shaft. ### 2.1.3 Control Subsystem - **Joystick (Input Transducer):** A two-axis Hall-effect potentiometer; deflection in X commands turn rate, in Y commands speed. - **Power Module (Controller):** A **PWM-driven MOSFET/IGBT H-bridge** for each motor. Accepts low-power joystick signal and modulates high-current motor supply. - **Microcontroller / Logic Board:** Stores driving profiles, monitors battery state-of-charge, and enforces safety limits. ### 2.1.4 Chassis / Frame Subsystem - **Base Frame:** Steel or aluminium rectangular structure housing motors, batteries, and electronics. - **Drive Wheel Position:** Determines FWD / MWD / RWD classification. - **Caster Wheels (Anti-tip):** Small passive wheels providing stability and preventing rearward tip-over. ### 2.1.5 Seating & Postural Subsystem - **Captain's Seat / Rehab Seat:** Either a basic sling-style captain's chair or a custom-molded rehab seat. - **Power Seating Functions (Optional):** Tilt, recline, seat-elevate, leg-elevate. ## 2.2 Drive Configurations — A Comparative Analysis ### 2.2.1 Rear-Wheel Drive (RWD) - **Drive wheels** are located at the **rear**; casters at the front. - **Advantage:** Excellent stability at high speeds, predictable straight-line tracking. - **Disadvantage:** Large turning radius (~1100 mm), prone to "fish-tailing" on uneven terrain. ### 2.2.2 Mid-Wheel Drive (MWD) - **Drive wheels** are located **directly under the user's centre of gravity**. - **Advantage:** Tightest turning radius (~600 mm), most intuitive steering — feels like the user "pivots in place." - **Disadvantage:** "Caster flutter" can occur on rough surfaces; requires a sixth anti-tip wheel. ### 2.2.3 Front-Wheel Drive (FWD) - **Drive wheels** at the front, casters at the rear. - **Advantage:** Superior curb-climbing, excellent outdoor / rough-terrain performance. - **Disadvantage:** Counterintuitive steering — front wheels pull rather than push. ## 2.3 Principle of Differential Steering (The "How" Behind the Motion) A power wheelchair turns by **differential speed control** between the left and right drive wheels — exactly the same principle used in **skid-steer loaders** and **tank tracks**. The instantaneous centre of rotation (ICR) is computed as follows: - If both wheels rotate at **equal speed and direction** → the wheelchair travels in a **straight line**. - If the **right wheel slows** (or stops) while the left wheel continues → the wheelchair **turns right**. - If the two wheels rotate in **opposite directions at equal speed** → the wheelchair **pivots in place** about its geometric centre. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does this matter?** > This is the **single most examinable concept** in EPWs. Almost every KTU Part B question will require the student to explain the differential-speed steering principle and relate it to a drive-configuration diagram. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter / Concept | Formula or Expression | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Motor Output Torque | $T_{out} = T_{motor} \times G \times \eta_{gear}$ | N·m | $G$ = gear ratio, $\eta_{gear}$ = gear efficiency (~0.85) | | 2 | Wheel Angular Speed | $\omega_{wheel} = \dfrac{v}{r_{wheel}}$ | rad/s | $v$ = linear speed (m/s), $r$ = wheel radius (m) | | 3 | Required Push Force (Slope) | $F_{grade} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | N | $m$ = total mass (kg), $\theta$ = slope angle | | 4 | Rolling Resistance | $F_{roll} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos\theta$ | N | $C_{rr}$ = rolling-resistance coefficient (~0.015) | | 5 | Total Tractive Force | $F_{total} = F_{grade} + F_{roll} + F_{aero}$ | N | $F_{aero}$ negligible at EPW speeds | | 6 | Required Motor Power | $P = F_{total} \cdot v$ | W | Must be doubled for two-motor system | | 7 | Battery Energy Capacity | $E = V_{bus} \times C_{Ah} \times DoD$ | Wh | $DoD$ = depth-of-discharge (typically 0.8) | | 8 | Estimated Range | $R = \dfrac{E \cdot \eta_{sys}}{P_{draw}}$ | km | $\eta_{sys}$ = total drivetrain efficiency | | 9 | Grade Climb Max | $\theta_{max} = \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{T_{total}}{m \cdot g \cdot r}\right)$ | degrees | Static-torque limit condition | | 10 | Turning Radius (RWD) | $R_{turn} = \dfrac{L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{\vert v_R - v_L \vert}$ | m | $L$ = wheelbase, $v_R$, $v_L$ = wheel speeds | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The drivetrain principles underlying EPWs are **directly applied** in: - **Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs)** in factories - **Mars Exploration Rovers** (Curiosity, Perseverance) — which use rocker-bogie + differential drive - **Warehouse AMRs** (Autonomous Mobile Robots) by Amazon, MiR - **Smart electric wheelchairs** with LIDAR-based obstacle avoidance > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** > A KTU examiner will reward students who can connect EPW mechanics to broader **mobile-robotics** concepts. This cross-disciplinary link demonstrates Outcome-Based Education (OBE) mastery of the CO4 (Design and analyse rehabilitation devices) course outcome. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Motor Torque Required to Climb a Specified Grade **Problem Setup (Typical KTU 14-mark question):** A power wheelchair of total mass **$m = 100$ kg** (user + chair) must climb a **$8^\circ$** ramp. The drive-wheel radius is **$r = 0.16$ m**, and the rolling-resistance coefficient is **$C_{rr} = 0.015$**. The gearhead ratio is **$G = 20\!:\!1$**, with **$\eta_{gear} = 0.85$**. Calculate the **minimum torque each motor** must deliver (assume two-wheel drive, equal load sharing). ### Step 1 — Resolve the forces on the slope The wheelchair experiences two mechanical resistances on an incline: 1. A **gravitational component** pulling it down the slope. 2. A **rolling-resistance force** from tyre deformation. ### Step 2 — Write the gravitational force along the slope The component of weight acting parallel to the slope surface is: $$ F_{grade} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta $$ Substituting the numerical values: $$ F_{grade} = 100 \times 9.81 \times \sin(8^\circ) $$ Since $\sin(8^\circ) = 0.1392$: $$ F_{grade} = 100 \times 9.81 \times 0.1392 = 136.5 \;\text{N} $$ ### Step 3 — Write the rolling-resistance force The normal force on the slope is reduced by the cosine of the angle: $$ F_{roll} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos\theta $$ Substituting values: $$ F_{roll} = 100 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 \times \cos(8^\circ) $$ Since $\cos(8^\circ) = 0.9903$: $$ F_{roll} = 100 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 \times 0.9903 = 14.57 \;\text{N} $$ ### Step 4 — Sum the two resistance components The total tractive force that both drive wheels together must produce is: $$ F_{total} = F_{grade} + F_{roll} = 136.5 + 14.57 = 151.07 \;\text{N} $$ ### Step 5 — Convert tractive force to wheel torque The torque required at the wheel axle is: $$ T_{wheel} = F_{total} \cdot r $$ Substituting: $$ T_{wheel} = 151.07 \times 0.16 = 24.17 \;\text{N·m} $$ ### Step 6 — Distribute the load between two motors With two identical motors sharing the load equally: $$ T_{wheel,\,per\,motor} = \dfrac{T_{wheel}}{2} = \dfrac{24.17}{2} = 12.09 \;\text{N·m} $$ ### Step 7 — Back-calculate the required motor (input) torque The gearhead multiplies the motor torque. To find the motor-side torque, we divide by the gear ratio and the gear efficiency: $$ T_{motor} = \dfrac{T_{wheel,\,per\,motor}}{G \cdot \eta_{gear}} $$ Substituting: $$ T_{motor} = \dfrac{12.09}{20 \times 0.85} = \dfrac{12.09}{17.0} = 0.711 \;\text{N·m} $$ ### Step 8 — Final Answer $$ \boxed{\,T_{motor} \;\approx\; 0.71\;\text{N·m per motor (input shaft)}\,} $$ **Verification:** The motor must supply **~0.71 N·m**, which is realistic for a 150–200 W PMDC gearmotor. ✓ ## 3.2 Derivation: Range of an EPW on a Single Battery Charge **Given:** - Battery: **$V_{bus} = 24$ V**, **$C_{Ah} = 75$ Ah**, depth-of-discharge $DoD = 0.8$ - Total system efficiency **$\eta_{sys} = 0.65$** (controller + motor + gearhead combined) - Average power draw **$P_{draw} = 180$ W** (typical for mixed indoor/outdoor use) ### Step 1 — Calculate the usable battery energy $$ E_{battery} = V_{bus} \times C_{Ah} \times DoD $$ Substituting: $$ E_{battery} = 24 \times 75 \times 0.8 = 1440 \;\text{Wh} $$ ### Step 2 — Apply the system-efficiency factor Only a fraction of the stored energy reaches the wheels: $$ E_{usable} = E_{battery} \times \eta_{sys} = 1440 \times 0.65 = 936 \;\text{Wh} $$ ### Step 3 — Divide by the average power draw to get the operating time $$ t = \dfrac{E_{usable}}{P_{draw}} = \dfrac{936}{180} = 5.2 \;\text{hours} $$ ### Step 4 — Convert time to range using the nominal speed At an average speed of **$v = 6$ km/h** (standard EPW cruise): $$ R = v \times t = 6 \times 5.2 = 31.2 \;\text{km} $$ $$ \boxed{\,\text{Estimated Range} \;\approx\; 31\;\text{km}\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Tip:** > In KTU board exams, when range is asked, always state your **assumed speed** explicitly. Marks are deducted when the student computes a range number without showing the unit-time conversion step. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Drive Configurations | Performance Criterion | RWD (Rear-Wheel Drive) | MWD (Mid-Wheel Drive) | FWD (Front-Wheel Drive) | |---|---|---|---| | **Turning Radius** | Largest (~1100 mm) | Smallest (~600 mm) | Moderate (~900 mm) | | **Steering Intuition** | Moderate | Best (pivots on user) | Worst (front wheels pull) | | **Straight-Line Stability** | Best | Good | Good | | **Curb Climbing** | Good | Moderate | Best | | **Rough-Terrain Performance** | Moderate | Poor (caster flutter) | Best | | **Anti-Tip Wheel Required** | Optional | Mandatory (6th wheel) | Optional | | **Typical Use Case** | Standard indoor/outdoor | Tight indoor spaces | Active outdoor users | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EPW ```mermaid flowchart LR A[User Input
Joystick or Sip-and-Puff] --> B[Controller Module
Microcontroller + Logic] B --> C[Power Module
PWM H-Bridge Driver] C --> D1[Left Drive Motor
PMDC Gearmotor] C --> D2[Right Drive Motor
PMDC Gearmotor] D1 --> E1[Left Drive Wheel] D2 --> E2[Right Drive Wheel] F[Battery Pack
24V Deep Cycle] --> C F --> B G[Caster Wheels
Stability Support] -.-> H[Wheelchair Frame] E1 --> H E2 --> H H --> I[Seating System
Captain or Rehab Seat] B --> J[Display Panel
Battery and Status LEDs] B --> K[Safety Cutoff
50A Circuit Breaker] ``` ## 4.2 Drive-Configuration Wheel-Layout Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FWD[Front-Wheel Drive Layout] A1[Front Drive Wheels] --- B1[User Seat Position] --- C1[Rear Caster Wheels] end subgraph MWD[Mid-Wheel Drive Layout] A2[Front Caster Wheels] --- B2[User Seat Position] --- C2[Rear Caster Wheels] A2 --- D2[Mid Drive Wheels] end subgraph RWD[Rear-Wheel Drive Layout] A3[Front Caster Wheels] --- B3[User Seat Position] --- C3[Rear Drive Wheels] end ``` ## 4.3 Differential Steering State Diagram ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Straight Straight --> TurnRight: Left wheel faster than right Straight --> TurnLeft: Right wheel faster than left TurnRight --> Straight: Equal speeds TurnLeft --> Straight: Equal speeds TurnRight --> PivotInPlace: Equal opposite speeds TurnLeft --> PivotInPlace: Equal opposite speeds PivotInPlace --> Straight: User releases joystick to center ``` ## 4.4 Closed-Loop Speed Control Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR SetPoint[Joystick Setpoint
Desired Speed] --> Sum[Summing Junction] Sum --> Ctrl[PI Controller
in Power Module] Ctrl --> PWM[PWM H-Bridge
Motor Driver] PWM --> Motor[Drive Motor] Motor --> Wheel[Drive Wheel] Wheel --> Encoder[Optical Encoder
Tachogenerator] Encoder --> Feedback[Feedback Signal] Feedback --> Sum Battery[24V Battery Pack] --> PWM ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define an Electric Power Wheelchair. List the three standard drive-wheel configurations used in EPWs.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An Electric Power Wheelchair (EPW) is a battery-powered mobility device for users with severe locomotor impairment, employing two or more electric motors and an electronic controller for self-propelled, joystick-controlled movement. The three standard drive configurations are: **(i) Front-Wheel Drive (FWD)** — drive wheels at the front; **(ii) Mid-Wheel Drive (MWD)** — drive wheels under the user's centre of gravity; and **(iii) Rear-Wheel Drive (RWD)** — drive wheels at the rear. *(1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks)* ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is differential steering? How is turning achieved in a two-motor EPW?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Differential steering is the principle by which a vehicle steers by varying the rotational speeds of its left and right drive wheels. In a two-motor EPW, **turning is achieved by sending different PWM duty cycles** to the left and right motor H-bridges. If the right motor slows (or reverses) while the left motor continues, the EPW turns right; if both motors rotate at equal and opposite speeds, the chair pivots in place about its geometric centre. *(1 + 2 = 3 marks)* ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Set A]` **(a)** Describe the major **subsystems of an Electric Power Wheelchair** with a neat block diagram. Explain the function of each subsystem. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare **Front-Wheel, Mid-Wheel, and Rear-Wheel Drive** configurations in terms of turning radius, stability, curb-climbing ability, and clinical use cases. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a): A complete EPW consists of **five subsystems**: 1. **Power Subsystem** (1 mark): Rechargeable battery pack (typically 2 × 12 V, 35–75 Ah deep-cycle lead-acid or Li-ion), a CV/CC charger, and a 50 A circuit breaker for overcurrent protection. 2. **Drive Subsystem** (1 mark): Two independent PMDC/BLDC gearmotors (150–450 W each) with planetary gear reduction (10:1 to 30:1) coupled to drive wheels. 3. **Control Subsystem** (2 marks): Joystick (Hall-effect 2-axis transducer), power module (PWM-driven MOSFET/IGBT H-bridge), and microcontroller enforcing safety profiles, acceleration ramps, and battery management. 4. **Chassis Subsystem** (1 mark): Steel/aluminium frame supporting drive wheels, caster wheels (for stability), and the seating platform. 5. **Seating Subsystem** (1 mark): Captain's seat or custom rehab seat with optional powered functions — tilt, recline, seat-elevate, leg-elevate. 6. **Block diagram** (1 mark): As shown in Section 4.1 of these notes. #### Model Solution — Part (b): | Parameter | FWD | MWD | RWD | |---|---|---|---| | Turning Radius | Moderate (~900 mm) | Smallest (~600 mm) | Largest (~1100 mm) | | Stability on Slopes | Good | Excellent (CoG over drive wheels) | Excellent | | Curb Climbing | Best (drive wheels push up) | Moderate | Good | | Intuition of Steering | Counter-intuitive | Most intuitive | Moderate | | Clinical Use Case | Active outdoor users | Indoor tight spaces, paediatric | Standard indoor/outdoor | *(1 mark for each correct row + 1 mark for clinical-application summary = 7 marks)* > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > A common mistake students make in Part (a) is **listing components** (e.g., "joystick, motor, battery") without categorising them into the **five engineering subsystems**. Examiners allocate marks by *subsystem*, not by individual part. Failing to structure the answer into subsystems can cost **2–3 marks**. In Part (b), students often confuse the **turning radius order** — remember: MWD < FWD < RWD. --- ### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Set B]` *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **differential-speed steering principle** used in electric wheelchairs. Derive the expression for the **turning radius** in terms of left and right wheel speeds. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An EPW with total mass **$m = 120$ kg** is required to climb a **$10^\circ$** ramp at a constant speed of **$1.5$ m/s**. The drive-wheel diameter is **$320$ mm**, the coefficient of rolling resistance is **$0.018$**, and the gearhead ratio is **$25\!:\!1$** with efficiency **$0.88$**. Calculate the **minimum torque each motor** must deliver and the **total power** required at the wheels. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a): **Differential-Speed Steering Principle** (3 marks): A two-motor EPW has its left and right wheels driven independently. By varying the relative speeds, the wheelchair can: - Travel in a straight line (equal speeds, same direction). - Execute a curve (one wheel faster than the other). - Pivot in place (equal speeds, opposite directions). **Derivation of Turning Radius** (4 marks): Let $L$ be the wheelbase (distance between left and right drive wheels), $v_L$ and $v_R$ be the linear velocities of the left and right wheels. The instantaneous centre of rotation (ICR) lies on the line joining the two wheel contact points, perpendicular to the wheelbase. The angular velocity of the chair about the ICR is: $$ \omega = \dfrac{v_R - v_L}{L} $$ The distance from the ICR to the right wheel is: $$ R_R = \dfrac{v_R}{\omega} = \dfrac{v_R \cdot L}{v_R - v_L} $$ Similarly, the distance to the left wheel is: $$ R_L = \dfrac{v_L \cdot L}{v_R - v_L} $$ The **turning radius of the chair's centre** is the average of these two distances: $$ R_{turn} = \dfrac{R_R + R_L}{2} = \dfrac{L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L} $$ $$ \boxed{\,R_{turn} = \dfrac{L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{\vert v_R - v_L \vert}\,} $$ **Special Cases:** - If $v_R = v_L$ → $R_{turn} \to \infty$ (straight line). - If $v_R = -v_L$ → $R_{turn} = 0$ (pivot in place). #### Model Solution — Part (b): **Step 1 — Force on the grade:** $$ F_{grade} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta = 120 \times 9.81 \times \sin(10^\circ) $$ Since $\sin(10^\circ) = 0.1736$: $$ F_{grade} = 120 \times 9.81 \times 0.1736 = 204.4 \;\text{N} $$ **Step 2 — Rolling resistance force:** $$ F_{roll} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos\theta = 120 \times 9.81 \times 0.018 \times \cos(10^\circ) $$ Since $\cos(10^\circ) = 0.9848$: $$ F_{roll} = 120 \times 9.81 \times 0.018 \times 0.9848 = 20.86 \;\text{N} $$ **Step 3 — Total tractive force:** $$ F_{total} = 204.4 + 20.86 = 225.26 \;\text{N} $$ **Step 4 — Wheel torque (total, both wheels):** $$ T_{wheel,\,total} = F_{total} \times r = 225.26 \times 0.16 = 36.04 \;\text{N·m} $$ **Step 5 — Per-motor wheel torque:** $$ T_{wheel,\,per} = \dfrac{36.04}{2} = 18.02 \;\text{N·m} $$ **Step 6 — Per-motor input torque (motor shaft):** $$ T_{motor} = \dfrac{T_{wheel,\,per}}{G \times \eta_{gear}} = \dfrac{18.02}{25 \times 0.88} = \dfrac{18.02}{22.0} = 0.819 \;\text{N·m} $$ **Step 7 — Total power at the wheels:** $$ P_{wheels} = F_{total} \times v = 225.26 \times 1.5 = 337.9 \;\text{W} $$ $$ \boxed{\,T_{motor} \;\approx\; 0.82\;\text{N·m per motor} \quad\text{and}\quad P_{wheels} \;\approx\; 338\;\text{W}\,} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to halve the wheel torque** when computing per-motor torque (loses 1 mark). > 2. **Including drivetrain efficiency in the wrong direction** — it always **reduces** motor-side output for a given wheel-side input (or **increases** motor-side input for a given wheel-side output). > 3. **Mixing up units** — the rolling-resistance force requires the full **$m \cdot g$** (not just the user's mass) and is multiplied by $\cos\theta$, **not** $\sin\theta$. > 4. **Forgetting to write the units** — a 14-mark question without units will lose **1–2 marks** even if the calculation is correct. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Electric Power Wheelchairs:** - **Definition:** EPW = battery-powered, motor-driven, joystick-controlled mobility device for users unable to self-propel a manual chair. - **Three Drive Configurations:** FWD, MWD, RWD. **MWD has the smallest turning radius**; **FWD has the best curb-climbing**; **RWD has the best straight-line stability**. - **Steering Principle:** **Differential-speed control** between two independent drive motors — same as tank/skid-steer robotics. - **Standard Battery:** **24 V** nominal bus (two **12 V** deep-cycle batteries in series), capacity **35–75 Ah**, **DoD = 0.8** typical. - **Standard Motors:** Two **PMDC or BLDC gearmotors**, **150–450 W** each, with **planetary gearhead** (10:1–30:1) and efficiency ~0.85. - **Formula to Memorise:** - $F_{grade} = m g \sin\theta$ - $F_{roll} = m g C_{rr} \cos\theta$ - $T_{wheel} = F \times r$ - $T_{motor} = T_{wheel} / (G \times \eta)$ - $R_{turn} = (L/2) \cdot (v_R + v_L) / \vert v_R - v_L \vert$ - $E_{battery} = V \times C_{Ah} \times DoD$ - **Subsystems (5):** Power, Drive, Control, Chassis, Seating — examiners expect this structure. - **Safety Components:** **50 A circuit breaker**, **joystick-centre deadband**, **acceleration ramps** in the controller firmware. - **Standards:** ISO 9999 class **12 22 06**, FDA Class I medical device, ANSI/RESNA WC-1 / WC-2 performance standards. - **Clinical Decision Rule:** RWD for outdoor speed, MWD for tight indoor manoeuvring, FWD for active users who climb curbs frequently. - **Cross-Curricular Link:** The differential-drive principle is shared with **AGVs, AMRs, and Mars Rovers** — mention this in answers for higher-order (Apply/Analyse) marks.

topics.title: Multifunctional Wheelchairs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT753 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multifunctional Wheelchairs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Multifunctional Wheelchair** is a powered, mechatronically integrated mobility-assistive device that extends beyond the basic functions of conventional manual or powered wheelchairs. It incorporates multiple biomechanical, sensory, and intelligent control subsystems to enable the user to perform activities such as **standing, stair climbing, reclining, tilt-in-space, all-terrain navigation, and autonomous/semi-autonomous mobility**, thereby addressing Activities of Daily Living (ADL) and Instrumental ADL (IADL) requirements of individuals with severe locomotor disabilities.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with Rehabilitation Engineering, a multifunctional wheelchair is classified as an **Assistive Technology Device (ATD)** under the WHO-GATE 5P framework (People, Policy, Products, Provision, Personnel) and is engineered under the **Universal Design** and **Design for All (DfA)** philosophy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Multifunctional wheelchairs form the bridge between *conventional mobility aids* and *robotic rehabilitation systems*. They are assessed under Course Outcome **CO3** of PEBMT753 — *Design and evaluate assistive mobility systems for persons with disabilities.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a standard car versus a modern **SUV with 4-wheel drive, hill-descent control, automatic transmission, and reclining seats**:

| Standard Wheelchair | Multifunctional Wheelchair |
|---|---|
| Works only on smooth, flat floors (like a city car on a smooth road) | Works on stairs, slopes, grass, gravel (like an SUV) |
| Fixed sitting posture | Adjustable sitting, standing, reclining, tilt |
| User-driven manually or single motor | Multiple coordinated motors + sensors + controllers |
| Single input (push rim or joystick) | Multiple input modes (joystick, sip-and-puff, voice, head array) |

**Plain English Intuition:** A multifunctional wheelchair is essentially a *small, personal, electric vehicle tailored to a disabled user's body* — it does not just move them from Point A to B; it changes their body posture, navigates obstacles, and in some cases, *thinks* for them using sensors.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **Payload capacity:** Typically **100–150 kg** (user + accessories)
- **Operating speed:** **5–10 km/h** (indoor), **up to 15 km/h** (outdoor)
- **Climbing angle (gradient):** **0° to 12°** (≈ 20% slope) for standard; **up to 35°–45°** for stair-climbers
- **Battery voltage:** **24 V DC** (most common), sometimes **48 V DC** for high-power units
- **Stair riser height compatibility:** **150–200 mm** (standard architectural riser)
- **Battery range:** **15–30 km** per charge cycle
- **Turning radius:** **0.5–1.2 m**

> [!NOTE]
> **Disability Coverage:** Multifunctional wheelchairs primarily serve persons with **spinal cord injury (SCI) at C5–L1**, **muscular dystrophy**, **cerebral palsy**, **poliomyelitis**, **lower-limb amputation**, and **geriatric mobility impairment**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Classification of Multifunctional Wheelchairs

KTU Module 3 identifies **seven major functional categories** of multifunctional wheelchairs. Each category is described below with operational logic, enabling technology, and clinical use case.

### 2.1.1 Standing Wheelchairs

- **Function:** Lifts the user from a seated to a fully upright standing posture using linear actuators (electric or hydraulic).
- **Mechanism:** A scissor-lift or four-bar linkage, driven by 1–2 linear actuators, raises the seat platform while footrests, knee blocks, and chest/torso support stabilize the user.
- **Why needed:** Pressure sore prevention (off-loading the ischial tuberosities), improved circulation, bone density retention, psychological well-being, eye-level social interaction, and reach-to-grasp tasks.
- **Engineering challenge:** Maintaining **center of gravity (CoG)** within the wheelbase when the user stands. A standing wheelchair with a 1.5 m tall standing user has its CoG at ~1.0 m height — a toppling moment of ~750 N·m for a 75 kg user.

### 2.1.2 Stair-Climbing Wheelchairs

- **Function:** Ascends and descends standard architectural stairs without external lift infrastructure.
- **Technologies used:**
  * **Tracked systems** (continuous rubber tracks with sprockets) — e.g., TopChair-S
  * **Articulated leg/wheel hybrids** (wheel pairs that rotate and step) — e.g., iBOT
  * **Star-wheel / polygonal wheel clusters** — e.g., the TopChair series
  * **Self-balancing two-wheeled** — e.g., Model S-CE from Scalevo

### 2.1.3 Reclining / Tilt-in-Space Wheelchairs

- **Tilt-in-space:** Rotates the entire seat-back assembly about a fixed pivot (typically near the user's hip joint) without changing hip/knee angles. **Range: 0°–45° tilt.**
- **Reclining:** Changes the seat-to-back angle. **Range: 90°–180° (or beyond to fully supine).**
- **Why needed:** Pressure redistribution, postural drainage, rest, catheterization, and management of orthostatic hypotension.

### 2.1.4 All-Terrain / Outdoor Wheelchairs

- Uses **pneumatic wide tires**, **suspension systems**, **4WD drivetrains**, and **higher ground clearance** (≥ 100 mm).

### 2.1.5 Power-Assist Wheelchairs

- Hybrid: manual chair retrofitted with **brushless DC hub motors** on the wheels; provides 2×–4× assistance based on torque sensor input from the push rim.

### 2.1.6 Smart / Robotic Wheelchairs

- Adds **perception (ultrasonic, LiDAR, IR)**, **localization (encoders, IMU)**, and **planning (path-planning algorithms)** to a powered wheelchair. Examples: **Smart Wheelchair of KTU/CMU lineage**, **Snoezelen wheelchairs**, **Wheelchair Pilot 2020 (WP20)**.

### 2.1.7 Modular / Configurable Wheelchairs

- Wheelbase, seat width, armrests, footrests, and controller mounting can be swapped/adjusted without tools — supports pediatric growth, progressive disease, and multi-user environments.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations are essential for solving numerical problems on multifunctional wheelchair design in KTU ESE.

| **#** | **Parameter / Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Engineering Meaning** |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|---|
| 1 | Pulling / driving force on a slope | $F = m \cdot g \cdot (\sin\theta + \mu \cos\theta)$ | N | Force required from motor to climb incline $\theta$ with rolling friction coefficient $\mu$ |
| 2 | Gravitational Potential Energy for stair climb | $E_{PE} = m \cdot g \cdot h$ | J (or Wh) | Energy stored in battery to lift user to step height $h$ |
| 3 | Motor torque at wheel | $T = F \cdot r$ | N·m | Torque needed at wheel radius $r$ |
| 4 | Motor power (linear motion) | $P = F \cdot v$ | W | Power for translation at velocity $v$ |
| 5 | Motor power (rotational) | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | W | Power = torque × angular velocity |
| 6 | Battery energy capacity | $E_{bat} = V \cdot I \cdot t = V \cdot C_{Ah}$ | Wh | Usable stored energy |
| 7 | Range of wheelchair | $R = \dfrac{E_{bat} \cdot \eta_{total}}{F_{drag} + F_{roll}}$ | m | Total distance per charge |
| 8 | Stability against tipping on slope | $\tan\theta_{max} = \dfrac{d}{2h_{CoG}}$ | dimensionless | Critical tipping angle; $d$ = track width, $h_{CoG}$ = CoG height |
| 9 | Actuator force (standing) | $F_{act} = \dfrac{m \cdot g \cdot h_{stand}}{\eta \cdot L_{arm}}$ | N | Force in linear actuator of effective arm $L_{arm}$ with efficiency $\eta$ |
| 10 | Recharge time | $t_{chg} = \dfrac{C_{Ah}}{I_{chg}}$ | h | Time to fully charge battery |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these formulas are used:** $F = mg(\sin\theta + \mu\cos\theta)$ is the most frequently asked numerical in KTU ESE for "calculate force/power/battery range of a powered wheelchair on an inclined surface." Always draw a **free-body diagram** and identify which forces are doing positive vs. negative work.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Clinical Rehabilitation:** Restores verticality in SCI patients, reducing decubitus ulcer incidence by ~30% (per WHO Rehabilitation 2030 data).
- **Disaster Management:** Stair-climbing wheelchairs are deployed in earthquake-prone regions of **Kerala, Japan, and Nepal** for evacuating mobility-impaired persons from multi-storey buildings.
- **Inclusive Tourism:** All-terrain wheelchairs enable beach, forest, and snow mobility (e.g., **Beachwheelchair** design by Motivation Charitable Trust).
- **Smart Cities:** Robotic wheelchairs integrate with **IoT-based navigation** in airports (Singapore Changi, Bengaluru T2) for passenger assistance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Force and Power Required to Climb a Stair

**Problem Setup:** A multifunctional wheelchair with mass $m_w = 40$ kg carries a user of mass $m_u = 75$ kg. The wheel radius is $r = 0.15$ m. The wheelchair must climb a stair of riser height $h = 0.18$ m in time $t = 4$ s. Compute (a) the required motor torque, and (b) the required motor power. Assume efficiency $\eta = 0.85$.

### Step 1: Total mass to be lifted

$$
m_{total} = m_w + m_u = 40 + 75 = 115 \text{ kg}
$$

**Textual logic:** The actuator must lift the *combined* mass of chair and user, since both are translated vertically together.

### Step 2: Gravitational force opposing lift

$$
F_g = m_{total} \cdot g = 115 \times 9.81 = 1128.15 \text{ N}
$$

**Textual logic:** On Earth, every kilogram experiences a downward pull of 9.81 N — the wheelchair must overcome this during vertical rise.

### Step 3: Effective force at the actuator (accounting for efficiency)

$$
F_{act} = \dfrac{F_g}{\eta} = \dfrac{1128.15}{0.85} = 1327.24 \text{ N}
$$

**Textual logic:** Because no mechanism is 100% efficient, 15% of the input energy is lost to friction in joints, gears, and motor windings. We must therefore apply *more* force than the ideal 1128.15 N.

### Step 4: Motor torque at the wheel

$$
T = F_{act} \cdot r = 1327.24 \times 0.15 = 199.09 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Textual logic:** Torque is force × moment arm. The wheel radius acts as the moment arm; a smaller wheel needs *more* torque to deliver the same force.

### Step 5: Vertical velocity during the climb

$$
v = \dfrac{h}{t} = \dfrac{0.18}{4} = 0.045 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Textual logic:** Vertical lift of 18 cm distributed over 4 s gives an average vertical speed of 4.5 cm/s.

### Step 6: Required motor power

$$
P = F_{act} \cdot v \cdot \dfrac{1}{\eta} = 1327.24 \times 0.045 \times \dfrac{1}{0.85}
$$

**Textual logic:** Power = force × velocity, but we again divide by efficiency because the stated velocity is the *output*, not the motor shaft velocity.

### Step 7: Numerical evaluation

$$
P = \dfrac{1327.24 \times 0.045}{0.85} = \dfrac{59.726}{0.85} = 70.27 \text{ W}
$$

**Final Answer:** Motor torque ≈ **199.1 N·m**; Motor power ≈ **70.3 W** (≈ 0.094 hp — easily supplied by a 24 V, 5 A DC gear motor).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Battery Range on a Horizontal Floor

**Problem:** Same wheelchair, with a $V = 24$ V battery rated $C_{Ah} = 35$ Ah. Rolling friction coefficient $\mu_r = 0.02$. Total system efficiency $\eta_{total} = 0.75$. Average speed $v = 1.4$ m/s. Find the **range in km**.

### Step 1: Total available battery energy

$$
E_{bat} = V \cdot C_{Ah} = 24 \times 35 = 840 \text{ Wh}
$$

**Textual logic:** A 24 V battery delivering 35 A for one hour stores 840 Watt-hours of chemical energy.

### Step 2: Usable mechanical energy (after efficiency)

$$
E_{mech} = E_{bat} \cdot \eta_{total} = 840 \times 0.75 = 630 \text{ Wh}
$$

**Textual logic:** Only 75% of the electrical energy becomes mechanical work at the wheels; the rest is lost as heat in motor windings, gear mesh, and rolling resistance.

### Step 3: Rolling resistance force

$$
F_{roll} = \mu_r \cdot m_{total} \cdot g = 0.02 \times 115 \times 9.81 = 22.56 \text{ N}
$$

**Textual logic:** Rolling friction acts opposite to motion. With a rubber tire on smooth tile, $\mu_r \approx 0.02$.

### Step 4: Power demand at the chosen speed

$$
P_{demand} = F_{roll} \cdot v = 22.56 \times 1.4 = 31.59 \text{ W}
$$

**Textual logic:** This is the *output* power needed at the wheels to overcome rolling friction. Note: the motor must supply $\dfrac{31.59}{0.75} = 42.1$ W to deliver 31.59 W mechanically.

### Step 5: Time of travel

$$
t = \dfrac{E_{mech}}{P_{demand}} = \dfrac{630}{31.59} = 19.94 \text{ h}
$$

### Step 6: Range (distance)

$$
R = v \cdot t = 1.4 \times 19.94 = 27.92 \text{ km}
$$

**Final Answer:** Approximately **27.9 km** on a single charge — consistent with the marketed 25–30 km range of mid-tier powered wheelchairs.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Safe Slope (Tipping Stability)

**Problem:** Track width of a wheelchair $d = 0.55$ m. With the user seated, the CoG height above ground is $h_{CoG} = 0.65$ m. At what slope angle will the chair tip sideways?

### Step 1: Critical tipping equation

$$
\tan\theta_{max} = \dfrac{d/2}{h_{CoG}} = \dfrac{0.275}{0.65}
$$

**Textual logic:** A chair tips when the vertical line from the CoG falls outside the wheelbase. The right triangle formed has horizontal leg = half track width and vertical leg = CoG height.

### Step 2: Numerical evaluation

$$
\tan\theta_{max} = 0.4231
$$

### Step 3: Critical angle in degrees

$$
\theta_{max} = \arctan(0.4231) = 22.93^{\circ}
$$

**Final Answer:** The chair begins to tip at slopes greater than **≈ 23°** (≈ 42% grade). For safety, manufacturers typically rate a chair for slopes up to **half** this angle, i.e., **~10°–12°**, to include a margin for bumps, accelerations, and user motion.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Functional Specifications

| **Parameter** | **Standing WC** | **Stair-Climbing WC** | **Tilt/Recline WC** | **Smart/Robotic WC** | **All-Terrain WC** |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Primary function | Posture change | Vertical obstacle | Pressure relief | Autonomous nav | Rough surface |
| Number of actuators | 1–2 | 2–4 | 1–3 | 2 (drive) + 1 (steer) | 2 (drive) |
| Sensors | Tilt, position | IMU, contact | Tilt encoder | LiDAR, US, IMU, Camera | None/IMU |
| Battery voltage (V) | 24 | 24–48 | 24 | 24 | 24–36 |
| Climbing angle | 6°–10° | 35°–45° (stair) | 6°–10° | 6°–10° | 15°–25° |
| User-control modes | Joystick, button | Joystick, button | Attendant or auto | Joystick, voice, head | Joystick |
| Typical cost (INR) | 1.5–4 L | 5–15 L | 0.8–2 L | 4–10 L | 2–5 L |
| Clinical use | SCI, MS, MD | SCI in multi-storey | Pressure ulcer risk | Severe CP, high-level SCI | Outdoor mobility |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Increasing multifunctionality increases the **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of the chair and hence the **control complexity**. A standard powered wheelchair has 2 DOF (linear + angular); a standing wheelchair has 4–5 DOF; a stair-climbing one may have 6–8 DOF. This directly affects controller design (PID, fuzzy, neural network).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Multifunctional Wheelchair

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **subsystem interactions** in a generic multifunctional wheelchair — the architecture used in smart/robotic variants, which subsumes all other types.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Input Layer] --> B[Controller Unit MCU or DSP]
    B --> C[Motor Driver H-Bridge PWM]
    C --> D[Drive Motors BLDC or Geared DC]
    D --> E[Wheel / Track / Leg Mechanism]
    E --> F[Mechanical Chassis and Frame]
    B --> G[Linear Actuator Controller]
    G --> H[Posture Actuators Stand, Tilt, Recline]
    H --> F
    I[Sensor Array Layer] --> J[Sensor Fusion Unit Kalman Filter]
    J --> B
    I1[Ultrasonic Array] --> I
    I2[IR Obstacle Sensor] --> I
    I3[IMU Accelerometer Gyro] --> I
    I4[Wheel Encoders] --> I
    I5[LiDAR or Depth Camera] --> I
    K[Power Management Layer] --> B
    K --> C
    K --> G
    K --> I
    L[24V or 48V Li-ion Battery Pack] --> K
    M[BMS Battery Management System] --> L
    M --> K
    N[User Feedback Display] --> B
    O[Safety Module Emergency Stop] --> B
    P[Wireless Comms Bluetooth or Wi-Fi] --> B
    P --> Q[Mobile App or Caregiver Dashboard]
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style I fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style J fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style L fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style M fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **User Input Layer** (joystick, sip-and-puff, head-array, voice) sends commands to the **Controller Unit** (typically an STM32 or Arduino-class MCU, or a DSP for advanced filtering).
- The **Controller** drives both **Drive Motors** (via motor driver H-bridge) and **Posture Actuators** (via the linear actuator controller).
- The **Sensor Array** (ultrasonic, IR, IMU, encoders, LiDAR) feeds data to a **Sensor Fusion Unit** (often using a Kalman filter), which closes the feedback loop back to the Controller.
- The **Power Management Layer** (Battery + BMS) supplies regulated voltage to all subsystems, with over-current, over-voltage, and thermal protection.
- **Safety Module** (E-stop button, tilt-cutoff) and **Wireless Comms** (Bluetooth/Wi-Fi) connect the chair to external caregivers and remote diagnostics.

## 4.2 State Machine for Multifunctional Wheelchair Modes

The following Mermaid state diagram shows how the wheelchair transitions between operational modes — a common viva question in KTU ESE.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> DriveMode: Joystick Input
    Idle --> StandMode: Stand Button
    Idle --> ReclineMode: Recline Button
    Idle --> TiltMode: Tilt Button
    DriveMode --> StandMode: Speed Zero and Stand Request
    DriveMode --> StairClimb: Stair Detected
    DriveMode --> ObstacleAvoid: Sensor Alarm
    ObstacleAvoid --> DriveMode: Path Cleared
    StairClimb --> DriveMode: Stair Cleared
    StandMode --> Idle: User Seats
    ReclineMode --> Idle: User Returns Upright
    TiltMode --> Idle: Tilt Angle Zero
    DriveMode --> EmergencyStop: E Stop Pressed
    StandMode --> EmergencyStop: E Stop Pressed
    StairClimb --> EmergencyStop: E Stop Pressed
    EmergencyStop --> Idle: Manual Reset
    Idle --> [*]: Power Off
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The chair **always powers up in Idle**.
- From Idle, the user can transition to any posture mode (Stand, Recline, Tilt) or to DriveMode.
- During DriveMode, the chair continuously monitors the environment; if a **stair is detected**, it switches to StairClimb; if an **obstacle is detected**, it switches to ObstacleAvoid and resumes Drive once clear.
- **Any mode can transition to EmergencyStop**, which freezes all actuators and requires manual reset.

## 4.3 Sensor-to-Actuator Mapping (Functional Flow)

| **Sensor** | **Detects** | **Actuator Activated** | **Safety Response** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ultrasonic array | Wall/obstacle within 30 cm | Drive motor speed reduced | Stop + audible alarm |
| IR cliff sensor | Stair edge (drop) | All drive motors halted | Reverse 50 cm |
| IMU (pitch) | Tilt beyond 12° | Drive current limited | Visual warning on display |
| Wheel encoders | Slip (one wheel stalled) | Torque redistribution | Reduce speed, alarm |
| LiDAR | Person in path | Soft stop | Voice prompt "Please move" |
| Seat pressure mat | User has left seat | Disable drive | Beep every 10 s |
| Battery voltage sensor | $V < 21$ V (low) | Reduce max current | Flashing LED |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a multifunctional wheelchair. List any four functions it performs beyond basic mobility.
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set A]**

**Model Answer:**
A multifunctional wheelchair is a powered, sensor-integrated mobility device that performs one or more functions *in addition to* basic locomotion. Four such functions are:

1. **Standing** – elevates user to upright posture.
2. **Stair climbing** – negotiates vertical architectural steps.
3. **Tilt-in-space / Reclining** – redistributes pressure and aids rest.
4. **All-terrain / autonomous navigation** – travels on uneven surfaces or self-drives using sensors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award 1 mark for the definition and 0.5 mark *each* for the four functions (2 marks). Total = 3 marks.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between tilt-in-space and reclining mechanisms in a wheelchair. State one clinical indication for each.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest]**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Tilt-in-Space** | **Reclining** |
|---|---|---|
| Pivot axis | Near the user's hip | Variable, along the backrest |
| Hip & knee angles | Unchanged | Increased (back reclines) |
| Pressure redistribution | Shear-free | May cause shear |
| Clinical use | Postural drainage, hypotension, rest | Catheterization, supine rest, wound care |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3, Q7(a)]**

**(a)** Explain the **architecture of a stair-climbing wheelchair** with neat block diagram. Discuss any **three stair-climbing mechanisms** with their merits and demerits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A multifunctional wheelchair of total mass $m = 120$ kg must climb a 10° incline at a constant speed of $v = 1.0$ m/s. The coefficient of rolling resistance is $\mu = 0.018$, and the wheel radius is $r = 0.16$ m. The drive system efficiency is $\eta = 0.80$. Compute:
- (i) The force required at the wheels
- (ii) The torque required at each of the two drive motors
- (iii) The total power drawn from the battery
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Stair-climbing wheelchair architecture:**

```
User + Seat Platform
       |
[Controller + IMU + Encoders]
       |
[Motor Driver H-Bridge]
       |
[Drive Motors x 2]
       |
[Stair Mechanism: Tracks / Star Wheels / Legs]
       |
[Frame + Stabilizer Wheels]
       |
[24/48 V Li-ion Battery + BMS]
```

**Three mechanisms:**

1. **Continuous Track System**
   - *Merits:* Simple, continuous contact, handles debris.
   - *Demerits:* Slow (0.3–0.5 m/s), heavy, high battery drain, awkward on flat floor.

2. **Articulated Wheel-Leg Hybrid** (e.g., iBOT, Scewo)
   - *Merits:* Two-wheel self-balancing + climbing; compact; can elevate user.
   - *Demerits:* Complex control, expensive (INR 8–15 L), needs trained operator.

3. **Star/Polygonal Wheel Cluster** (e.g., TopChair)
   - *Merits:* Stable, no major balance issues, intuitive operation.
   - *Demerits:* Limited stair riser compatibility (~150–180 mm), jerky ride on flat.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often *describe only the mechanical aspect* and forget to mention the **controller + IMU feedback**, which is essential for stability. Marks are reserved for the control subsystem — do not omit it.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Given:** $m = 120$ kg, $\theta = 10°$, $v = 1.0$ m/s, $\mu = 0.018$, $r = 0.16$ m, $\eta = 0.80$, $g = 9.81$ m/s², 2 motors.

**(i) Force at the wheels:**

The total resistance force on a slope combines **gravity component** and **rolling resistance**:

$$
F = m \cdot g \cdot (\sin\theta + \mu \cos\theta)
$$

Plugging in:

$$
\sin 10° = 0.1736, \quad \cos 10° = 0.9848
$$

$$
F = 120 \times 9.81 \times (0.1736 + 0.018 \times 0.9848)
$$

$$
F = 1177.2 \times (0.1736 + 0.01773) = 1177.2 \times 0.19133
$$

$$
F = 225.27 \text{ N}
$$

**(ii) Torque at each of two motors:**

Total torque at wheels (sum of both motors):

$$
T_{total} = F \cdot r = 225.27 \times 0.16 = 36.04 \text{ N·m}
$$

For two motors with equal sharing:

$$
T_{each} = \dfrac{T_{total}}{2} = \dfrac{36.04}{2} = 18.02 \text{ N·m}
$$

**(iii) Total power drawn from battery:**

Output mechanical power at wheels:

$$
P_{out} = F \cdot v = 225.27 \times 1.0 = 225.27 \text{ W}
$$

Battery power input (accounting for efficiency):

$$
P_{bat} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta} = \dfrac{225.27}{0.80} = 281.59 \text{ W}
$$

**Final Answers:**

- Force at wheels ≈ **225.3 N**
- Torque per motor ≈ **18.0 N·m**
- Battery power ≈ **281.6 W** (≈ 11.7 A at 24 V)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Errors:** (1) Forgetting to **divide torque by 2** for a two-motor drivetrain. (2) Confusing **mechanical output power** with **electrical input power** — efficiency $\eta$ must be applied *only once*, in the correct direction. (3) Using $mg\sin\theta$ alone, missing the rolling resistance term $\mu mg\cos\theta$ (loses 1–2 marks).

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Q8(b)]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **architecture of a smart/robotic wheelchair**. Discuss the role of **sensor fusion** and **path planning** in such systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A standing wheelchair is to lift a 70 kg user to a standing height of 1.5 m in 6 s. The scissor-lift mechanism has an effective arm length of $L = 0.25$ m and a mechanical efficiency of $\eta = 0.70$. Compute:
- (i) The force the linear actuator must deliver
- (ii) The minimum actuator stroke required
- (iii) The energy consumed by the actuator
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Architecture** (as per Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram, redrawn verbally):

- **User Interface Layer:** Joystick / Sip-and-Puff / Head-array / Voice.
- **Perception Layer:** LiDAR, ultrasonic, IR, depth camera.
- **Sensor Fusion Unit:** Combines all sensor data using a Kalman filter to estimate chair pose, obstacle map, and user intent.
- **Decision & Planning Layer:** Path planner (A*, D*, or RRT) + behavior arbiter.
- **Control Layer:** PID/State-space controllers for each drive and posture motor.
- **Actuation Layer:** BLDC drive motors + linear posture actuators.
- **Safety Layer:** Watchdog timer, E-stop, tilt-cutoff.
- **Comms Layer:** Wi-Fi/Bluetooth to caregiver app.

**Role of Sensor Fusion:**
- Combines **complementary strengths** — LiDAR is precise at long range, ultrasonic is cheap at short range, IMU gives orientation, encoders give wheel odometry. Fused output gives a robust, low-variance estimate of chair state and surroundings.

**Role of Path Planning:**
- Generates a **collision-free trajectory** from current pose to goal pose, accounting for dynamic obstacles (people walking) and kinematic constraints (minimum turning radius).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students frequently draw a generic "input → output" diagram without naming the **specific algorithms** (Kalman, A*, PID). Examiners expect at least one named algorithm at the B-level (7-mark) question.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Given:** $m = 70$ kg, $h_{stand} = 1.5$ m, $t = 6$ s, $L = 0.25$ m, $\eta = 0.70$, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**(i) Actuator force:**

The actuator must lift the user against gravity. The mechanical advantage of the scissor linkage is approximately $L / (h_{stand} / 2)$ for symmetric arms; for a conservative design, the actuator must provide *at minimum* the user's full weight divided by efficiency (worst-case):

$$
F_{act} = \dfrac{m \cdot g}{\eta} = \dfrac{70 \times 9.81}{0.70} = \dfrac{686.7}{0.70} = 981.0 \text{ N}
$$

**(ii) Minimum actuator stroke:**

A symmetric scissor mechanism has geometric relation:

$$
\text{Stroke} = 2 \cdot \sqrt{L^2 - \left(\dfrac{L \cdot h_{seat}}{h_{stand}}\right)^2 \big/ \text{etc.}}
$$

For a simplified model, the total arm length traversed is approximately:

$$
\text{Stroke} \approx 2 \cdot L \cdot \left(1 - \cos\alpha_{max}\right)
$$

For $\alpha_{max} = 60°$ (typical standing angle of scissor arm from horizontal):

$$
\text{Stroke} = 2 \times 0.25 \times (1 - \cos 60°) = 0.5 \times (1 - 0.5) = 0.25 \text{ m} = 250 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iii) Energy consumed by the actuator:**

Gravitational PE stored in the lifted user:

$$
E_{PE} = m \cdot g \cdot h = 70 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 = 1030.05 \text{ J}
$$

Energy drawn from battery (accounting for efficiency):

$$
E_{bat} = \dfrac{E_{PE}}{\eta} = \dfrac{1030.05}{0.70} = 1471.5 \text{ J}
$$

**Final Answers:**

- Actuator force ≈ **981 N**
- Minimum stroke ≈ **250 mm**
- Energy ≈ **1471.5 J** (≈ 0.41 Wh per standing transition)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Errors:** (1) Treating standing as a *purely vertical* lift with 100% efficiency. (2) Forgetting that during the *descent* phase, energy can be partially recovered (regenerative) — a viva question often asks about this. (3) Stroke calculation geometry — students often forget that scissor lift stroke is **2× the linear travel of one arm**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A multifunctional wheelchair is a *mechatronic* assistive device that adds ≥1 function beyond basic powered mobility.
- **Categories (must know all 7):** Standing, Stair-climbing, Tilt/Recline, All-terrain, Power-assist, Smart/Robotic, Modular.
- **Core equation to memorize:** $F = m g (\sin\theta + \mu \cos\theta)$ — used in nearly every numerical.
- **Tipping condition:** $\tan\theta_{max} = d / (2 h_{CoG})$ — a **safety-critical** design constraint.
- **Battery sizing:** Energy $\approx V \cdot C_{Ah}$; range $\approx E_{mech} / (F \cdot v)$.
- **Stair-climbing mechanisms:** Tracks, star-wheels, articulated legs, balancing two-wheelers.
- **Standing benefits:** Pressure relief, circulation, bone density, psychosocial.
- **Sensors in smart wheelchairs:** Ultrasonic, IR, IMU, LiDAR, encoders, pressure mats.
- **Sensors fused using:** Kalman filter (most common) or complementary filter.
- **Standards to be aware of:** **ISO 7176** series (wheelchair performance), **RESNA WC-1**, **BIS IS 14423**.
- **Clinical use:** SCI, MS, MD, CP, polio, geriatric — CoG of clinical discussion.
- **Cost range (India):** Basic ₹ 50k–2 L; Standing ₹ 1.5–4 L; Stair-climbing ₹ 5–15 L; Robotic ₹ 4–10 L.
- **Always draw FBD** before any force/power numerical.
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** Manual = 0; Powered = 2; Standing = 4–5; Stair-climbing = 6–8; Robotic = 8+.
- **Efficiency $\eta$** is applied to convert *output* → *input*; never the reverse.
- **Universal Design and Design-for-All** are the philosophical basis for multifunctional wheelchair development.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Quick-Fire Mnemonic for Multifunctional Wheelchair Categories:** **"S-STAR-PM"**
> **S**tanding, **S**tair-climbing, **T**ilt/Recline, **A**ll-terrain, **R**obotic, **P**ower-assist, **M**odular.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
