<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

An **Upper Limb Prosthesis** is a custom-engineered, biomechanical replacement device designed to substitute, partially or fully, the functional and/or cosmetic role of a missing or congenitally absent upper extremity (forequarter, shoulder disarticulation, trans-humeral, trans-radial, wrist disarticulation, or partial hand). The device is biomechanically interfaced to the residual limb (stump) through a custom-molded **socket**, mechanically actuated through a hierarchy of subsystems (terminal device, wrist unit, elbow unit, shoulder unit), suspended onto the body by a **harness-suspension system**, and operated by either **body-powered (mechanical cable-driven)** or **externally powered (myoelectric/switch-controlled)** means.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner Standard):**
> A *prosthesis* (Greek: *pro* = "in place of" + *tithenai* = "to place") is an artificial device that replaces a missing body part. The **upper limb prosthesis** is a multi-component kinematic chain engineered to restore prehension, manipulation, and cosmetic normalcy lost due to amputation.

### 1.1.1 Levels of Upper Limb Amputation (Defines Prosthesis Type)

| Amputation Level | Prosthesis Type | Key Functional Challenge |
|---|---|---|
| Partial Hand | Finger/thumb prosthesis | Fine prehension |
| Wrist Disarticulation | Long trans-radial type | Wrist unit elimination |
| Trans-radial (Below Elbow, BE) | Below-Elbow (BE) | Forearm lever, elbow retained |
| Elbow Disarticulation | Elbow Disarticulation type | External elbow hinge |
| Trans-humeral (Above Elbow, AE) | Above-Elbow (AE) | Elbow + terminal device control |
| Shoulder Disarticulation | Shoulder Disarticulation type | Shoulder + elbow + TD control |
| Forequarter (Interscapulothoracic) | Forequarter type | Whole limb replacement |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Human Robotic Arm Kit"**
> Imagine a child's **modular construction kit** (e.g., a robotics arm kit) where each joint — the shoulder, elbow, wrist, and gripper — is a separate, swappable component. The kit must be:
> 1. **Anchored** to the body (socket = the mounting bracket).
> 2. **Wired** so that the brain (or body) can send signals to it (cables or electrodes).
> 3. **Held in place** even when dangling (suspension harness = the shoulder strap of a backpack).
> 4. **Cosmetically finished** to look human (cosmetic glove = the painted outer shell).
>
> The **upper limb prosthesis works on exactly the same modular principle** — it is a *biomechanical kinematic chain* of five modular blocks: **(1) Socket, (2) Suspension, (3) Control System, (4) Joint Units (Wrist, Elbow, Shoulder), (5) Terminal Device (Hand/Hook)**.

## 1.3 Critical Design Metrics (KTU Mandatory)

Every upper limb prosthesis is evaluated against standardized KTU/Rehab-board parameters:

- **Mass:** < **0.5 kg** for a hand-only prosthesis; < **1.5 kg** for a full shoulder-disarticulation prosthesis.
- **Pinch force:** **50–100 N** (voluntary opening hook); **10–30 N** (cosmetic hand).
- **Operating cable force (body-powered):** typically **30–70 N** excursion of **5–7 cm**.
- **Myoelectric signal threshold:** **20–200 µV** (RMS), bandwidth **20–500 Hz**.
- **Power consumption:** < **300 mW** for one channel of myoelectric control.
- **Power autonomy:** Battery must last **≥ 1 full day** (~16 hours of intermittent use).
- **Service life:** **3–5 years** for socket; **1–2 years** for cable/harness; **6 months** for cosmetic glove.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** A prosthetic prescription is *not* a one-size-fits-all decision. The prosthetist must consider the patient's **amputation level, age, occupation, dominant hand, cognitive ability, vocational needs, financial constraint, and residual-limb condition** before selecting the component combination.

## 1.4 The Five Functional Subsystems — Quick Map

| # | Subsystem | Primary Function | Example Components |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Socket** | Biomechanical interface; load transfer | PTB, Muenster, Northwestern sockets |
| 2 | **Suspension System** | Holds prosthesis onto body | Harness, suction liner, pin lock, sleeve |
| 3 | **Control System** | Converts intent → motion | Bowden cable, EMG electrodes, switch |
| 4 | **Joint Units** | Restore articulation | Wrist rotator, elbow hinge, shoulder unit |
| 5 | **Terminal Device (TD)** | Performs grasp/prehension | Hook, mechanical hand, myoelectric hand |

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Subsystem 1: The Socket (Stump-Socket Interface)

The **socket** is the custom-fabricated biomechanical coupling between the residual limb and the prosthesis. It is the single most critical component — *if the socket fails, no other component matters*.

### 2.1.1 Functions of a Socket
1. **Load transfer:** Distributes ground-reaction/functional loads over the **residual limb's tolerant areas** (weight-bearing surfaces) and away from pressure-intolerant zones.
2. **Suspension anchor:** Provides the mechanical interlock for the suspension system.
3. **Force containment:** Transmits muscle contractions, body-weight, and external loads.
4. **Position retention:** Maintains alignment of the residual limb within the prosthesis.
5. **Comfort & proprioception:** Provides sensory feedback by intimate skin contact.

### 2.1.2 Standard Socket Designs (KTU-Favorite)

| Socket | Level | Key Design Principle |
|---|---|---|
| **PTB (Patellar Tendon Bearing)** | Trans-radial (BE) | Weight borne on patellar tendon, not stump end |
| **Muenster Socket** | Short trans-radial | High medial-lateral brim for self-suspension |
| **Northwestern University Socket** | Trans-radial | Quadrilateral shape, self-suspending brim |
| **Humeroscapulothoracic** | Trans-humeral | Encloses humerus with shoulder cap |
| **Sauerbuch / Kruckenberg** | Trans-humeral (older) | Lumbrical-shift load transfer |
| **Shoulder Disarticulation Socket** | Shoulder disarticulation | Encloses scapula + clavicle |
| **Forequarter Socket** | Forequarter | Encloses thorax, extends to sternum |

### 2.1.3 Step-by-Step Socket Fabrication Logic
1. **Casting** → Plaster wrap negative mold of the stump.
2. **Casting rectification** → Plaster positive modified to relieve pressure-intolerant areas (e.g., bony prominences, nerves, scar tissue) and load-tolerant areas (e.g., patellar tendon, medial tibial flare, soft tissue).
3. **Lamination** → Lay-up of fiberglass/carbon fiber + acrylic or epoxy resin over the positive mold.
4. **Liner fit** → Inner silicone/pe-lite liner for skin comfort.
5. **Attachment plate** → Distal end fitting for connecting to the shank.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PTB Works:** The patellar tendon is a **load-tolerant soft-tissue structure** that can bear up to **3–5× body weight** per cm². The PTB socket offloads the distal end (which is pressure-intolerant in most BE amputees) and forces the load through the patellar tendon, the medial tibial flare, and the popliteal fossa — this is a textbook biomechanical *load-redistribution* principle.

---

## 2.2 Subsystem 2: The Suspension System

The **suspension system** holds the prosthesis onto the residual limb against gravity, inertial forces, and functional loads.

### 2.2.1 Classification of Suspension Methods

| Type | Mechanism | Common Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Anatomical (self-suspending)** | Anatomical contours of stump + socket | Muenster, NW socket |
| **Suction** | Air-negative pressure by sealing sleeve | BE/TE sockets with silicone liner |
| **Pin Lock (Shuttle Lock)** | Pin on liner clicks into lock on socket | Modern BE/AE prostheses |
| **Lanyard** | Strap inside socket pulled by user | TE amputees |
| **Harness / Strap** | Figure-8 or figure-9 shoulder harness | Trans-humeral, shoulder disarticulation |
| **Vacuum-Assisted (elevated vacuum)** | Active pump maintains sub-atmospheric pressure | High-activity BE |

### 2.2.2 The Figure-8 and Figure-9 Harnesses (KTU Important)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Figure-8 Harness (Trans-Humeral Standard)**
> - Anterior strap passes over the **contralateral shoulder**.
> - Axilla loop on the amputated side.
> - Two main functions: (i) **Suspension** of the prosthesis, (ii) **Control** of the elbow lock and terminal device through a single Bowden cable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Figure-9 Harness (Trans-Radial)**
> - Anterior suspensor strap over the contralateral shoulder.
> - Single control attachment strap on the amputated side.
> - Used to operate the terminal device and (optionally) a wrist unit.

The **Bowden cable** is a flexible mechanical transmission consisting of:
- An **inner stainless-steel stranded cable** (1.6–2.0 mm diameter).
- An **outer housing** (steel coil or polymer liner) that defines the cable path.
- **End fittings** at the housing and at the cable terminus.

When the user **biscapular abduction** (squeezes shoulders together) → cable tension rises → terminal device or elbow operates.

---

## 2.3 Subsystem 3: The Control System

### 2.3.1 Body-Powered (Cable-Driven) Control

This is the **most prescribed system worldwide** due to reliability, low cost, light weight, and high tactile feedback.

**Operational Physics:** When the user moves the shoulder, the harness pulls the cable; the inner cable slides inside the housing, transferring force over a curved path with minimal friction loss.

**Force Transmission Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{cable} = \frac{F_{out}}{F_{in}} = 1 - \mu \cdot \theta$$

where:
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (typically **0.05–0.15** for steel-on-polymer),
- $\theta$ = total bend angle of the cable path in **radians**.

**Voluntary Opening (VO) vs. Voluntary Closing (VC) Hook:**

| Feature | Voluntary Opening (VO) | Voluntary Closing (VC) |
|---|---|---|
| Default state | **Open** (rubber-band held) | **Closed** (spring held) |
| User pulls cable to | **Close** (grip) | **Open** (release) |
| Grip force | Limited by rubber band (~**50 N**) | Adjustable by user (up to **200 N**) |
| Useful for | Holding objects indefinitely | Holding fragile or heavy objects |
| Sensory feedback | Cable tension = grip | Cable tension = grip |
| Most common | **Yes** (standard) | Optional |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why VO is Standard:** In a VO hook, when the user **releases** the cable, the rubber band *automatically* opens the hook, freeing the hand. This allows the user to relax the shoulder muscles while holding an object — **energy-efficient for sustained grasp**.

### 2.3.2 Externally Powered Control (Myoelectric)

In a **myoelectric (myo) prosthesis**, surface electrodes (typically **stainless steel or Ag/AgCl**, in pairs) detect the **EMG (electromyographic) signal** from residual muscles. The signal is amplified, filtered, rectified, smoothed, and used to drive a DC motor that operates the terminal device or elbow.

**EMG Signal Processing Pipeline:**

$$EMG_{raw}(t) \xrightarrow{\text{Bandpass 20–500 Hz}} EMG_{filt}(t) \xrightarrow{\text{Full-wave rectify}} \vert EMG_{filt}(t) \vert \xrightarrow{\text{LPF 2–6 Hz}} EMG_{envelope}(t) \xrightarrow{\text{Threshold compare}} Motor \ Command$$

**Control Strategy Variants:**

| Strategy | Electrodes | Operation |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-site, on/off** | 1 electrode pair | Open vs. close (one muscle) |
| **Two-site proportional** | 2 electrode pairs | Open with flexor, close with extensor; speed/force proportional to EMG amplitude |
| **Pattern recognition** | 4–8 electrodes | Machine-learning classifier maps EMG patterns to multi-function commands |
| **TMR (Targeted Muscle Reinnervation)** | Re-innervated muscles | Amplified signal from re-routed nerves |

**Myoelectric Performance Formulas (KTU High-Yield):**

$$SNR = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{V_{signal,RMS}}{V_{noise,RMS}} \right) \quad [\text{dB}]$$

$$V_{motor} = K_{amp} \cdot \overline{\vert EMG(t) \vert} - V_{threshold}$$

$$F_{grip} = K_{motor} \cdot I_{coil} = K_{motor} \cdot \frac{V_{motor} - K_{motor} \cdot \omega}{R_{coil}}$$

where $K_{amp}$ is the amplifier gain (~**1000–5000×**), $V_{threshold}$ is the trigger voltage (~**20–200 µV RMS**), and $K_{motor}$ is the motor torque constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Myoelectric Power Budget:**
> $$\ P_{consumed} = I \cdot V_{battery} \approx 200 \text{ mA} \times 7.2 \text{ V} = 1.44 \text{ W (peak)}, \approx 250 \text{ mW (average)}$$
> A **7.2 V, 1500 mAh Li-ion battery** therefore powers a single-channel myoelectric hand for **~8 hours of intermittent use**.

### 2.3.3 Hybrid Control

Combines **body-powered elbow** (high reliability) with **myoelectric terminal device** (good dexterity). Used in trans-humeral amputees where cable-pulling the elbow and the TD simultaneously is impractical.

---

## 2.4 Subsystem 4: Joint Units (Wrist, Elbow, Shoulder)

### 2.4.1 Wrist Units

| Type | Operation | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Friction Wrist** | Rotates against friction; position retained | General, low-cost |
| **Quick-Disconnect Wrist** | Push-button release swaps TDs | Multiple-grip needs |
| **Locking Wrist (Lamination Ring)** | Manual lock, multiple preset angles | Heavy work, AE |
| **Constant-Friction Wrist** | Friction ring tightened to user preference | Standard |
| **Powered Wrist Rotator** | Motor-driven pronation/supination | Myoelectric AE |
| **Flexion Wrist** | Allows wrist flexion for bilateral tasks | Bilateral amputee |

### 2.4.2 Elbow Units

| Type | Operation | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **External Hinge Elbow** | Linked to triceps pad, voluntary flexion/extension | Elbow disarticulation |
| **Internal Elbow Mechanism** | Bowden cable actuates elbow lock + forearm lift | Trans-humeral |
| **Step-Up / Step-Down Hinge** | Step-up = 3:1 ratio; step-down = 1:3 | Length compensation |
| **Hosmer / USMC Elbow** | Manual lock + lift | Standard AE |
| **Utah Arm Elbow** | Myoelectric lock | Hybrid prostheses |
| **Boston Arm** | Myoelectric elbow | Advanced hybrid |

**Mechanical Advantage of Step-Up Hinges:**

$$MA = \frac{L_{lever,effort}}{L_{lever,load}}$$

For a typical step-up hinge: $MA = 3:1$, so **30 N of cable force lifts 90 N** of forearm weight.

### 2.4.3 Shoulder Units

- **Shoulder Cap + Friction Hinge:** Standard, allows flexion/extension and abduction of the prosthesis.
- **Ball-and-Socket Shoulder:** Multiaxial motion, used in shoulder disarticulation and forequarter.
- **Saddle or Thoracic Sling:** Anchors to thorax when scapula is absent.

---

## 2.5 Subsystem 5: The Terminal Device (TD)

The **TD** is the "hand" of the prosthesis — the part that actually performs **prehension**.

### 2.5.1 Types of Terminal Devices

| Type | Mass (g) | Pinch Force (N) | Cost | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Hook (VO)** | 100–200 | 50–100 | Low | Manual labor |
| **Hook (VC)** | 150–250 | 20–200 | Medium | Precision work |
| **Mechanical Hand (VO)** | 300–450 | 25–50 | Medium | Light, cosmetic, casual |
| **Mechanical Hand (VC)** | 350–500 | 30–100 | Medium | Higher grip |
| **Myoelectric Hand** | 400–600 | 60–100 | High | All-day cosmesis |
| **Cosmetic (Passive) Hand** | 100–200 | 0 | Low–Medium | Cosmetic only |
| **Specialty TDs** | varies | varies | High | Sports, music, tools |

### 2.5.2 Cosmetic Glove (External Covering)

The **cosmetic glove** is a thin PVC or silicone cover placed over the TD to mimic skin, fingernails, and skin creases. It is the **first part to wear out** (typical life: **6 months**) due to UV, sweat, and mechanical stress.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** The cosmetic glove **is not structural**; it does not transmit load. It is purely a **visual and psychological** restoration. The actual structural and functional TD is the metal/plastic device *inside* the glove.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Reference Formulas for KTU 2024 Examination**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bowden-cable efficiency | $\eta = 1 - \mu \theta$ | $\mu$ = friction, $\theta$ = bend (rad) |
| 2 | Cable force at TD | $F_{TD} = F_{body} \cdot \eta$ | $F_{body}$ = shoulder force |
| 3 | Mechanical advantage of step-up hinge | $MA = L_{effort}/L_{load}$ | Ratio of lever arms |
| 4 | SNR of EMG | $SNR = 20 \log_{10}(V_s/V_n)$ | dB scale |
| 5 | Myoelectric threshold | $V_{control} = K_{amp} \cdot \overline{\vert EMG \vert} - V_{th}$ | Trigger for motor |
| 6 | Motor back-EMF | $V_{emf} = K_e \cdot \omega$ | Opposes supply |
| 7 | Grip torque (hook) | $T_{grip} = F_{cable} \cdot r_{lever}$ | Pinch force × moment arm |
| 8 | Suspension suction pressure | $P_{suc} = P_{atm} - P_{socket}$ | Sub-atmospheric |
| 9 | Battery life | $t = \frac{Q_{batt}[mAh]}{I_{load}[mA]}$ | Hours of operation |
| 10 | Hook grip force (VO) | $F_{grip} = n \cdot k \cdot x_{band}$ | n = bands, k = spring rate, x = stretch |
| 11 | Voluntary opening excursion | $E_{TD} = 2 \cdot r_{finger} \cdot \theta_{open}$ | Geometric |
| 12 | Load on patellar tendon (PTB) | $\sigma_{PT} = F_{total}/A_{PT}$ | $A_{PT} \approx 3{-}5 \text{ cm}^2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** The same physical equations used for these prostheses apply to industrial robotics arms. The PTB socket is essentially a *biomechanical wrist joint equivalent* in robotics. The Bowden cable is identical to a bicycle brake cable. The EMG amplifier is identical to a medical ECG/EEG amplifier. **Rehab engineering is applied mechatronics with a soft-tissue interface.**

---

## 2.7 Prosthesis Classification (KTU Taxonomy)

| Class | Actuation | Cost (USD) | Mass (g) | Sensory Feedback | Indications |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cosmetic (Passive)** | None | $500–$2,000 | 100–250 | None | Cosmetic restoration only |
| **Body-Powered (Mechanical)** | Body + cable | $1,500–$5,000 | 250–600 | High (cable tension) | All levels, esp. heavy labor |
| **Externally Powered (Myo/Switch)** | Battery + motor | $5,000–$30,000 | 400–900 | Low (audible click) | All levels, office/casual |
| **Hybrid** | Body + battery | $8,000–$20,000 | 500–800 | Mixed | Trans-humeral, mixed use |
| **Activity-Specific** | Various | $200–$5,000 | 100–800 | Variable | Sports, music, vocational |
| **Modular (e.g., bebionic, i-Limb)** | Multi-motor myo | $20,000–$70,000 | 500–700 | Sensorized | Advanced users |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bowden Cable Force Transmission Efficiency

We will derive the **efficiency equation** $\eta = 1 - \mu\theta$ from first principles, as it is a common 7-mark derivable question in KTU exams.

### Setup
Consider a Bowden cable of length $L$ bent through a total angle $\theta$ (in **radians**) over a curved path. The inner cable has normal force $N(x)$ on the housing at every point of contact.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let us take a small element of the cable of arc length $ds$. The capstan (belt-friction) equation for a small arc $d\phi$ gives:

$$\frac{dT}{T} = -\mu \, d\phi$$

This is a standard first-order ODE (capstan equation, also known as Euler's belt-friction formula). Integrating from the input end (force $F_{in}$ at $\phi = 0$) to the output end (force $F_{out}$ at $\phi = \theta$):

$$\int_{F_{in}}^{F_{out}} \frac{dT}{T} = -\int_{0}^{\theta} \mu \, d\phi$$

Applying the fundamental theorem of calculus:

$$\left[ \ln T \right]_{F_{in}}^{F_{out}} = -\mu \left[ \phi \right]_{0}^{\theta}$$

$$\ln F_{out} - \ln F_{in} = -\mu \theta$$

$$\ln \left( \frac{F_{out}}{F_{in}} \right) = -\mu \theta$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$\frac{F_{out}}{F_{in}} = e^{-\mu \theta}$$

Therefore, the **mechanical efficiency** of the Bowden cable is:

$$\eta_{cable} = \frac{F_{out}}{F_{in}} = e^{-\mu \theta}$$

### Linear Approximation (KTU Preferred Form)

For small $\mu\theta$ (which is the realistic case: $\mu \approx 0.1$, $\theta \approx \pi/2$ rad, so $\mu\theta \approx 0.157$), the Taylor expansion $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x$ gives the **engineer's linear form**:

$$\eta_{cable} \approx 1 - \mu \theta$$

### Numerical Example (Worked Out)

**Given:** $\mu = 0.10$, $\theta = \pi/2$ rad (90° bend), $F_{in} = 70$ N (typical shoulder excursion force).

**Step 1:** Compute $\eta$ (linear form).

$$\eta = 1 - (0.10)\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) = 1 - 0.1571 = 0.8429$$

**Step 2:** Compute $F_{out}$.

$$F_{out} = \eta \cdot F_{in} = 0.8429 \times 70 \text{ N} = 59.0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3:** Compare with exact exponential form.

$$\eta_{exact} = e^{-0.1571} = 0.8545$$

$$F_{out,exact} = 0.8545 \times 70 \text{ N} = 59.8 \text{ N}$$

The error between linear and exact is **0.8 N (~1.3%)**, which justifies the linear approximation in design.

### Real-World Interpretation
A 90° cable bend loses **~15%** of the input force to friction. This is why **prosthetists try to keep the cable path as straight as possible** and route the Bowden housing through large-radius curves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters Clinically:** If the patient must exert 70 N of shoulder force to lift 60 N at the hook, **the patient is "wasting" 10 N of effort in cable friction**. A simple rerouting through a larger bend radius can recover this loss.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Myoelectric Threshold Logic (State-Machine Model)

The myoelectric control system behaves as a **hysteresis-gated state machine**. We derive the trigger equations for the open/close states.

### State Variables
Let $x(t) = \overline{|EMG_{filt}(t)|}$ be the smoothed rectified EMG envelope (V).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define thresholds.

$$V_{th,open} = V_{th,close} + \Delta V_{hyst}$$

where $\Delta V_{hyst} > 0$ prevents *chattering* (rapid open/close oscillation near the threshold).

**Step 2:** Define the comparator output (state $S$).

$$S(t) = \begin{cases} 1 \;(\text{CLOSE}), & x(t) > V_{th,close} \text{ and } S(t^-) = 0 \\ 0 \;(\text{OPEN}), & x(t) < V_{th,open} \text{ and } S(t^-) = 1 \\ S(t^-), & \text{otherwise (hysteresis hold)} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3:** Motor voltage is then:

$$V_{motor}(t) = V_{bat} \cdot S(t)$$

**Step 4:** Motor current is governed by Ohm's law with back-EMF.

$$I_{motor}(t) = \frac{V_{bat} \cdot S(t) - K_e \cdot \omega(t)}{R_{coil}}$$

**Step 5:** Motor torque is proportional to current.

$$\tau_{motor}(t) = K_t \cdot I_{motor}(t) = K_t \cdot \frac{V_{bat} \cdot S(t) - K_e \cdot \omega(t)}{R_{coil}}$$

### Numerical Example

**Given:** $V_{bat} = 7.2$ V, $K_e = 0.01$ V·s/rad, $\omega = 50$ rad/s (no load), $R_{coil} = 4\ \Omega$, $K_t = 0.01$ N·m/A.

**Step 1:** Compute no-load back-EMF.

$$V_{emf} = K_e \cdot \omega = 0.01 \times 50 = 0.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2:** Compute no-load current.

$$I_{no-load} = \frac{7.2 - 0.5}{4} = 1.675 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3:** Compute no-load torque (very small).

$$\tau_{no-load} = 0.01 \times 1.675 = 0.01675 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4:** Compute stall current (worst case, $\omega = 0$).

$$I_{stall} = \frac{7.2}{4} = 1.8 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5:** Compute stall torque (max useful).

$$\tau_{stall} = 0.01 \times 1.8 = 0.018 \text{ N·m}$$

**Interpretation:** The small DC motor used in a myoelectric hand delivers ~**18 mN·m** of torque, which is then geared up by a **100:1 harmonic-drive or planetary gearbox** to provide **~1.8 N·m** at the finger — corresponding to a **60–100 N pinch force** at the fingertip.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The myoelectric system is a **closed-loop electromechanical system**. The back-EMF provides inherent **speed regulation** — as the motor speeds up, the effective voltage drops, and current (and thus torque) drops, preventing runaway. This is *exactly* the same physics as a DC motor in a power window of a car.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Voluntary-Opening Hook Grip Force

A VO hook uses **rubber bands** to close the fingers. The user pulls the cable to *open* the hook (against the bands), and releases the cable to *close* (grip).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Each rubber band behaves as a linear spring (for small stretches).

$$F_{band} = k \cdot x$$

where $k$ is the spring rate (N/m) and $x$ is the band stretch (m).

**Step 2:** If $n$ rubber bands are placed in parallel, the total closing force at the fingertips is:

$$F_{close} = n \cdot k \cdot x_{max}$$

**Step 3:** The pinch force at the tip is reduced by the lever ratio of the finger. Let the finger pivot be at distance $L_1$ from the cable anchor and $L_2$ from the fingertip:

$$F_{pinch} = F_{close} \cdot \frac{L_1}{L_2}$$

**Step 4:** Combining:

$$F_{pinch} = n \cdot k \cdot x_{max} \cdot \frac{L_1}{L_2}$$

### Numerical Example

**Given:** $n = 2$ bands, $k = 200$ N/m, $x_{max} = 0.04$ m (4 cm stretch), $L_1 = 0.02$ m, $L_2 = 0.05$ m.

**Step 1:** Compute total band force.

$$F_{close} = 2 \times 200 \times 0.04 = 16 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2:** Compute pinch force.

$$F_{pinch} = 16 \times \frac{0.02}{0.05} = 16 \times 0.4 = 6.4 \text{ N}$$

This is far below the typical 50–100 N spec. To increase pinch force, the patient adds more bands (increasing $n$) or replaces stock bands with thicker ones (increasing $k$). A typical setup of **5 bands** with $k = 400$ N/m gives:

$$F_{pinch} = 5 \times 400 \times 0.04 \times 0.4 = 32 \text{ N}$$

Adding another 3 bands: $F_{pinch} = 5.6 \text{ bands} \times \dots \approx 70{-}100 \text{ N}$ — typical adult operational spec.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Component Choice Decision Tree

The following matrix guides prescription of upper-limb prosthetic components based on patient profile.

| Patient Profile | Amputation Level | Recommended TD | Recommended Control | Recommended Socket | Suspension |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Manual laborer, BE | Trans-radial | VO Hook (steel) | Body-powered (cable) | PTB or NW | Figure-9 harness |
| Office worker, BE | Trans-radial | Myo hand | Myoelectric | Muenster | Self-suspending / pin lock |
| Manual laborer, AE | Trans-humeral | VO Hook | Body-powered (dual cable) | Humeroscapulothoracic | Figure-8 + chest strap |
| Office worker, AE | Trans-humeral | Myo hand | Hybrid (body elbow + myo TD) | Humeroscapulothoracic | Figure-8 harness |
| Child, BE | Trans-radial | VC Hook (small) | Body-powered | Pediatric PTB | Figure-9, child-size |
| Bilateral, AE | Trans-humeral | Two hooks | Body-powered | Custom | Bilateral figure-8 |
| Cosmetic-only, BE | Trans-radial | Passive hand | None | Cosmetic | Self-suspending or vacuum |
| Athlete | Any | Activity-specific (e.g., swimming, cycling) | Body or custom | Custom | Custom |
| Heavy manual, partial hand | Partial hand | Custom silicone | None | Custom silicone | Anatomical |
| Elderly, low vision, BE | Trans-radial | VO Hook (light) | Body-powered | Simple PTB | Figure-9 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matrix Matters:** Each row represents a clinically-validated prescription pattern. In KTU vivas, examiners often ask *"If a 45-year-old manual laborer with a right trans-humeral amputation comes to your clinic, what prosthesis would you prescribe?"* — This matrix guides the answer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Modular Architecture of an Upper Limb Prosthesis

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the **hierarchical assembly** of a typical **trans-humeral (Above-Elbow) body-powered prosthesis**. All components and their functional interactions are shown.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Residual Limb Stump] --> B[Custom Socket]
    B --> C[Suspension Harness Figure-8]
    C --> D[Control Cable Bowden Type]
    D --> E[Elbow Unit with Lock]
    E --> F[Forearm Shank]
    F --> G[Wrist Unit Quick Disconnect]
    G --> H[Terminal Device Hook or Hand]
    B --> I[Soft Liner Silicone or Pelite]
    I --> A
    D --> J[Shoulder Sling]
    J --> A
    H --> K[Cosmetic Glove PVC]
    style A fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#cfe8fc,stroke:#06c,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#b58105,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#d4edda,stroke:#2c7a2c,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#e2d6f3,stroke:#6f42c1,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#e2d6f3,stroke:#6f42c1,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#e2d6f3,stroke:#6f42c1,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#a83246,stroke-width:3px
    style K fill:#fef3e2,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The **red-shaded block (A)** is the biological input; **blue-shaded (B)** is the biomechanical interface; **yellow (C)** is the suspension anchor; **green (D)** is the control transmission; **purple (E, F, G)** are the articulated joints; **deep red (H)** is the prehension output; **orange (K)** is the cosmetic cover.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Body-Powered Control Loop

This block diagram shows the **closed-loop force-feedback** model of a body-powered cable system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Patient Brain Intent] --> A2[Shoulder Girdle Motion Biscapular Abduction]
    A2 --> A3[Harness Tension Sensor Proprioception]
    A3 --> A4[Bowden Cable Inner Wire Slides]
    A4 --> A5[Terminal Device Hook Mechanism]
    A5 --> A6[Object Grasped]
    A6 --> A7[Load Force Felt at Cable]
    A7 --> A8[Cable Back-Tension]
    A8 --> A3
    style A1 fill:#fff,stroke:#000
    style A2 fill:#fff,stroke:#000
    style A3 fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#a08a00
    style A4 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#2c7a2c
    style A5 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#a83246
    style A6 fill:#cfe8fc,stroke:#06c
    style A7 fill:#fef3e2,stroke:#d97706
    style A8 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#2c7a2c
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** This is a **servo-loop with the patient as the controller**. The brain senses cable tension (proportional to grip force), adjusts shoulder force, and modulates grip. This is why **body-powered systems have excellent sensory feedback** — the user *feels* the load through the cable.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Myoelectric Signal Processing Pipeline

This diagram shows the **signal flow** from muscle to motor in a myoelectrically controlled terminal device.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Residual Muscle Biceps or Triceps] --> M2[Surface EMG Electrode Ag-AgCl]
    M2 --> M3[Differential Amplifier Gain 1000 to 5000]
    M3 --> M4[Bandpass Filter 20 to 500 Hz]
    M4 --> M5[Full-Wave Rectifier]
    M5 --> M6[Low-Pass Smoother 2 to 6 Hz]
    M6 --> M7[Threshold Comparator with Hysteresis]
    M7 --> M8[Logic Decoder Open or Close or Proportional]
    M8 --> M9[DC Motor Driver PWM]
    M9 --> M10[DC Gear Motor 100:1 Gearbox]
    M10 --> M11[Terminal Device Hand or Hook]
    M11 --> M12[Object Grasped]
    M12 --> M13[Tactile Sensor Optional]
    M13 --> M8
    style M1 fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33
    style M11 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#a83246
    style M12 fill:#cfe8fc,stroke:#06c
    style M3 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#2c7a2c
    style M9 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#b58105
    style M13 fill:#fef3e2,stroke:#d97706
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Note the **forward path** (M1 → M11) and the **optional feedback path** (M12 → M13 → M8). The latter is found in **advanced sensorized hands** (e.g., i-Limb, bebionic) that automatically increase grip if slip is detected.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Sockets at Different Amputation Levels (Schematic Map)

This diagram maps **socket geometry** to **amputation level** and shows the typical load-transfer pattern.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Trans-Radial BE Amputation] --> S1A[PTB Socket Patellar Tendon Bearing]
    S1 --> S1B[Muenster Socket Short BE]
    S1 --> S1C[Northwestern Socket Quadrilateral]
    S2[Elbow Disarticulation] --> S2A[External Hinge Socket]
    S3[Trans-Humeral AE Amputation] --> S3A[Humeroscapulothoracic Socket]
    S3 --> S3B[Sauerbuch Socket Older Design]
    S4[Shoulder Disarticulation] --> S4A[Shoulder Cap Socket]
    S5[Forequarter] --> S5A[Thoracic Extension Socket]
    style S1A fill:#cfe8fc,stroke:#06c
    style S1B fill:#cfe8fc,stroke:#06c
    style S1C fill:#cfe8fc,stroke:#06c
    style S2A fill:#d4edda,stroke:#2c7a2c
    style S3A fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#b58105
    style S3B fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#b58105
    style S4A fill:#e2d6f3,stroke:#6f42c1
    style S5A fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#a83246
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** As the **amputation level moves proximally** (up the limb toward the body), the socket must enclose more anatomy for suspension and load transfer. **Color-coding** by level: blue (BE), green (elbow disarticulation), yellow (AE), purple (shoulder disarticulation), red (forequarter).

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Classification of Upper Limb Prosthesis (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Upper Limb Prosthesis Selection] --> P1{Power Source}
    P1 -->|None| P2[Cosmetic Passive Prosthesis]
    P1 -->|Body Cable| P3[Body-Powered Prosthesis]
    P1 -->|Battery| P4[Externally Powered Prosthesis]
    P1 -->|Mixed| P5[Hybrid Prosthesis]

    P2 --> P2B[Passive Functional Tool Holder]
    P3 --> P3A[Single Cable BE]
    P3 --> P3B[Dual Cable AE]
    P4 --> P4A[Myoelectric Hand]
    P4 --> P4B[Switch-Controlled]
    P4 --> P4C[Pattern Recognition 4 to 8 Channel]
    P5 --> P5A[Body Elbow + Myo TD]
    style P2 fill:#cfe8fc,stroke:#06c
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]

**Q: Define a "Bowden cable" as used in upper-limb body-powered prostheses. State its typical force-transmission efficiency formula.**

**Model Answer:**

A Bowden cable is a flexible mechanical transmission used in body-powered upper-limb prostheses to convey the actuating force generated by the patient's shoulder-girdle motion to the terminal device (or elbow unit) located distally on the prosthesis. It consists of two parts:

1. **An inner stranded stainless-steel cable** (typically 1.6–2.0 mm diameter) that slides inside the housing to transmit tension.
2. **An outer housing** (steel-coil or polymer-lined) that defines the cable path and constrains the inner cable to slide in pure tension.

**Typical efficiency formula:**

$$\eta_{cable} = e^{-\mu\theta} \approx 1 - \mu\theta$$

where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction between inner cable and housing (~0.05–0.15) and $\theta$ is the total bend angle of the cable path in radians. For a typical 90° bend ($\theta = \pi/2$ rad) with $\mu = 0.10$, $\eta \approx 0.84$, meaning ~16% of the input force is lost to friction.

*(3 marks = 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for construction, 1 mark for formula with substitution.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]

**Q: Differentiate between Voluntary Opening (VO) and Voluntary Closing (VC) terminal devices in upper-limb prostheses.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Voluntary Opening (VO) | Voluntary Closing (VC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Default state** | **Open** (held open by rubber bands) | **Closed** (held closed by spring) |
| **User action to grip** | **Pull** the control cable (closes the TD) | **Pull** the control cable (releases grip) |
| **User action to release** | **Release** the control cable (TD auto-opens) | **Release** the control cable (TD auto-closes) |
| **Energy to maintain grip** | None (passive — patient can relax) | Patient must keep pulling (active) |
| **Grip force** | Limited by number/stiffness of rubber bands (50–100 N) | Adjustable by patient up to 200 N |
| **Indications** | Most common; sustained grip; light-to-medium work | Heavy or precision work; active patients |
| **Example** | Standard Hosmer hook | APRL or TRS VC hook |

*(3 marks = 1 mark for each of: default state, energy/grip force, and one application difference.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Read Carefully!**
> Common mark-loss zones in this topic:
> 1. **Confusing "PTB" and "PTS"** sockets — PTB is *Patellar Tendon Bearing* (for below-elbow), NOT posterior.
> 2. **Wrong default state for VO/VC** — VO is *OPEN by default*, not closed. This is the single most common 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Forgetting the cosmetic glove is non-structural** — examiners deduct ½ mark if you imply it bears load.
> 4. **Misnaming "Figure-8" and "Figure-9" harnesses** — they look similar; be precise.
> 5. **Mixing up Sauerbuch with Northwestern** — Sauerbuch is for trans-humeral; Northwestern is for trans-radial.
> 6. **Stating "myoelectric = always better"** — it is *not*. It is heavier, costlier, has less sensory feedback, and fails in sweat/water.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **five major subsystems of a trans-humeral body-powered upper-limb prosthesis**. State the function of each subsystem and name at least one specific component for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Bowden-cable force-transmission efficiency** $\eta = e^{-\mu\theta}$ from the capstan equation. Compute the efficiency for a 90° bend, $\mu = 0.10$, and discuss its design implications for cable routing in a body-powered prosthesis.

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
Residual Limb → Socket → Elbow Unit → Forearm Shank → Wrist → TD
       ↑            ↑            ↑
  Suspension    Control Cable   Harness Anchor
 (Figure-8)     (Bowden)        (Shoulder)
```

**Five Subsystems (1 mark each):**

1. **Socket:** Custom-fabricated interface that transmits forces between stump and prosthesis. *Example: Humeroscapulothoracic socket for trans-humeral level.*

2. **Suspension System:** Holds the prosthesis on the body. *Example: Figure-8 shoulder harness with axilla loop.*

3. **Control System:** Conveys patient intent to the TD. *Example: Bowden cable driven by biscapular abduction (3 cm humeral flexion).*

4. **Joint Units:** Restore articulation. *Example: Internal elbow mechanism with manual lock (Hosmer or USMC).*

5. **Terminal Device (TD):** Performs prehension. *Example: Hosmer 5X hook (VO) or mechanical hand.*

**Integration Statement (1 mark):** The five subsystems operate in series; the patient's residual limb is the input, and the prehension output is at the TD. Proper alignment and force flow among all five are essential for functional use.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the capstan equation. For a small arc $d\phi$, the change in cable tension is:

$$\frac{dT}{T} = -\mu \, d\phi$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Integrate from input ($\phi = 0$, $T = F_{in}$) to output ($\phi = \theta$, $T = F_{out}$):

$$\int_{F_{in}}^{F_{out}} \frac{dT}{T} = -\int_{0}^{\theta} \mu \, d\phi$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Solve:

$$\ln F_{out} - \ln F_{in} = -\mu \theta$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Exponentiate:

$$\eta = \frac{F_{out}}{F_{in}} = e^{-\mu \theta}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Substitute $\mu = 0.10$, $\theta = \pi/2$ rad:

$$\eta = e^{-0.10 \times \pi/2} = e^{-0.1571} = 0.8545 \approx 85.5\%$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Compute force loss. If $F_{in} = 70$ N, $F_{out} = 0.8545 \times 70 = 59.8$ N. **Loss = 10.2 N (~14.5%).**

**Step 7 (1 mark):** **Design implications:**
- Route Bowden cable along the **largest possible bend radius** (avoid sharp 90° turns).
- Use **low-friction polymer-lined housings** ($\mu < 0.10$).
- For long cables (> 30 cm), use a **single straight run** rather than multiple bends.
- Use **multi-stranded cable** (more flexible, lower effective $\mu$).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model — Internal Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **working principle of a myoelectrically controlled upper-limb prosthesis**, with a block diagram of the signal-processing pipeline. List the typical EMG signal characteristics and the battery-power budget.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **body-powered** and **myoelectric** upper-limb prostheses on at least **six parameters** (mass, cost, sensory feedback, pinch force, reliability, maintenance, etc.). State one clinical scenario where each is preferred.

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
A myoelectric prosthesis detects the **electromyographic (EMG)** signals from residual muscles using surface electrodes. These signals (originally ~20–200 µV RMS in the 20–500 Hz band) are amplified, rectified, smoothed, and compared against a threshold. If the envelope exceeds the threshold, a controller drives a DC motor that operates the terminal device (open or close). In **proportional control**, the motor speed/force is proportional to the EMG amplitude; in **on/off control**, it is a binary state.

**Signal-Processing Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
Muscle → [EMG Electrode] → [Diff. Amplifier (×1000–5000)] →
[Bandpass 20–500 Hz] → [Full-Wave Rectifier] → [Smoother LPF 2–6 Hz] →
[Threshold Comparator w/ Hysteresis] → [Motor Driver PWM] → [DC Motor + Gearbox] → [TD]
```

**EMG Characteristics (1 mark):**
- Amplitude: **20–200 µV RMS** at skin surface
- Bandwidth: **20–500 Hz** (dominant energy 50–150 Hz)
- Source impedance: **~10 kΩ** at electrode-skin interface
- SNR: **20–40 dB** for good electrode placement

**Battery-Power Budget (1 mark):**
- Voltage: **7.2 V** (Li-ion)
- Capacity: **1500–2000 mAh**
- Average current: **~250 mA**
- Average power: **~1.8 W**
- Operating time: **~6–8 hours intermittent use / ~2 hours continuous**

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Body-Powered | Myoelectric |
|---|---|---|
| **Mass** | 250–600 g (lighter) | 400–900 g (heavier) |
| **Cost (USD)** | $1,500–$5,000 (cheaper) | $5,000–$30,000 (costlier) |
| **Sensory feedback** | **High** (cable tension) | Low (motor sound only) |
| **Pinch force** | 50–100 N (hook) | 60–100 N (hand) |
| **Reliability** | **Very high** (no electronics) | Moderate (electronics, sweat sensitivity) |
| **Maintenance** | Cable/harness ~1–2 yr; cheap | Electronics service, expensive |
| **Cosmesis** | Poor (cable visible) | **Excellent** (no cable) |
| **Battery** | None | Required, daily charging |
| **Water/sweat** | Tolerates well | **Fails** if electrodes wet |
| **Grip in cold** | OK | Reduced (muscle signal drops) |

**Clinical Scenarios (2 marks):**

- **Body-powered preferred for:** A **45-year-old male manual laborer** with a right trans-humeral amputation who works outdoors in construction and needs a **reliable, water-resistant, low-maintenance** prosthesis with strong sensory feedback for tool use.

- **Myoelectric preferred for:** A **28-year-old female office worker** with a left trans-radial amputation who prioritizes **cosmesis, light office tasks, and social acceptance** and has good muscle control in the residual limb.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3, Upper Limb Prosthesis Components**

### A. The 5 Subsystems (Mnemonic: **"S-S-C-J-T"**)
1. **S**ocket — biomechanical interface (PTB, NW, Muenster for BE; humeroscapulothoracic for AE).
2. **S**uspension — harness, suction, pin-lock, anatomical.
3. **C**ontrol — body-powered (Bowden cable) OR myoelectric (EMG) OR hybrid.
4. **J**oints — wrist unit, elbow unit, shoulder unit.
5. **T**erminal Device — hook, mechanical hand, myo hand, cosmetic hand.

### B. Bowden Cable (Top 3 Facts)
- Two parts: **inner stranded cable + outer housing**.
- Efficiency: $\eta = e^{-\mu\theta} \approx 1 - \mu\theta$.
- 90° bend at $\mu=0.10$ loses **~15%** of force.

### C. VO vs VC Hook (Top 3 Facts)
- **VO = Open by default** (rubber bands hold it open; pulling cable CLOSES it).
- **VC = Closed by default** (spring holds it closed; pulling cable OPENS it).
- VO is **energy-efficient for sustained grasp**; VC gives **adjustable grip force**.

### D. Myoelectric (Top 4 Facts)
- EMG signal: **20–200 µV RMS, 20–500 Hz**.
- Bandwidth filter: **20–500 Hz**; smoother: **2–6 Hz**.
- Power: **7.2 V Li-ion, ~1.5 W, ~8 h intermittent**.
- Motor back-EMF provides inherent speed regulation.

### E. Socket Mnemonic — "PT-MN-HS-SD-FQ"
- **PT**B (Patellar Tendon Bearing) → BE
- **M**uenster / **N**orthwestern → short BE
- **H**umeroscapulothoracic → AE
- **S**houlder disarticulation → SD socket
- **F**orequarter → thoracic extension socket

### F. Common Examiner Traps
1. Confusing **PTB** (patellar tendon) with "posterior."
2. Saying myoelectric is "always better" — *it is not; it has poor sensory feedback and sweat sensitivity.*
3. Forgetting the **cosmetic glove is non-structural**.
4. Mixing up **Figure-8** (AE) and **Figure-9** (BE) harnesses.
5. Stating **EMG frequency** as 0–20 Hz — *wrong, it is 20–500 Hz.*

### G. Numerical Constants to Memorize
- Pinch force: **50–100 N** (VO hook), **60–100 N** (myo hand).
- Cable force: **30–70 N** input.
- Bowden cable: $\mu$ = **0.05–0.15**.
- Mass: **< 0.5 kg** (BE), **< 1.5 kg** (shoulder disarticulation).
- Battery: **7.2 V, 1500 mAh** → ~**8 h** life.
- Cosmesis glove life: **6 months**.
- Socket life: **3–5 years**.

### H. One-Line Definition of Each Component
- **Socket:** Custom interface between stump and prosthesis.
- **Harness:** Strap system that suspends and controls the prosthesis.
- **Bowden cable:** Flexible force-transmission line (inner cable + outer housing).
- **Hook:** Two-fingered prehension device (can be VO or VC).
- **Mechanical hand:** Five-fingered cosmetic hand with internal mechanism.
- **Myoelectric hand:** Motor-driven hand controlled by EMG signals.
- **Wrist unit:** Connects TD to forearm; allows rotation/quick-change.
- **Elbow unit:** Restores flexion/extension at the elbow.
- **Cosmetic glove:** PVC/silicone outer cover (non-structural).

> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Components of the Upper Limb Prosthesis. All the best for KTU 2024 ESE!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT753 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cosmetic Prosthesis (Aesthetic / Passive Prosthesis):** A custom-fabricated medical device designed to replicate the **anatomical form, color, and surface texture** of a missing, congenitally absent, or traumatically deformed body part, with the **primary objective of restoring cosmetic appearance** rather than active functional movement.

In the context of KTU 2024 Rehabilitation Engineering (PEBMT753, Module 3), cosmetic prostheses are positioned alongside functional prostheses and body-powered / externally-powered devices. They serve a critical **psychosocial role** by reinstating the user's body image, social confidence, and integration into community life. Common clinical examples include:

- **Ocular prostheses** (artificial eyes)
- **Auricular prostheses** (artificial ears)
- **Nasal prostheses** (artificial noses)
- **Maxillofacial prostheses** (cheek, orbital, mid-facial)
- **Cosmetic hand / finger covers** (passive glove-type limbs)
- **Cosmetic shells** over existing functional prostheses

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology Distinction:** A *cosmetic prosthesis* is **NOT** the same as a *cosmetic cover/skin*. A cosmetic cover is a decorative sleeve worn OVER a functional mechanical limb. A cosmetic prosthesis is a **stand-alone restoration** of a missing body part.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a cosmetic prosthesis as a **"high-fidelity wax statue"** designed by a biomedical engineer instead of an artist. Imagine you break a precious porcelain doll. A master restorer does not just stick the pieces back — they sculpt a *new identical head* using silicone, hand-paint the iris in microscopic detail, embed real human hair strand by strand into the silicone, and mount it so seamlessly that even under bright light, the onlooker cannot tell which is the original and which is the replica.

A cosmetic prosthesis achieves the same goal — but on a **human being**, where the stakes are not just visual but also **psychological, social, and identity-related**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Context:** A 2017 WHO report estimates that approximately **1 in 1,000 newborns** worldwide is born with a congenital facial defect (e.g., microtia — underdeveloped ear). Road traffic accidents and oncological resections (cancer surgery) further drive demand. Cosmetic prostheses offer a non-surgical, reversible, and hygienic rehabilitation pathway.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Skin Surface Reflectance | **35 % – 65 %** | Determines color matching accuracy |
| Adult Iris Diameter | **11 mm – 12 mm** | Critical for ocular prosthesis fitting |
| Standard Auricular Height | **55 mm – 65 mm** | Anthropometric reference for ear prostheses |
| Silicone Cure Temperature | **23 °C – 180 °C** | Range for room-temp and high-temp vulcanizing silicones |
| Service Life (well-maintained) | **1.5 – 5 years** | Typical replacement cycle |
| Skin Tone Matching (CIELAB ΔE) | **≤ 2.0** | Perceptually indistinguishable color difference |
| Osseointegration Healing Period | **3 – 6 months** | For bone-anchored craniofacial prostheses |

---

## 1.4 Why Cosmetic Prostheses Matter in Rehabilitation

1. **Psychological restoration** — addresses grief, depression, and social anxiety.
2. **Non-invasive alternative** to repeated reconstructive surgery.
3. **Reversible** — can be removed, modified, or replaced.
4. **Hygienic and replaceable** — especially important for oncology patients.
5. **Anatomical protection** — covers sensitive graft sites or surgical cavities.
6. **Functional support** — restores facial symmetry, prevents drooling (labial prostheses), and supports eyeglasses (auricular).
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Cosmetic Prostheses

Cosmetic prostheses are classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

### Axis A — Anatomical Region
- **Craniofacial:** Ocular, Auricular, Nasal, Orbital, Mid-facial, Composite
- **Somato (Body) Prostheses:** Hand, Finger, Foot, Breast (external), Nipple-areolar

### Axis B — Retention Mechanism
- **Anatomical retention** — uses undercuts, cavities, or anatomic contours (e.g., ocular prosthesis seats in the enucleated socket)
- **Adhesive-retained** — uses medical-grade skin adhesives (e.g., Secure Medical Adhesive, Hollister)
- **Mechanically retained** — snaps onto spectacles, eyeglass frames, or a craniofacial bone-anchored abutment
- **Osseointegrated / Implant-retained** — uses titanium fixtures anchored into bone (Branemark system)
- **Magnetically retained** — rare-earth magnets (e.g., NdFeB) embedded in prosthesis and underlying cap

### Axis C — Material Composition
- **Polymer-based:** Silicone elastomers (most common), Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC), Polyurethane
- **Hybrid:** Silicone + acrylic base
- **3D-Printed Photopolymer Resins** (emerging)

---

## 2.2 Core Materials in Depth

### 2.2.1 Medical-Grade Silicone Elastomer (MDX4-4210, Silastic 732, etc.)
- **Why chosen:** Biocompatible (USP Class VI), hypoallergenic, UV-stable, colorable, and flexible to mimic skin pliability.
- **Two sub-types:**
  - **RTV (Room-Temperature Vulcanizing):** Mixed and poured; cures at ~23 °C.
  - **HTV (High-Temperature Vulcanizing):** Cured at ~180 °C; greater mechanical strength.

### 2.2.2 Acrylic (PMMA — Polymethyl Methacrylate)
- **Used for:** Ocular prostheses (white of the eye).
- **Properties:** Rigid, non-porous, easily polished, but does not flex like silicone.

### 2.2.3 Pigments and Intrinsic Coloring
- **Dry earth pigments, medical-grade silicone pigments** are intrinsically mixed into the base elastomer.
- **Extrinsic painting** is layered on the cured surface for veins, freckles, and micro-details.
- **Fiber inclusions:** Nylon or human hair for eyelashes, eyebrows, and scalp hair.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The reason silicone dominates facial prosthetics is its **low glass-transition temperature (Tg ≈ −125 °C)** — meaning it stays flexible at body temperature and mimics the soft-tissue modulus (≈ 0.1–1 MPa) far better than rigid acrylics.

---

## 2.3 The Color-Matching Pipeline (Theoretical)

Color matching is the most clinically critical and technically demanding step. The accepted industry standard is the **CIELAB (L\*a\*b\*) color space** defined by the **Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage (CIE)** in 1976.

### 2.3.1 The CIELAB Color Difference Equation

$$
\Delta E_{ab}^{\ast} = \sqrt{(L_2^{\ast} - L_1^{\ast})^2 + (a_2^{\ast} - a_1^{\ast})^2 + (b_2^{\ast} - b_1^{\ast})^2}
$$

Where:
- $L^{\ast}$ = Lightness (0 = black, 100 = white)
- $a^{\ast}$ = Green–Red axis (−128 = green, +127 = red)
- $b^{\ast}$ = Blue–Yellow axis (−128 = blue, +127 = yellow)

### 2.3.2 Color-Difference Acceptance Bands

| ΔE Value | Human Perception | KTU / Clinical Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| **< 1.0** | Imperceptible | Excellent (instrument match) |
| **1.0 – 2.0** | Perceptible only on close inspection | **Acceptable threshold** |
| **2.0 – 3.5** | Perceptible at conversational distance | Marginal; re-pigment |
| **> 3.5** | Clearly visible mismatch | Reject / re-fabricate |

---

## 2.4 Design Considerations (Engineering Side)

A cosmetic prosthesis must satisfy the **BIO-T-M-S-H** checklist (a KTU mnemonic):

| Letter | Criterion | Engineering Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| **B** | Biocompatibility | ISO 10993 cytotoxicity & sensitization tests |
| **I** | Iris / Inset alignment | Within 1 mm of contralateral side |
| **O** | Optical translucency | For thin skin (eyelid, lip) ≤ 1.5 mm wall |
| **T** | Thickness & Translucency | Mimics dermal light scattering (Lambertian) |
| **M** | Margins (feathered) | Blends into natural skin crease |
| **S** | Surface texture | Captures pores, wrinkles, micro-relief |
| **H** | Hygiene / cleanability | Non-porous surface, alcohol-wipe tolerant |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these for any numerical / design problem in the exam.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CIELAB color difference | $\Delta E = \sqrt{(\Delta L)^2 + (\Delta a)^2 + (\Delta b)^2}$ | Dimensionless | Color-matching verification |
| 2 | Spectral reflectance integration | $R = \int_{\lambda_1}^{\lambda_2} S(\lambda) \cdot \rho(\lambda) \, d\lambda$ | — | Skin surface reflectance |
| 3 | Tear strength of silicone | $T_s = F / t$ | N/mm | Material selection |
| 4 | Modulus of elasticity (skin mimic) | $E \approx 0.1$ to $\vert 1 \vert$ | MPa | Silicone shore hardness |
| 5 | Osseointegration pull-out force | $F_{po} = \sigma_{bone} \cdot A_{thread}$ | N | Implant retention |
| 6 | Curing shrinkage compensation | $L_{final} = L_{cast} (1 + \alpha \Delta T)$ | mm | Mold design |
| 7 | Service life (UV aging) | $t_{life} \approx k \cdot t_{E50}$ | years | Replacement scheduling |
| 8 | Surface area to weight ratio | $SA:W = A_{skin} / m_{pros}$ | cm²/g | Comfort assessment |
| 9 | Hygienic Porosity limit | $P \leq 0.5 \ \vert \text{percent} \vert$ | % | Anti-bacterial criterion |
| 10 | Skin thermal conductivity | $k_{skin} \approx 0.2$ to $\vert 0.5 \vert$ | W/m·K | Silicone simulation target |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations power the **CAD/CAM digital workflow** for facial prosthetics — from 3D scanning the contralateral side, mirror-imaging it, slicing the geometry, and feeding it to a multi-material 3D printer. The CIELAB equation specifically is the **acceptance gate** in any modern color-matching protocol.

---

## 2.6 Why This Knowledge Matters in Real Engineering

- **Clinical:** Enables anaplastologists to deliver a prosthesis that passes the "social-distance test."
- **Industrial:** Drives the multi-million-dollar craniofacial prosthetics market (e.g., **Straits Orthodontics, Technovent, Modern Art Foundry**).
- **Research:** Underpins 3D bioprinting of skin analogs and pigmented tissue engineering.
- **Defense / Forensics:** Cosmetic covers on prosthetics help in identification of soldiers and accident victims.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Workflow: From Patient Consultation to Delivered Prosthesis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this 10-step pipeline — it is the most testable diagram in this module.**

### Step 1 — Patient Assessment and Impression Taking
- The anaplastologist evaluates the defect dimensions, surrounding skin condition, mobility, and the patient's expectations.
- An **impression** of the defect site is taken using irreversible hydrocolloid (alginate) or medical-grade silicone putty.
- The patient's **contralateral (mirror-image) side** is also captured, often by 3D surface scanning (e.g., Artec Eva, Vectra H1).

### Step 2 — Working Cast (Plaster / 3D-Printed Resin Model)
- The impression is poured with Type III dental stone or scanned and 3D printed.
- This working cast replicates the defect surface with micrometer accuracy.

### Step 3 — Sculpting the Master Pattern
- A wax-up (often modeling clay or sculpting wax) is shaped **directly on the cast** to mimic the missing anatomy.
- For paired organs (eye, ear), the **contralateral side is mirrored** digitally or by hand.

### Step 4 — Try-In Verification
- The wax pattern is placed on the patient.
- Margins, alignment, projection, and symmetry are verified.
- Patient feedback is incorporated before final fabrication.

### Step 5 — Mold Fabrication
- A **multi-part mold** (typically 3-piece or 4-piece) is made using dental stone or RTV silicone rubber.
- The mold accommodates undercuts, parting lines, and injection channels.

### Step 6 — Silicone Packing and Intrinsic Pigmentation
- Medical-grade silicone is mixed with base pigments (red, yellow, brown, black, white) to match the patient's skin base tone.
- The silicone is **packed into the mold** in thin, layered increments to mimic dermal depth gradient.

### Step 7 — Curing
- The mold is clamped and placed in a **pressure pot** (to remove air bubbles) or a controlled-temperature oven.
- **RTV cure:** 23 °C for 24 hours.
- **HTV cure:** 180 °C for 1–2 hours (in a post-cure oven).

### Step 8 — Deflashing and Margin Feathered
- Once cured, the prosthesis is carefully demolded.
- Excess flash (material spill from mold seam) is trimmed with fine scissors / scalpel.
- Margins are feathered to a **sub-millimeter edge** so they blend with the natural skin crease.

### Step 9 — Extrinsic Coloration and Detailing
- Surface detail is added: **freckles, veins, capillary networks, moles**.
- Veins are typically drawn with cotton threads of blue-red pigment under a thin layer of clear silicone glaze.
- The iris (for ocular prostheses) is hand-painted on a separate disc, laminated between two clear PMMA layers, and set into the scleral blank.

### Step 10 — Final Delivery, Retention Fitting, and Patient Education
- Retention is verified (adhesive test, magnetic snap, or osseointegration abutment).
- Patient is educated on **insertion, removal, cleaning, and storage**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Retention Method Trade-Offs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This 4×6 matrix is a high-yield KTU Part B question generator.**

| Retention Method | Retention Force | Pros | Cons | Indications | Typical Service |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Anatomical** | Variable; depends on undercut depth | Zero consumables, easy insertion | Limited to well-defined defects | Ocular, partial ear | 3–5 years |
| **Adhesive** | 0.5 – 2.0 N/cm² | Non-invasive, universal | Daily application, skin irritation | Nose, large facial | 6–12 months (adhesive cycles) |
| **Spectacle-mounted** | Mechanical interlock | No skin contact | Cosmetically limited | Auricular, nasal | 1–2 years |
| **Osseointegrated** | 10 – 30 N per abutment | Secure, hygienic, daily-wear | Surgical, 3–6 month healing, cost | Auricular, orbital, nasal | 5–10 years |
| **Magnetic** | 2 – 5 N | Easy snap-on, no skin glue | Limited strength, corrosion risk | Mid-facial, finger | 1–3 years |
| **Combination** | Engineered | Best of all worlds | Complex design | Composite defects | 3–5 years |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Color-Match Derivation (CIELAB Application)

**Problem:** A patient's contralateral (reference) skin measured in CIELAB coordinates is $L_1^{\ast} = 65.2$, $a_1^{\ast} = 18.4$, $b_1^{\ast} = 22.7$. The trial prosthesis is measured as $L_2^{\ast} = 64.5$, $a_2^{\ast} = 17.9$, $b_2^{\ast} = 21.6$. **Determine if the prosthesis is clinically acceptable.**

**Step 1 — Compute the channel-wise differences:**

$$
\Delta L^{\ast} = L_2^{\ast} - L_1^{\ast} = 64.5 - 65.2 = -0.7
$$

$$
\Delta a^{\ast} = a_2^{\ast} - a_1^{\ast} = 17.9 - 18.4 = -0.5
$$

$$
\Delta b^{\ast} = b_2^{\ast} - b_1^{\ast} = 21.6 - 22.7 = -1.1
$$

**Step 2 — Square each difference:**

$$
(\Delta L^{\ast})^2 = (-0.7)^2 = 0.49
$$

$$
(\Delta a^{\ast})^2 = (-0.5)^2 = 0.25
$$

$$
(\Delta b^{\ast})^2 = (-1.1)^2 = 1.21
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the squared differences:**

$$
\Sigma = 0.49 + 0.25 + 1.21 = 1.95
$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root to obtain ΔE:**

$$
\Delta E = \sqrt{1.95} \approx 1.396
$$

**Step 5 — Compare against the clinical acceptance threshold:**

$$
\Delta E \approx 1.40 \ \ \Longrightarrow \ \ 1.0 \leq \Delta E \leq 2.0
$$

**Step 6 — Verdict:**

> [!NOTE]
> **ΔE ≈ 1.40 falls within the "Acceptable Threshold" band (1.0 – 2.0). The prosthesis may be released; however, a marginal re-pigmentation with a slight red-orange boost is recommended to push ΔE below 1.0 for instrument-level perfection.**

**Conversion logic summary:** This step-by-step procedure is the **universal color acceptance test** in any modern anaplastology lab. A KTU question can simply swap the numbers and ask the student to repeat the computation.

---

## 3.4 Service Life and Material Aging Derivation

**Problem:** A silicone ear prosthesis shows an Arrhenius-type UV-degradation rate constant of $k = 0.05$ per year at body temperature. Using the simple first-order decay model of color stability, after how many years will the prosthesis exhibit a perceptible color shift (ΔE > 3.5)?

The first-order decay of color stability is given by:

$$
C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-k t}
$$

The perceptible shift condition is met when:

$$
\Delta E = \Delta E_0 \cdot (1 - e^{-k t}) \geq 3.5
$$

For an initial color difference $\Delta E_0 = 8.0$ (typical for a fresh prosthesis when first compared to aged skin):

$$
1 - e^{-k t} = \frac{3.5}{8.0} = 0.4375
$$

$$
e^{-k t} = 1 - 0.4375 = 0.5625
$$

$$
-k t = \ln(0.5625)
$$

$$
t = -\frac{\ln(0.5625)}{k} = -\frac{(-0.5754)}{0.05}
$$

$$
t \approx 11.5 \ \text{years}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Although the math suggests > 10 years, in practice the average **service life is 2 – 5 years** because the patient's own skin tone changes (sun exposure, aging) faster than the prosthesis, creating a relative mismatch. The **KTU takeaway** is that *replacement is patient-driven, not material-driven*.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example: Osseointegrated Retention Pull-Out Force

**Given:** A craniofacial bone-anchored auricular prosthesis uses a titanium abutment with effective thread surface area $A = 25 \ \text{mm}^2$. Bone ultimate shear stress $\sigma_{bone} \approx 70 \ \text{MPa}$. Find the theoretical pull-out force.

**Step 1 — Apply the simple stress equation:**

$$
F_{po} = \sigma_{bone} \cdot A_{thread}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values (with unit conversion $25 \ \text{mm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^2$):**

$$
F_{po} = (70 \times 10^{6} \ \text{Pa}) \cdot (25 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^2)
$$

$$
F_{po} = 70 \cdot 25 = 1750 \ \text{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply a safety factor of 3 (clinical standard):**

$$
F_{safe} = \frac{1750}{3} \approx 583 \ \text{N}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This means a single osseointegrated abutment can safely retain over **58 kg** of prosthetic load — far more than the ~0.5 N typical daily use. This redundancy is intentional, allowing for impact loads, child play, and accidental tugging.
