<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define an Upper Extremity Orthosis and list its four primary goals.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** An **Upper Extremity Orthosis (UEO)** is an externally applied biomedical device fitted to the shoulder, arm, elbow, forearm, wrist, hand, or fingers to support, align, prevent, or correct deformity, and to improve functional capability of the upper limb (as per **ISO 8549-3**).
>
> **Four Primary Goals (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Protection** — guard healing tissues (fractures, tendons, nerves)
> 2. **Immobilization / Rest** — eliminate unwanted joint motion
> 3. **Mobilization / Functional substitution** — replace lost muscle action
> 4. **Correction / Prevention** — apply forces to reverse or prevent deformity

---

### **Q2. What is the Three-Point Pressure System? Why is it fundamental to orthotic design?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** The **Three-Point Pressure System (3-PPS)** is the **universal biomechanical principle** of orthotic force application in which **three discrete force vectors** act on a body segment — **one corrective force** in one direction, and **two counter-forces** in the opposite direction, all separated by **lever arms** about a joint axis. The system produces a **force couple** that creates rotational equilibrium.
>
> **Why fundamental (1 mark):** It allows a clinician to **control joint motion** using a **rigid external frame**, transmitting forces through the skin to bones. By adjusting the **magnitude, location, and direction** of the three forces, any joint can be **immobilized, restricted, or mobilized** — making 3-PPS the **engineering foundation** of every upper extremity orthosis.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A — Full 14-Mark Question** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Three-Point Pressure System** as applied to a **shoulder abduction orthosis (Airplane Splint)**. Derive the relationship between corrective force, lever arm, and deforming moment. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition and Setup (2 marks):**
The Three-Point Pressure System (3-PPS) requires three collinear force vectors acting on a body segment, separated by lever arms, such that the sum of moments about the joint axis is zero. In a shoulder abduction orthosis holding the arm at **90° abduction**, the three forces are:

- **F₁ (Iliac crest pad)** — counter-force acting **UP** (pushes pelvis down)
- **F₂ (Chest pad, on the contralateral thorax at the level of the humeral head)** — **corrective force** acting **DOWNWARD** toward the joint
- **F₃ (Wrist/forearm cuff pad)** — counter-force acting **UP** (supports the weight of the forearm)

**2. Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
        F3 (UP)  ----------------  F2 (DOWN)
           \                       /
            \                     /
             \   ARM at 90°      /
              \   ABDUCTION     /
               \               /
                \             /
                 \           /
                  \         /
                   \       /
                    \     /
                     \   /
                      \ /
                   [SHOULDER JOINT AXIS]
                      / \
                     /   \
                    /     \
                   /       F1 (UP - Iliac Crest Pad)
```

**3. Derivation (3 marks):**

For rotational equilibrium about the shoulder joint axis:

$$\sum M_{shoulder} = 0$$

$$F_2 \cdot d_{chest} - F_g \cdot L_{arm} = 0$$

$$F_2 = \frac{F_g \cdot L_{arm}}{d_{chest}}$$

Where:
- $F_g$ = weight of the arm + forearm (deforming force)
- $L_{arm}$ = distance from shoulder joint to arm's center of mass
- $d_{chest}$ = distance from shoulder joint to chest pad

**4. Design Insight:** The **mechanical advantage** of the system is:

$$MA = \frac{d_{chest}}{L_{arm}}$$

Larger chest pad lever arm → smaller required corrective force → more comfort.

---

**Q. (b)** Classify **Upper Extremity Orthoses** based on **(i) function** and **(ii) anatomical region**. For each anatomical region, give **two clinical examples** with indication. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Functional Classification (3 marks):**

> **A. Static Orthosis** — No moving parts; holds a fixed position.
> - *Example:* Resting pan splint, Stack splint, Sarmiento brace.
>
> **B. Dynamic Orthosis** — Has moving parts (springs, hinges, rubber bands, motors) to apply continuous force or substitute lost function.
> - *Example:* Kleinert splint (post-flexor tendon repair), Wrist-driven tenodesis orthosis, Hinged elbow orthosis.

**2. Anatomical Classification (4 marks):**

| Region | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Shoulder (S)** | Shoulder Immobilizer — *Indication:* post-dislocation, humeral fracture | Airplane Splint (SAO) — *Indication:* brachial plexus injury, post-burn axillary contracture |
| **Elbow (E)** | Static Elbow Orthosis — *Indication:* post-burn, healing fracture | Dynamic Hinged Elbow — *Indication:* post-surgical stiffness, C5–C6 tetraplegia |
| **Wrist-Hand (WH)** | Wrist Cock-up Splint (WHO) — *Indication:* radial nerve palsy, carpal tunnel syndrome | Tenodesis Orthosis — *Indication:* C6 spinal cord injury |
| **Finger (F)** | Stack Splint — *Indication:* mallet finger | Anti-Boutonnière Splint — *Indication:* central slip injury |

---

### **QUESTION B — Alternative 14-Mark Question** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the **biomechanical design** of a **Wrist Cock-up Splint** for a radial nerve palsy patient. Show the **force couple** calculation assuming a deforming moment of 2.4 N·m, hand lever arm of 9 cm, and corrective cuff lever arm of 12 cm. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Clinical Justification (2 marks):**
In **radial nerve palsy** (commonly due to humeral shaft fracture), the patient loses active wrist extension, MCP extension, and thumb extension — producing **wrist drop** and inability to grasp objects. The **Wrist Cock-up Splint (WHO)** holds the wrist in **20°–30° extension**, restoring the natural **tenodesis effect** (finger flexion occurs automatically when the wrist is extended, due to passive tendon tension).

**2. Biomechanical Design (2 marks):**
The splint applies a **3-PPS** with:
- F₁ (palmar forearm cuff) — corrective force pushing wrist into extension
- F₂ & F₃ (dorsal hand piece and proximal dorsal strap) — counter-forces

**3. Force Couple Calculation (3 marks):**

Given:
- Deforming moment $M_g = 2.4$ N·m (gravity on the hand)
- Hand lever arm $L_h = 0.09$ m
- Corrective cuff lever arm $d_c = 0.12$ m

**Step 1: Calculate deforming force (hand weight):**
$$F_g = \frac{M_g}{L_h} = \frac{2.4}{0.09} = 26.67 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Apply moment equilibrium:**
$$F_c \cdot d_c = F_g \cdot L_h$$
$$F_c = \frac{2.4}{0.12} = 20.0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Interface pressure check (assume $A_{pad} = 0.0025$ m²):**
$$P = \frac{F_c}{A_{pad}} = \frac{20.0}{0.0025} = 8000 \text{ Pa} = 8.0 \text{ kPa}$$

**Result:** $8.0$ kPa **exceeds** the safe limit of **6.7 kPa** — recommend increasing pad area to **≥ 29.85 cm²** to prevent pressure ulcers.

---

**Q. (b)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working of a **Wrist-Driven Tenodesis Orthosis** for a **C6 spinal cord injury** patient. What is the underlying physiological principle that makes it work? **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Clinical Context (1 mark):**
A patient with a **C6 complete spinal cord injury** has:
- ✅ **Active wrist extension** (via extensor carpi radialis longus — C6)
- ❌ No active finger flexion
- ❌ No active thumb opposition

**2. Physiological Principle — Tenodesis Effect (2 marks):**
The **tenodesis effect** is the natural biomechanical coupling of the long finger flexor tendons (FDP, FDS) to wrist motion. When the wrist is **passively extended**, the flexor tendons are pulled taut, causing **automatic passive finger flexion** (power grip). Conversely, **wrist flexion** releases the tendons, allowing **finger extension** (release).

**3. Functional Block Diagram (2 marks):**
```
[Voluntary Wrist Extension]
        ↓
[Cable/Linkage System]
        ↓
[Tension to MCP Flexors + Thumb Opponens]
        ↓
[Power Grip Activated → Hold Cup, Fork, Toothbrush]
        ↓
[Voluntary Wrist Flexion → Release Object]
```

**4. Orthosis Components (1 mark):**
- **Dorsal forearm shell** with wrist hinge
- **MCP flexion assist** via outrigger and rubber-band traction
- **Thumb opposition post** to position thumb in opposition
- **Adjustable tension** to fine-tune grip force

**5. Functional Outcome (1 mark):**
The patient regains **active grasp and release** without external power — a textbook example of **leveraging residual function** to **substitute for lost function**, embodying the **ultimate goal of rehabilitation engineering**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in UEO Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the SAFE INTERFACE PRESSURE LIMIT of 6.7 kPa (50 mmHg).** Always compute and comment on interface pressure in any numerical problem. **(−2 marks if missed)**
>
> 2. **Writing only "Three-Point Pressure" without explaining force directions.** KTU examiners expect **explicit mention** of which force is corrective and which two are counter-forces. **(−1 mark)**
>
> 3. **Confusing STATIC vs DYNAMIC orthoses.** Static = no moving parts; Dynamic = hinges/springs/motors. Misclassification loses 2–3 marks.
>
> 4. **Skipping the diagram.** A "neat block diagram" is **MANDATORY** for any 7-mark question involving biomechanics. **(−2 marks minimum if absent)**
>
> 5. **Not stating the ISO standard (ISO 8549-3)** or the **acronym convention (W-H-F-E-S-O-D)** when defining an orthosis. Examiners reward precise terminology.
>
> 6. **Forgetting units in numerical problems.** Always write **N, m, N·m, kPa, °, mm**. A dimensionless answer is treated as incomplete.
>
> 7. **Neglecting the "design correction" step** — if computed pressure exceeds 6.7 kPa, you MUST suggest a corrective redesign (larger pad, additional foam).

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Upper Extremity Orthoses**

- ✅ **Definition (ISO 8549-3):** Externally applied device to influence neuro-musculoskeletal characteristics of the upper limb.
- ✅ **Acronym Convention:** W (Wrist) – H (Hand) – F (Finger) – E (Elbow) – S (Shoulder) – O (Orthosis); D (Dynamic).
- ✅ **Two Master Types:** STATIC (no moving parts) and DYNAMIC (springs / hinges / motors).
- ✅ **Three-Point Pressure System (3-PPS):** 1 corrective force + 2 counter-forces, collinear, separated by lever arms, producing rotational equilibrium ($\sum M = 0$).
- ✅ **Core Formula:** $F_c = F_{ext} \cdot (d_{ext} / d_c)$ — derived from moment balance.
- ✅ **SAFE INTERFACE PRESSURE:** Must stay **below 6.7 kPa (50 mmHg)** to prevent pressure ulcers (capillary closing pressure ≈ 4.3 kPa).
- ✅ **Shoulder Region:** Airplane Splint (SAO), Shoulder Immobilizer, SEWHO — used for burns, dislocations, brachial plexus injuries.
- ✅ **Elbow Region:** Static elbow orthosis (rest), Hinged/dynamic elbow orthosis (mobilization).
- ✅ **Wrist-Hand Region:** Wrist Cock-up Splint (radial nerve palsy), Tenodesis Orthosis (C6 SCI), Resting Pan (RA).
- ✅ **Finger Region:** Stack Splint (mallet), Figure-of-Eight (boutonnière), Silver Ring (swan neck), Kleinert/Duran (flexor tendon).
- ✅ **Material of Choice:** Low-temperature thermoplastics (Polypropylene, **E ≈ 1.5–2.0 GPa**, 3.2 mm thickness) for body-worn orthoses.
- ✅ **Tenodesis Principle:** Wrist extension → passive finger flexion; this is the **physiological hack** that makes C6 SCI patients grasp objects.
- ✅ **Mechanical Advantage (MA):** $MA = d_{effort} / d_{load}$ — greater lever arm = greater MA = smaller required muscle force.
- ✅ **Goal of any UEO:** Restore **ADL capability**, quantified by the **Functional Independence Measure (FIM: 18–126)**.
- ✅ **Engineering Trade-off:** Larger lever arm → smaller force needed, but bulkier orthosis. Balance is the designer's art.
- ✅ **Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of the upper limb ≈ 27** — the highest in the human body, making UEO design uniquely challenging.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intelligent Mobility Aids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT753 - REHABILILATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Intelligent Mobility Aids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intelligent Mobility Aids (IMAs)** are advanced, sensor-integrated, computer-controlled mobility devices that augment or restore independent locomotion for persons with physical disabilities by combining conventional assistive platforms (manual/powered wheelchairs, walkers, scooters) with embedded intelligence, environmental perception, and adaptive control algorithms.

In the context of **PEBMT753 – Rehabilitation Engineering (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4)**, Intelligent Mobility Aids are defined as **mechatronic systems** that fuse:

1. **Mechanical Base Platform** – The physical chassis (wheelchair frame, rollator walker, exoskeleton).
2. **Perception Layer** – Sensors (ultrasonic, IR, LiDAR, RGB-D cameras, IMU).
3. **Cognition Layer** – Microcontroller/processor running path-planning, SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping), and obstacle-avoidance algorithms.
4. **Actuation Layer** – Motor drivers, servo-controlled steering, and differential-drive wheels.
5. **Human–Machine Interface (HMI)** – Joysticks, sip-and-puff, voice recognition, EEG/EOG, or BCI.

The defining property is **autonomy or semi-autonomy** — the device makes *decisions* on behalf of (or in collaboration with) the user.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** As per Module 4 of the syllabus, an intelligent mobility aid is a *rehabilitation device that integrates sensors, actuators, and computational intelligence to provide navigational assistance, environmental awareness, and adaptive control for users with mobility impairments.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Self-Driving Wheelchair":**
> Imagine a conventional wheelchair that you push manually. Now, give it **eyes** (cameras, ultrasonic sensors), a **brain** (a small onboard computer), and **muscles that obey instructions** (motor-driven wheels). The result is a device that can "see" a wall, "decide" to turn left, and "move" — all without the user steering. It behaves like a **Tesla in miniature**, but for a person who cannot walk.

**Plain-English Summary:**
- **Conventional aid** = User does *all* the thinking and physical work.
- **Powered aid** = User provides *intent*; motors do the physical work.
- **Intelligent aid** = User provides *intent*; the system does the thinking **and** the physical work, with safety guarantees.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Intelligent Mobility Aids

| **Category** | **Typical User** | **Intelligence Level** |
|---|---|---|
| **Smart Powered Wheelchair (SPW)** | Quadriplegia, ALS, elderly | Semi-autonomous / Autonomous |
| **Intelligent Walker / Rollator** | Elderly, balance-impaired | Assistive navigation |
| **Robotic Exoskeleton (Lower Limb)** | Spinal cord injury (T6–L1) | Gait-restoration + obstacle response |
| **Autonomous Guided Vehicle (AGV) for Disabled** | Visually impaired (e.g., GuideCane) | Full autonomous guidance |
| **BCI-Controlled Wheelchair** | Locked-in syndrome | Brain-signal-driven |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** Clinical trials for IMAs use the **"Performance Measure of Intelligent Wheelchair Systems"** which scores tasks such as door traversal, corridor following, and obstacle avoidance on a **1–5 Likert scale**, where **1 = no autonomy** and **5 = full autonomy**.

---

## 1.4 Key Subsystems — The "Five Senses" of an IMA

1. **Sight (Exteroception):** Ultrasonic (range 2 cm – 400 cm), Infrared (range 10–80 cm), LiDAR (range up to 30 m), RGB-D cameras.
2. **Balance (Proprioception):** IMU — Accelerometer + Gyroscope + Magnetometer (9-DOF typical).
3. **Touch (Force/Contact):** Bumper switches, capacitive proximity sensors.
4. **Hearing (Auditory Cues):** Microphone arrays for voice control and acoustic event detection.
5. **Memory (Mapping):** SLAM-generated occupancy grid of the environment (resolution typically 5–10 cm/cell).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Layered Model

An Intelligent Mobility Aid operates on a **Sense → Think → Act** closed-loop paradigm. The KTU 2024 syllabus frames this as a **3-tier hierarchical control architecture**:

- **Tier 1 — Low-Level (Reflexive):** Real-time motor control, encoder feedback, current limiting. Loop time: **1–10 ms**.
- **Tier 2 — Mid-Level (Reactive):** Obstacle avoidance, wall-following, speed adaptation. Loop time: **50–200 ms**.
- **Tier 3 — High-Level (Deliberative):** Path planning, SLAM, goal-directed navigation. Loop time: **500 ms – 2 s**.

### 2.1.1 Why Three Tiers?
- **Safety** demands fast reaction (Tier 1) — a wheel cannot wait 2 s to stop.
- **Smoothness** demands medium-speed reactivity (Tier 2) — turn before you crash.
- **Efficiency** demands slow deliberation (Tier 3) — plan the shortest safe route.

---

## 2.2 Sensor Fusion — The Mathematical Heart

Modern IMAs use **Kalman Filter** or **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** to fuse IMU + wheel odometry + LiDAR data into a single pose estimate.

**Discrete-time state model:**

$$\mathbf{x}_k = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{x}_{k-1} + \mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{u}_k + \mathbf{w}_k$$

**Observation model:**

$$\mathbf{z}_k = \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{v}_k$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{x}_k$ = state vector at step $k$ — typically $\begin{bmatrix} x & y & \theta \end{bmatrix}^T$ (position + heading).
- $\mathbf{F}_k$ = state transition matrix.
- $\mathbf{B}_k$ = control input matrix.
- $\mathbf{u}_k$ = control input (wheel velocities $v_L, v_R$).
- $\mathbf{w}_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{Q}_k)$ = process noise.
- $\mathbf{H}_k$ = observation matrix.
- $\mathbf{v}_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{R}_k)$ = measurement noise.

---

## 2.3 Differential Drive Kinematics

A standard smart wheelchair uses a **two-wheel differential drive**. Given wheel angular velocities $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$:

$$v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_L + \omega_R)$$

$$\omega = \frac{r}{2L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$$

Where:
- $v$ = linear velocity of the wheelchair (m/s).
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s).
- $r$ = wheel radius (m).
- $L$ = distance between wheels (track width) (m).
- $2L$ is the wheelbase parameter — **not** the actual wheelbase (which is $2L$ here; some texts use $d$).

**Pose update (Euler integration):**

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \Delta t \cos(\theta_k)$$

$$y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \Delta t \sin(\theta_k)$$

$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \Delta t$$

---

## 2.4 Obstacle Avoidance — Virtual Force Field (VFF) Method

In the **VFF method** (Borenstein & Koren, 1989), each obstacle exerts a **repulsive force** on the wheelchair, while the goal exerts an **attractive force**.

$$F_{total} = F_{attract} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} F_{repulse,i}$$

**Repulsive force from obstacle $i$ at distance $d_i$:**

$$F_{repulse,i} = \frac{K_{rep}}{(d_i + \epsilon)^2} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{r}}_i$$

**Attractive force from goal at distance $d_g$:**

$$F_{attract} = K_{att} \cdot d_g \cdot \hat{\mathbf{g}}$$

Where $\hat{\mathbf{r}}_i$ is the unit vector from obstacle $i$ to the wheelchair, $\hat{\mathbf{g}}$ is the unit vector from wheelchair to goal, and $K_{rep}, K_{att}$ are tunable gains.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Equation** | **Variable Definitions** | **Units** | **Typical Values** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Linear velocity (diff. drive) | $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_L + \omega_R)$ | $r$ = wheel radius; $\omega_L, \omega_R$ = wheel speeds | m/s | 0.2 – 1.5 m/s |
| Angular velocity (diff. drive) | $\omega = \frac{r}{2L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | $L$ = half track width | rad/s | $\leq 1.5$ rad/s |
| Ultrasonic range | $d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $c$ = speed of sound ($\approx 343$ m/s); $\Delta t$ = echo time | m | 0.02 – 4.0 |
| Sampling time (Tier 1) | $T_s$ | Loop period | ms | 1 – 10 |
| SLAM grid resolution | $r_g$ | Cell size | m/cell | 0.05 – 0.10 |
| Battery autonomy | $E = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | $V$ = voltage, $I$ = current, $t$ = hours | Wh | 200 – 600 |
| VFF repulsive force | $F_{rep} = \frac{K_{rep}}{(d + \epsilon)^2}$ | $K_{rep}$ = gain, $d$ = distance, $\epsilon$ = small constant | N | $K_{rep} = 5$–$50$ |
| Sonar beam cone | $\theta_{cone}$ | Half-angle of beam | degrees | 15° – 30° |
| IMU update rate | $f_{IMU}$ | Sampling frequency | Hz | 50 – 200 |
| Max safe slope | $\alpha_{max}$ | Incline threshold | degrees | 8° – 12° |
| Stopping distance | $d_{stop} = \frac{v^2}{2\mu g}$ | $\mu$ = friction, $g$ = 9.81 m/s² | m | 0.3 – 1.0 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Always quote the speed of sound $c = 343$ m/s** (at 20°C) in ultrasonic ranging problems. KTU examiners penalize this as a "common constant error" if omitted.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospital Logistics:** Autonomous wheelchairs for patient transport in large hospital complexes (e.g., deployed at Singapore General Hospital).
- **Elderly Care:** Smart rollators with fall prediction (sensitivity > 95%) reduce hip-fracture incidence.
- **Disaster Response:** Exoskeletons originally built for paraplegics are now adapted for first-responder load-bearing.
- **Space:** NASA’s **X1 Robotic Exoskeleton** (a NASA-JSC + IHMC collaboration) is a direct descendant of mobility-aid research.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Differential-Drive Forward Kinematics

We derive the pose update of a wheelchair from the wheel angular velocities.

**Step 1 — Define the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR).**
For a differential-drive robot with two wheels separated by distance $2L$, the ICR lies on the line connecting the wheels. If the right wheel moves faster, the ICR is to the left.

**Step 2 — Compute the angular velocity of the chassis.**
The right wheel moves at linear speed $v_R = r \omega_R$. The left wheel moves at $v_L = r \omega_L$. The difference produces a rotation:

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{2L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{2L}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the linear velocity at the chassis midpoint.**
The midpoint velocity is the average of both wheel velocities:

$$v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate to get pose update over time interval $\Delta t$.**
Assuming $\Delta t$ is small (Euler integration), the position increments are:

$$\Delta x = v \cos(\theta) \cdot \Delta t$$

$$\Delta y = v \sin(\theta) \cdot \Delta t$$

$$\Delta \theta = \omega \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 5 — Final discrete update equations:**

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + v \Delta t \cos(\theta_k)$$

$$y_{k+1} = y_k + v \Delta t \sin(\theta_k)$$

$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega \Delta t$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **exact** form tested in KTU Module 4 numericals. Marks are awarded for stating the **assumption of small $\Delta t$** and the **Euler approximation** explicitly.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Pose Update After 2 Seconds

**Given:** A smart wheelchair with $r = 0.15$ m, $L = 0.25$ m, initial pose $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0) = (0, 0, 0)$ rad. Wheel speeds: $\omega_L = 2$ rad/s, $\omega_R = 3$ rad/s. Compute the pose at $t = 2$ s using $\Delta t = 0.1$ s.

**Step 1 — Compute linear and angular velocities.**

$$v = \frac{0.15}{2}(2 + 3) = \frac{0.15 \times 5}{2} = \frac{0.75}{2} = 0.375 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\omega = \frac{0.15}{2 \times 0.25}(3 - 2) = \frac{0.15}{0.5}(1) = 0.30 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Update for one step (k = 0 → 1):**

$$x_1 = 0 + 0.375 \times 0.1 \times \cos(0) = 0 + 0.0375 = 0.0375 \text{ m}$$

$$y_1 = 0 + 0.375 \times 0.1 \times \sin(0) = 0 \text{ m}$$

$$\theta_1 = 0 + 0.30 \times 0.1 = 0.030 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Update for k = 1 → 2:**

$$x_2 = 0.0375 + 0.375 \times 0.1 \times \cos(0.030) = 0.0375 + 0.0375 \times 0.9996 = 0.07500 \text{ m}$$

$$y_2 = 0 + 0.375 \times 0.1 \times \sin(0.030) = 0.0375 \times 0.0300 = 0.001125 \text{ m}$$

$$\theta_2 = 0.030 + 0.30 \times 0.1 = 0.060 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4 — Iterate 20 times ($t = 2$ s) to reach final pose.**

**General form of closed-form solution (for constant $\omega$ and $v$):**

$$x(t) = \frac{v}{\omega}[\sin(\theta_0 + \omega t) - \sin(\theta_0)]$$

$$y(t) = \frac{v}{\omega}[\cos(\theta_0) - \cos(\theta_0 + \omega t)]$$

**Step 5 — Plug in the constants:**

$$x(2) = \frac{0.375}{0.30}[\sin(0 + 0.30 \times 2) - \sin(0)] = 1.25 \times \sin(0.60) = 1.25 \times 0.5646 = 0.7058 \text{ m}$$

$$y(2) = \frac{0.375}{0.30}[\cos(0) - \cos(0.60)] = 1.25 \times (1 - 0.8253) = 1.25 \times 0.1747 = 0.2184 \text{ m}$$

$$\theta(2) = 0 + 0.30 \times 2 = 0.60 \text{ rad} \approx 34.38°$$

**Final Pose:** $(0.706, 0.218, 0.60 \text{ rad})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The wheelchair traces an **arc of a circle** with radius $R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.375}{0.30} = 1.25$ m, turning left (positive $\omega$). This is a **classic KTU long-answer test pattern**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Ultrasonic Distance Measurement

Ultrasonic sensors (e.g., HC-SR04) emit a 40 kHz burst and listen for the echo. Distance is computed from time-of-flight.

**Step 1 — Speed of sound in air (at 20°C):**

$$c = 331 + 0.6 T \quad \text{(m/s, with } T \text{ in °C)}$$

**Step 2 — Round-trip time-of-flight equation:**

$$\Delta t = \frac{2d}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for distance:**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Numerical Example:** At $T = 20°C$, $c = 343$ m/s. If $\Delta t = 5.83$ ms (echo delay):

$$d = \frac{343 \times 5.83 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{2.000}{2} = 1.000 \text{ m}$$

> [!TIP]
> The factor of **2** in the denominator is the most-skipped step by KTU students. The pulse travels *to* the object *and back*.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of IMA Categories

| **Parameter** | **Smart Wheelchair** | **Intelligent Walker** | **Robotic Exoskeleton** | **BCI Wheelchair** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Power** | 2×24V DC motors | 2×12V DC | High-torque brushless | Same as SPW |
| **Sensors** | Sonar + IMU + Camera | Sonar + Force sensors | IMU + EMG + Load cell | EEG headset + sonar |
| **Control Latency** | 50–200 ms | 100–300 ms | 10–50 ms (real-time gait) | 1–3 s (BCI latency) |
| **Autonomy Level** | 2 – 4 (semi-auto) | 1 – 2 (assistive) | 2 – 3 (shared control) | 1 – 2 (user-driven) |
| **Cost (approx.)** | $5,000 – $30,000 | $1,500 – $8,000 | $50,000 – $100,000 | $15,000 – $40,000 |
| **Clinical Use** | Quadriplegia, MS | Elderly, Parkinson | SCI (T6 – T12) | ALS, Locked-in |
| **Key Algorithm** | SLAM + A* | VFF + Fall detection | ZMP-based gait | P300 speller + Bayes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Intelligent Wheelchair Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Intent Layer\nJoystick, Voice, BCI, Sip-and-Puff] --> B[High-Level Planner\nSLAM, A-Star Path Planning]
    B --> C[Mid-Level Controller\nObstacle Avoidance VFF, Wall Following]
    C --> D[Low-Level Controller\nPID Motor Speed, Encoder Feedback]
    D --> E[Actuator Layer\nBLDC Motors, Servo Steering]
    E --> F[Wheelchair Chassis\nDifferential Drive Base]
    F --> G[Physical Environment]
    G --> H[Sensor Array\nSonar, IR, LiDAR, IMU, Camera]
    H --> I[Sensor Fusion\nEKF, Occupancy Grid]
    I --> B
    H --> C
    I --> D
    J[Battery Management\n24-48V Li-ion BMS] --> E
    J --> K[Embedded Processor\nRaspberry Pi 4 / Jetson Nano / STM32]
    K --> B
    K --> C
    K --> D
    L[Safety Subsystem\nEmergency Stop Bumper, Tilt Sensor] --> D
    L --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows form a **closed loop**. User intent enters at the top, plans are generated, executed by motors, and the *same* sensors that observe the world also feed back into every level — this is the essence of intelligent control.

---

## 4.2 Hierarchical Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Tier 3\nDeliberative\nGoal: Reach Room 204] --> B[Tier 2\nReactive\nSub-goal: Turn left at next corridor]
    B --> C[Tier 1\nReflexive\nVelocity command to motors]
    C --> D[Environment]
    D --> E[Sensors]
    E --> C
    E --> B
    E --> A
```

---

## 4.3 Sensor Placement Schematic (Top-View of Wheelchair)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FRONT[Front Array]
        S1[Sonar F1\n0-4m 15 deg cone]
        S2[Sonar F2\n0-4m 15 deg cone]
        S3[IR F3\n0-0.8m narrow]
        CAM[RGB-D Camera\nup to 5m FoV 60 deg]
    end
    subgraph SIDE[Side Arrays]
        S4[Sonar L1]
        S5[Sonar R1]
    end
    subgraph REAR[Rear Array]
        S6[Sonar B1]
        S7[Sonar B2]
    end
    subgraph CENTER[Onboard Core]
        IMU[9-DOF IMU\n100 Hz]
        CPU[Processor]
        BAT[24V Li-ion]
    end
    FRONT --> CPU
    SIDE --> CPU
    REAR --> CPU
    IMU --> CPU
    BAT --> CPU
    CPU --> M[Left and Right Motor Drivers]
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Tree for Obstacle Response

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Sonar reads distance d] --> B{d less than 50 cm?}
    B -- No --> C[Maintain speed and heading]
    B -- Yes --> D{d less than 20 cm?}
    D -- No --> E[Reduce speed by 50 percent]
    E --> F{Side clear?}
    F -- Left --> G[Turn left 15 deg]
    F -- Right --> H[Turn right 15 deg]
    F -- Both --> I[Stop and reverse 30 cm]
    D -- Yes --> I
    I --> J[Replan path via Tier 3]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Drawing such **state machines** in the answer sheet for Part B (14-mark) questions earns **2–3 easy marks** even when the derivation is incomplete.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
Define an Intelligent Mobility Aid. List the three main layers of its control architecture.

**Model Answer:**
An Intelligent Mobility Aid (IMA) is a mechatronic device that combines a conventional mobility platform (wheelchair, walker, or exoskeleton) with sensors, processors, and actuators to provide semi-autonomous or autonomous navigation for users with mobility impairments.

The three control layers are:
1. **High-Level (Deliberative):** Path planning, SLAM, goal selection — loop time 0.5–2 s.
2. **Mid-Level (Reactive):** Obstacle avoidance, wall following — loop time 50–200 ms.
3. **Low-Level (Reflexive):** Motor speed/torque control with encoder feedback — loop time 1–10 ms.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for naming the three layers + 1 for their functions/loop times.)*

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
What is SLAM? Why is it important in an intelligent wheelchair?

**Model Answer:**
**SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)** is the computational process by which a robot builds a map of an unknown environment while simultaneously keeping track of its own location within that map.

**Importance in a smart wheelchair:**
- Enables **autonomous navigation** in unmapped indoor spaces (hospitals, homes).
- Allows the wheelchair to **return to a charging dock** or designated location.
- Supports **dynamic replanning** when obstacles (e.g., a person walking by) appear.
- Provides **context-aware speed adjustment** (e.g., slow down near doorways).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for localization + 1 for mapping, or any two valid applications.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose ONE)

### Question A (Option 1) — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a smart powered wheelchair. Explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the forward kinematics of a differential-drive wheelchair and compute the pose after $t = 4$ s given $r = 0.20$ m, $L = 0.30$ m, $\omega_L = 1.5$ rad/s, $\omega_R = 2.5$ rad/s, $\Delta t = 0.1$ s, and initial pose $(0, 0, 0)$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The architecture has **five functional blocks** (as shown in SECTION 4.1):

1. **User Interface (HMI):** Accepts user intent — joystick, voice, sip-and-puff, or BCI. Translates high-level commands (e.g., "go to kitchen") into discrete goals.
2. **Perception (Sensor Layer):** Ultrasonic/IR/LiDAR for distance, IMU for orientation, cameras for vision. Outputs raw measurements to fusion unit.
3. **Cognition (Processor + Algorithms):** Runs EKF/SLAM for localization, A* for path planning, VFF for obstacle avoidance. Generates velocity setpoints.
4. **Actuation (Motor Drivers + Wheels):** BLDC motors with gear reduction. Receives PWM commands. Encoders feed back wheel speeds.
5. **Safety Layer:** Emergency stop bumper, tilt sensor, current limiter. Has **highest priority** — overrides all other layers in fault conditions.

**Energy Source:** 24 V or 48 V Li-ion battery with BMS.

**Communication:** CAN bus or ROS (Robot Operating System) topics for inter-module messaging.

*(7 marks: 2 for block diagram + 1 each for 5 blocks' explanations.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute linear and angular velocities:**

$$v = \frac{0.20}{2}(1.5 + 2.5) = \frac{0.20 \times 4.0}{2} = 0.40 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\omega = \frac{0.20}{2 \times 0.30}(2.5 - 1.5) = \frac{0.20}{0.60}(1.0) = 0.333 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply closed-form solution (since $\omega$ is constant):**

$$x(t) = \frac{v}{\omega}[\sin(\omega t) - \sin(0)] = \frac{0.40}{0.333}\sin(0.333 \times 4)$$

$$x(4) = 1.20 \times \sin(1.333) = 1.20 \times 0.9711 = 1.165 \text{ m}$$

$$y(t) = \frac{v}{\omega}[\cos(0) - \cos(\omega t)] = 1.20 \times [1 - \cos(1.333)]$$

$$y(4) = 1.20 \times [1 - 0.2384] = 1.20 \times 0.7616 = 0.914 \text{ m}$$

$$\theta(4) = 0 + 0.333 \times 4 = 1.333 \text{ rad} \approx 76.4°$$

**Final pose at $t = 4$ s:**

$$(x, y, \theta) = (1.165 \text{ m}, \ 0.914 \text{ m}, \ 1.333 \text{ rad})$$

**Verification of radius of curvature:**

$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.40}{0.333} = 1.20 \text{ m}$$

The wheelchair moves along a circular arc of radius **1.20 m** turning left.

*(7 marks: 2 for kinematic derivation + 1 for substitution + 2 for $x, y$ values + 1 for $\theta$ + 1 for final pose/arc interpretation.)*

---

### Question B (Option 2) — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the Virtual Force Field (VFF) method for obstacle avoidance in a smart wheelchair. How are repulsive and attractive forces computed? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ultrasonic sensor on a smart wheelchair measures an echo time of $\Delta t = 8.75$ ms at 25°C. Calculate the distance to the obstacle. If the wheelchair moves at 0.5 m/s, what is the minimum detection range required for safe stopping, given a braking deceleration of $1.5$ m/s²? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **Virtual Force Field (VFF)** method, proposed by Borenstein and Koren (1989), treats the wheelchair as a particle moving under the influence of **virtual forces** in a 2D potential field.

**Components of the VFF method:**

1. **Occupancy Grid Map:** The environment is divided into cells (e.g., $10 \times 10$ cm). Each cell stores a certainty value $C_{i,j} \in [0, 1]$ representing obstacle probability.
2. **Repulsive Force from Obstacles:** Each occupied cell exerts a repulsive force inversely proportional to the square of the distance:

$$F_{rep,i} = \frac{K_{rep}}{(d_i + \epsilon)^2} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{r}}_i$$

where $d_i$ is the distance to cell $i$, $\epsilon$ is a small constant to avoid singularity at $d = 0$, and $\hat{\mathbf{r}}_i$ is the unit vector from the cell to the wheelchair.

3. **Attractive Force from Goal:** The target location pulls the wheelchair:

$$F_{att} = K_{att} \cdot d_g \cdot \hat{\mathbf{g}}$$

where $d_g$ is the distance to the goal, and $\hat{\mathbf{g}}$ is the unit vector toward the goal.

4. **Resultant Force and Motion:** The total force is:

$$F_{total} = F_{att} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} F_{rep,i}$$

The wheelchair adjusts its heading to align with $F_{total}$.

**Advantages:**
- Computationally lightweight (suitable for embedded processors).
- Naturally combines obstacle avoidance and goal seeking.
- Real-time reactive behavior.

**Limitations:**
- **Local minima problem** — the wheelchair may get trapped between two obstacles where $F_{total} \approx 0$.
- Requires careful tuning of $K_{rep}$ and $K_{att}$.

*(7 marks: 2 for concept + 2 for repulsive + 2 for attractive + 1 for limitations.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Part 1 — Compute distance using ultrasonic sensor:**

**Step 1 — Speed of sound at 25°C:**

$$c = 331 + 0.6 \times 25 = 331 + 15 = 346 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply time-of-flight equation:**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \frac{346 \times 8.75 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{3.0275}{2} = 1.514 \text{ m}$$

**Part 2 — Compute minimum safe stopping distance:**

**Step 1 — Use kinematics equation of motion (final velocity = 0):**

$$v_f^2 = v_i^2 - 2 a d_{stop}$$

$$0 = (0.5)^2 - 2 \times 1.5 \times d_{stop}$$

$$d_{stop} = \frac{0.25}{3.0} = 0.0833 \text{ m} \approx 8.33 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with detection range:**

- Detection range: **1.514 m**
- Required stopping distance: **0.083 m**
- **Safety margin:** $1.514 - 0.083 = 1.431$ m

**Conclusion:** The sensor detects the obstacle **1.431 m before** the wheelchair needs to begin braking. The system is **safe** with a large safety margin. For higher speeds (e.g., 1.5 m/s), the stopping distance would be $\frac{(1.5)^2}{2 \times 1.5} = 0.75$ m — still well within the 1.514 m range.

*(7 marks: 2 for $c$ calculation + 1 for $d$ calculation + 2 for stopping distance derivation + 1 for numerical evaluation + 1 for safety conclusion.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 IMA Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in ultrasonic distance ($d = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$, not $c \cdot \Delta t$). Cost: **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing $L$ and $2L$** in the differential-drive formula. The syllabus defines $L$ as **half** the wheel separation. Cost: **1 mark**.
> 3. **Skipping the Euler integration assumption** in pose-update derivations. Cost: **1 mark**.
> 4. **Omitting units** in final numerical answers. KTU examiners *do* deduct for unitless answers. Cost: **0.5 mark** per missing unit.
> 5. **Drawing block diagrams without arrows** showing the feedback direction. Cost: **1–2 marks** in 7-mark questions.
> 6. **Mixing up SLAM and GPS** — GPS does **not** work indoors; SLAM is the correct indoor localization method. Cost: **1 mark** in conceptual questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Intelligent Mobility Aids:**

- ✅ **IMA Definition** = Sensor + Processor + Actuator + Intelligence, on a mobility platform.
- ✅ **Three Control Tiers:** Deliberative (slow, smart) → Reactive (medium) → Reflexive (fast, safe).
- ✅ **Differential Drive Kinematics:** $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_L + \omega_R)$; $\omega = \frac{r}{2L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$.
- ✅ **Pose Update:** Euler integration with $\Delta t$ — must state the assumption.
- ✅ **Ultrasonic Distance:** $d = \frac{c \Delta t}{2}$; $c = 343$ m/s at 20°C, $c = 346$ m/s at 25°C.
- ✅ **Stopping Distance:** $d_{stop} = \frac{v^2}{2 a}$ — used in safety analysis.
- ✅ **VFF Method:** Repulsive (from obstacles) + Attractive (to goal) = Resultant force.
- ✅ **SLAM** = Simultaneous Localization and Mapping — works **indoors**, unlike GPS.
- ✅ **Sensor Suite:** Sonar (long range, wide cone), IR (short range, narrow), LiDAR (precise, expensive), IMU (orientation), Camera (semantic understanding).
- ✅ **Wheelchair Path:** Constant $\omega$ → circular arc; $R = v / \omega$.
- ✅ **BCI Wheelchairs** are slow (1–3 s latency) but enable mobility for locked-in patients.
- ✅ **Safety Override:** Emergency stop has the **highest priority** — overrides all planning.
- ✅ **Standard Clinical Metric:** Likert 1–5 autonomy scale (1 = none, 5 = full).
- ✅ **Wheelchair Battery:** 24 V / 48 V Li-ion, 200–600 Wh typical.
- ✅ **Track width $2L$** is the parameter used in KTU formulas — do not confuse with full wheelbase.

> **End of Module 4 — Intelligent Mobility Aids Notes**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme, PEBMT753 Rehabilitation Engineering*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT753 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Robotic Manipulation Aids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robotic Manipulation Aids** are externally powered, computer-controlled electromechanical devices designed to restore, substitute, or augment the upper-limb manipulative functions of individuals with physical disabilities. They are engineered to perform **Activities of Daily Living (ADLs)**, vocational tasks, and environmental interaction tasks that would otherwise be impossible or excessively fatiguing for the user.

In strict KTU terminology, these aids fall under the classification of **Assistive Robotic Devices (ARDs)** and are a sub-class of **Wheelchair-Mounted Robotic Arms (WMRAs)** when fixed to a powered mobility base, or **Standalone Desktop Manipulators** when placed on a workstation. They are governed by the principles of **Human-Robot Interaction (HRI)**, **cooperative control**, and **anthropomorphic kinematics**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (The "Mechanical Hand" Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a person who has lost the use of their arm, but their brain still knows *how* to reach for a glass of water, *where* the cup is, and *how much force* to apply to grip it without crushing it. A robotic manipulation aid is essentially a **prosthetic arm without the human body** — a mechanical "remote hand" that lives on a wheelchair or desk, and obeys the user's high-level intent (where to go, what to grab) while the motors and sensors take care of the low-level mechanics (lifting, gripping, avoiding collisions).

Think of it like a **crane at a shipping port** that is being controlled by a person on the ground using a small joystick. The crane does the heavy lifting, the human provides the intelligence. In a rehabilitation manipulator, the user provides the *intent* (via input modalities like joysticks, sip-and-puff, or head-tracking), and the robot provides the *effort* and *precision*.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Industrial & Rehabilitation Definitions)

> [!TIP]
> Memorize these standard metrics — they appear frequently in KTU 14-mark questions as "compare any two manipulators" or "list specifications" type sub-parts.

The following **bold** parameters define the performance envelope of any rehabilitation manipulator:

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** Typically **5 to 7 DOF** for upper-limb mimicry (3 for shoulder positioning, 1 for elbow, 1 for wrist rotation, 1 for gripper open/close).
- **Payload Capacity:** Usually **1.5 kg to 5 kg** for ADL tasks (a cup of water ≈ 250 g, a book ≈ 0.5 kg).
- **Reach (Workspace Radius):** Approximately **0.6 m to 1.2 m** from the mounting point.
- **Positional Repeatability:** **±0.5 mm to ±2 mm**, defined per **ISO 9283**.
- **End-Effector Speed:** Typically **0.1 m/s to 0.5 m/s** (slow, for safety).
- **Weight of the Arm:** **6 kg to 15 kg** (must not destabilize the wheelchair).

## 1.4 Classification of Robotic Manipulation Aids

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Classification Framework:**

| Class | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Wheelchair-Mounted Robotic Arm (WMRA)** | Mounted on powered wheelchair chassis | Manus ARM (Exact Dynamics), Raptor (Kinova) |
| **Desktop / Vocational Manipulator** | Fixed to a workstation for office tasks | Desktop Vocational Assistant Robot (DeVAR) |
| **Bedside / Mobile Manipulator** | Mobile base, used in clinical settings | HelpMate, Care-O-bot |
| **Body-Worn / Orthotic Manipulator** | Worn on the body as an exoskeleton | Wrist-mounted orthotic aids |
| **Surgical / Therapeutic Robot (adjacent)** | Re-training motor function | InMotion ARM (Bionik Labs) |

## 1.5 Historical Context (KTU Board Favourite)

> [!CAUTION]
> A **5-mark question** in KTU 2024 ESE was: *"Briefly trace the evolution of rehabilitation robotics."* Always remember the milestones below.

- **1963:** First documented case of a computer-assisted manipulator (Case Institute of Technology, Rancho Los Amigos).
- **1980s:** **Manus ARM** (developed in the Netherlands) — the first commercially viable wheelchair-mounted rehabilitation arm.
- **1990s:** **DeVAR** (Desktop Vocational Assistant Robot) by Stanford / Palo Alto VA — used at office desks.
- **2000s:** **Assistive Robotic Manipulator (ARM)** by the University of Pennsylvania.
- **2010s–Present:** Cloud-connected, AI-vision-guided arms like **JACO (Kinova)**, **iARM (Assistive Innovations)**, and **OB1** by Sastra Robotics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — The Five Subsystems

A rehabilitation manipulator is not just a mechanical arm. It is a **cyber-physical system** composed of five tightly integrated subsystems. Understanding this hierarchy is the foundation of every KTU essay on this topic.

1. **Mechanical Subsystem (Kinematic Chain):** The physical links, joints (revolute R or prismatic P), and the end-effector (gripper). For rehabilitation arms, the dominant architecture is an **anthropomorphic 6-DOF chain** (e.g., JACO arm: 6 revolute joints + 3-finger gripper = effectively 7 DOF).

2. **Actuation Subsystem:** **DC servo motors** or **brushless DC (BLDC) motors** coupled with **harmonic-drive gear reducers** to provide high torque at low speed. Torque at joint $i$ is computed as:
   $$\tau_i = J_i^T(\theta) \cdot F_{ext}$$
   where $J_i^T(\theta)$ is the **transpose of the Jacobian matrix** evaluated at the joint configuration $\theta$, and $F_{ext}$ is the external force at the end-effector.

3. **Sensing Subsystem:** Encoders (position), **strain gauges / force-torque sensors** (for force feedback), **tactile sensors** in the gripper, and **proximity sensors** (infrared / ultrasonic) for collision avoidance.

4. **Control Subsystem:** The microcontroller / DSP that executes trajectory planning. The dominant control law is the **Computed-Torque Controller**:
   $$\tau = M(\theta)\ddot{\theta} + C(\theta,\dot{\theta})\dot{\theta} + G(\theta) + J^T F_{ext}$$
   where $M(\theta)$ is the inertia matrix, $C(\theta,\dot{\theta})$ captures Coriolis/centrifugal terms, and $G(\theta)$ is the gravity vector.

5. **Human-Machine Interface (HMI) Subsystem:** The input modality — joystick, sip-and-puff, head-tracker, eye-tracker, or **single-switch scanning** for users with severe motor limitations.

## 2.2 Kinematic Foundations (Denavit-Hartenberg Convention)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) parameters** are the *universal language* of robot kinematics in KTU exams. You will be expected to set up a DH table for at least a 2-DOF or 3-DOF planar arm.

A general rigid link in 3D space is described by **four DH parameters**:
- $a_i$ (link length, in metres)
- $\alpha_i$ (link twist, in radians)
- $d_i$ (link offset, in metres)
- $\theta_i$ (joint angle, in radians)

The homogeneous transformation matrix from frame $i-1$ to frame $i$ is:

$$
^{i-1}T_i = \begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\cos\theta_i \\
\sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\sin\theta_i \\
0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The forward kinematics of an $n$-joint manipulator is the chained product:

$$
^{0}T_n = {^{0}T_1} \cdot {^{1}T_2} \cdot {^{2}T_3} \cdots {^{n-1}T_n}
$$

## 2.3 The Jacobian Matrix — Mapping Joint Space to Cartesian Space

The **Jacobian** $J(\theta)$ relates the end-effector linear/angular velocity $\dot{x}$ in Cartesian space to the joint velocities $\dot{\theta}$:

$$\dot{x} = J(\theta)\dot{\theta} \quad \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times n}$$

The joint torque required to produce a desired end-effector force $F$ is given by the principle of **virtual work**:

$$\tau = J(\theta)^T F$$

> [!WARNING]
> A **singular configuration** occurs when $\det(J(\theta)) = 0$. At singularities, the manipulator loses one or more DOFs and joint velocities can become unbounded. For rehabilitation arms, singularities must be **algorithmically avoided** to prevent jerky, unsafe motion that could harm the user.

## 2.4 Workspace Analysis

The **dexterous workspace** is the set of points reachable by the end-effector with all orientations possible. The **reachable workspace** is the set of points the end-effector can reach with at least one orientation.

For a 2-link planar arm with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$:
- **Inner radius:** $r_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$
- **Outer radius:** $r_{max} = L_1 + L_2$

The reachable area is an **annulus** of area $A = \pi(L_1^2 + L_2^2)$ (when $L_1 \neq L_2$, with a disk of area $\pi(L_1 - L_2)^2$ excluded).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> This is your **one-page cheat sheet** for the 14-mark derivations. Print it. Memorize the form, not just the symbols.

| S.No. | Equation | Meaning / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $^{0}T_n = \prod_{i=1}^{n} {^{i-1}T_i}$ | Forward Kinematics (Chained DH transforms) | dimensionless (4×4 matrix) |
| 2 | $\dot{x} = J(\theta)\dot{\theta}$ | Velocity Kinematics | m/s, rad/s |
| 3 | $\tau = J(\theta)^T F$ | Static Force-Torque relationship (virtual work) | N·m, N |
| 4 | $\det(J) = 0$ | Singularity condition (loss of DOF) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\tau = M(\theta)\ddot{\theta} + C(\theta,\dot{\theta})\dot{\theta} + G(\theta) + J^T F_{ext}$ | Computed-Torque Control Law | N·m |
| 6 | $r_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ | Inner workspace boundary | metres |
| 7 | $r_{max} = L_1 + L_2$ | Outer workspace boundary | metres |
| 8 | $A = \pi (L_1^2 - L_2^2)$ | Reachable workspace area (annulus) | m² |
| 9 | $V_{max} = J(\theta) \cdot \dot{\theta}_{max}$ | Maximum Cartesian speed at a configuration | m/s |
| 10 | $P_{motor} = \tau_{motor} \cdot \omega_{motor}$ | Mechanical power per joint motor | Watts |
| 11 | $F_{grip} = \mu \cdot F_{normal}$ | Coulomb friction grip model (gripper holding force) | Newtons |
| 12 | $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert q_i \vert$ | Manipulability Index (Yoshikawa's measure) | dimensionless |
| 13 | $T = \frac{1}{2}\dot{\theta}^T M(\theta) \dot{\theta}$ | Kinetic Energy of the manipulator | Joules |
| 14 | $W = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i g h_i$ | Gravitational Potential Energy | Joules |
| 15 | $\dot{x}_{des} = K_p (x_{des} - x) + K_d (\dot{x}_{des} - \dot{x})$ | PD-position control law in Cartesian space | m/s |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The mathematics above is not academic — it directly drives engineering decisions:
- **Rehabilitation arm design:** Use the Jacobian singularity analysis to plan joint limits that *avoid* singularities in the user's typical ADL reach zones (e.g., the mouth, the table surface).
- **Control firmware:** The computed-torque law is what allows a Manus arm to lift a 1.5 kg cup smoothly without jerking when the user toggles the joystick.
- **Safety certification:** ISO 13482 and IEC 80601-2-78 require singularity-safe trajectories and force-limited operation — the formulas in this table are the basis for compliance.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Forward Kinematics of a 2-DOF Planar Arm

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **high-yield derivation** — KTU has asked this in the December 2022 ESE (14-mark question, part a). Master it.

**Problem Statement:** Consider a 2-link planar manipulator moving in the $x$–$y$ plane. Link 1 has length $L_1$ and joint angle $\theta_1$ (measured from the $x$-axis). Link 2 has length $L_2$ and joint angle $\theta_2$ (measured relative to link 1). Derive the position $(x, y)$ of the end-effector.

**Step 1 — Set up the DH table for the 2-link planar arm:**

Since both joints are **revolute** and motion is planar, the DH parameters simplify dramatically:

| Link $i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_1$ | 0 | 0 | $\theta_1$ |
| 2 | $L_2$ | 0 | 0 | $\theta_2$ |

**Step 2 — Write $^{0}T_1$:**

Substituting into the general DH transformation:

$$
^{0}T_1 = \begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_1 & -\sin\theta_1 & 0 & L_1\cos\theta_1 \\
\sin\theta_1 & \cos\theta_1 & 0 & L_1\sin\theta_1 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Write $^{1}T_2$** (with joint variable $\theta_1 + \theta_2$ in the second joint's frame, since $\theta_2$ is measured relative to link 1):

$$
^{1}T_2 = \begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 & 0 & L_2\cos\theta_2 \\
\sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_2 & 0 & L_2\sin\theta_2 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply the two matrices to obtain $^{0}T_2$:**

$$
^{0}T_2 = {^{0}T_1} \cdot {^{1}T_2}
$$

Computing element-by-element. The rotation part:
$$
R = \begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2 - \sin\theta_1\sin\theta_2 & -(\cos\theta_1\sin\theta_2 + \sin\theta_1\cos\theta_2) & 0 \\
\sin\theta_1\cos\theta_2 + \cos\theta_1\sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2 - \sin\theta_1\sin\theta_2 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Apply the trigonometric identities:
- $\cos(A+B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$
- $\sin(A+B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$

The translation part (end-effector position):

$$
x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

$$
y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

**Final Result — End-Effector Position Equations:**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
x(\theta_1, \theta_2) &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
y(\theta_1, \theta_2) &= L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\end{aligned}
}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Jacobian of the 2-DOF Planar Arm

> [!NOTE]
> The Jacobian is required for velocity mapping and singularity analysis. KTU asks this in **7-mark sub-parts** frequently.

**Step 1 — Differentiate the position equations from §3.1 with respect to time:**

$$
\dot{x} = -L_1 \sin\theta_1 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1 - L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)
$$

$$
\dot{y} = L_1 \cos\theta_1 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)
$$

**Step 2 — Factor out $\dot{\theta}_1$ and $\dot{\theta}_2$ into a matrix form:**

$$
\dot{x} = [-L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)] \dot{\theta}_1 + [-L_2\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)] \dot{\theta}_2
$$

$$
\dot{y} = [L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)] \dot{\theta}_1 + [L_2\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)] \dot{\theta}_2
$$

**Step 3 — Write in matrix form $\dot{x} = J\dot{\theta}$:**

$$
\boxed{
J(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix}
-L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\
L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
\end{bmatrix}
}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the determinant for singularity analysis:**

$$
\det(J) = [-L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)] \cdot L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) - [-L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)] \cdot [L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)]
$$

Expanding and simplifying using the identity $\sin(A - B) = \sin A \cos B - \cos A \sin B$:

$$
\det(J) = L_1 L_2 \sin\theta_2
$$

**Singularity Condition:** $\det(J) = 0 \Rightarrow \sin\theta_2 = 0 \Rightarrow \theta_2 = 0$ or $\theta_2 = \pi$.

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- When $\theta_2 = 0$, the arm is **fully stretched out** (folded along a single line) — the end-effector can only move along a single tangent direction.
- When $\theta_2 = \pi$, the arm is **folded back on itself** — the end-effector is stationary at the elbow.

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: Workspace Boundary of a 2-DOF Arm

> [!NOTE]
> Workspace analysis is frequently asked as a 7-mark problem in Part B of KTU ESE.

**Problem:** Find the reachable workspace of a 2-link planar arm with $L_1 = 0.5$ m and $L_2 = 0.4$ m.

**Step 1 — Apply the formulas:**

$$
r_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert = \vert 0.5 - 0.4 \vert = 0.1 \text{ m}
$$

$$
r_{max} = L_1 + L_2 = 0.5 + 0.4 = 0.9 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the area of the reachable annulus:**

$$
A = \pi (r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2) = \pi (0.9^2 - 0.1^2) = \pi (0.81 - 0.01) = 0.80\pi \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{A \approx 2.513 \text{ m}^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with rehabilitation context:**

For a Manus-arm-like device, this means a user seated at a wheelchair can reach:
- **Closest point:** 10 cm from the shoulder mount (e.g., touch the wheelchair's own armrest).
- **Farthest point:** 90 cm away (e.g., reach a shelf, a table, or a door handle).
- **Total area covered:** ~2.5 m² in front of the user — sufficient to cover an office desk and a kitchen counter from a stationary wheelchair position.

## 3.4 Worked Derivation 4: Joint Torque from End-Effector Force

> [!NOTE]
> This is a direct application of the virtual-work principle, commonly tested as a 3-mark short problem.

**Problem:** A 2-DOF planar arm is at configuration $\theta_1 = 30°$, $\theta_2 = 45°$. The end-effector is commanded to apply a force $F = [F_x, F_y]^T = [5, 0]^T$ N. Compute the required joint torques. Take $L_1 = 0.4$ m, $L_2 = 0.3$ m.

**Step 1 — Substitute the angles (in radians) into the Jacobian:**

$$
\theta_1 = \frac{\pi}{6} \approx 0.5236, \quad \theta_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.7854
$$

$$
\theta_1 + \theta_2 \approx 1.309 \text{ rad} \approx 75°
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the Jacobian numerically:**

$$
J = \begin{bmatrix}
-0.4\sin(30°) - 0.3\sin(75°) & -0.3\sin(75°) \\
0.4\cos(30°) + 0.3\cos(75°) & 0.3\cos(75°)
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Computing each entry:

- $\sin 30° = 0.5$, $\sin 75° \approx 0.9659$
- $\cos 30° \approx 0.8660$, $\cos 75° \approx 0.2588$

$$
J = \begin{bmatrix}
-0.4(0.5) - 0.3(0.9659) & -0.3(0.9659) \\
0.4(0.8660) + 0.3(0.2588) & 0.3(0.2588)
\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}
-0.4898 & -0.2898 \\
0.4240 & 0.0776
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $J^T$:**

$$
J^T = \begin{bmatrix}
-0.4898 & 0.4240 \\
-0.2898 & 0.0776
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply $\tau = J^T F$ with $F = [5, 0]^T$:**

$$
\tau_1 = (-0.4898)(5) + (0.4240)(0) = -2.449 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
\tau_2 = (-0.2898)(5) + (0.0776)(0) = -1.449 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\tau_1 \approx -2.45 \text{ N·m}, \quad \tau_2 \approx -1.45 \text{ N·m}}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** The negative sign indicates that the joint motors must apply a **counter-torque** to balance the force, and the elbow motor needs less torque than the shoulder motor — consistent with the fact that the force is being applied far from the elbow and close to the shoulder, creating a larger moment arm at the shoulder.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram — Rehabilitation Robotic Manipulator

The diagram below captures the **complete signal and energy flow** of a wheelchair-mounted rehabilitation robotic arm, from the user's intent to the gripper's action. This is a standard 14-mark "draw and explain" question in KTU ESE.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User with Disability] --> B[Input Modality Layer]
    B --> B1[Joystick]
    B --> B2[Sip-and-Puff]
    B --> B3[Head Tracker IMU]
    B --> B4[EOG / Eye Tracker]
    B --> B5[Single Switch Scanner]
    B1 --> C[High-Level Command Processor]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C
    C --> D[Trajectory Planner]
    D --> E[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    E --> F[Joint Space Controller]
    F --> G[Motor Driver Array]
    G --> H1[Joint 1 Actuator]
    G --> H2[Joint 2 Actuator]
    G --> H3[Joint 3 Actuator]
    G --> H4[Joint 4 Actuator]
    G --> H5[Joint 5 Actuator]
    G --> H6[Joint 6 Actuator]
    H1 --> I[Kinematic Chain and End Effector Gripper]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    H5 --> I
    H6 --> I
    I --> J[Task Environment Object]
    I --> K[Force Torque Sensor]
    K --> L[Safety Supervisor]
    I --> M[Proximity and Tactile Sensors]
    M --> L
    L --> C
    L --> F
    F --> N[Power Supply Battery Pack]
    N --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The signal flow is *bidirectional* — commands flow downward (user → motor → gripper), while sensor feedback flows upward (force, position, proximity → controller → planner). The **Safety Supervisor** is a *hard-real-time watchdog* that can halt all motion within 50 ms if a collision is imminent.

## 4.2 Hierarchical Control Architecture (Three-Layer Model)

The control software of any modern rehabilitation manipulator is organised into three nested layers. This diagram is a KTU favourite for "describe the control architecture" sub-parts.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1 High Level Decision Making] --> L2[Layer 2 Mid Level Task Execution]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Low Level Servo Control]
    L1 --> L1A[Task Planner Object Selection]
    L1 --> L1B[Mode Manager Autonomous vs Shared]
    L1 --> L1C[User Intent Interpreter HMI]
    L2 --> L2A[Trajectory Generator]
    L2 --> L2B[Inverse Kinematics Engine]
    L2 --> L2C[Obstacle Avoidance Module]
    L3 --> L3A[Joint Servo Loops PD or PID]
    L3 --> L3B[Motor Driver PWM]
    L3 --> L3C[Encoder Feedback Decoders]
    L2D[Sensor Fusion IMU Force Vision] --> L2
    L3D[Safety Watchdog E Stop] --> L3
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Input Modality Selection

The most critical design decision in a rehabilitation manipulator is the **input modality** — how the user communicates intent. Below is a decision-flow architecture for matching user capability to input device.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    U[User Functional Assessment] --> Q1{Can user move upper limb voluntarily?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1[Use Hand Held Joystick]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Can user move head voluntarily?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> A2[Use Head Tracker IMU based]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Can user sip and puff reliably?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> A3[Use Sip and Puff Pneumatic Switch]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Can user control eye gaze?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> A4[Use EOG or Eye Tracker]
    Q4 -- No --> A5[Use Single Switch Scanning with binary click]
    A1 --> O[Selected Input Modality]
    A2 --> O
    A3 --> O
    A4 --> O
    A5 --> O
```

## 4.4 Component-Level Mechanical Architecture (Kinematic Chain)

The mechanical design of a Manus-class 6-DOF rehabilitation arm is an **anthropomorphic chain** that mimics the human shoulder–elbow–wrist anatomy. The diagram below shows the **DH frame assignment** for a typical 6-DOF arm.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B[Base Mount on Wheelchair] --> J1[Revolute Joint 1 Shoulder Yaw Axis Z0]
    J1 --> L1[Link 1 Upper Arm]
    L1 --> J2[Revolute Joint 2 Shoulder Pitch Axis Z1]
    J2 --> L2[Link 2 Elbow Boom]
    L2 --> J3[Revolute Joint 3 Elbow Pitch Axis Z2]
    J3 --> L3[Link 3 Forearm]
    L3 --> J4[Revolute Joint 4 Wrist Roll Axis Z3]
    J4 --> L4[Link 4 Wrist Housing]
    L4 --> J5[Revolute Joint 5 Wrist Pitch Axis Z4]
    J5 --> L5[Link 5 Wrist Bracket]
    L5 --> J6[Revolute Joint 6 Wrist Yaw Axis Z5]
    J6 --> E[Three Finger Gripper End Effector]
    L1 --- A1[DH Frame 1 Origin at Joint 1]
    L2 --- A2[DH Frame 2 Origin at Joint 2]
    L3 --- A3[DH Frame 3 Origin at Joint 3]
    L4 --- A4[DH Frame 4 Origin at Joint 4]
    L5 --- A5[DH Frame 5 Origin at Joint 5]
    E --- A6[DH Frame 6 at Gripper Tool Center Point]
```

> [!TIP]
> In a 14-mark answer, when you draw this diagram, **always label the joint axes** ($Z_0$ to $Z_5$) and explicitly mark the **Tool Center Point (TCP)** at the gripper fingertips. KTU examiners award an extra 1–2 marks for correctly identifying the TCP.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q1.** Define a *Robotic Manipulation Aid* as used in rehabilitation engineering. List **any four** functional specifications used to characterise such a device.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **robotic manipulation aid** is a computer-controlled, externally powered electromechanical device designed to restore or augment the upper-limb manipulative capability of persons with physical disabilities, enabling them to perform **Activities of Daily Living (ADLs)** and vocational tasks.

**Four functional specifications** (1/2 mark each, total 2 marks):
1. **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** Typically 5–7 anthropomorphic DOF.
2. **Payload Capacity:** 1.5–5 kg for ADL objects.
3. **Reach (Workspace Radius):** 0.6–1.2 m from mount.
4. **End-Effector Speed:** 0.1–0.5 m/s for safety.
5. **Positional Repeatability:** ±0.5–2 mm (per ISO 9283).
6. **Input Modality:** Joystick, sip-and-puff, head-tracker, etc.

*(Any four specifications carry 2 marks; the definition carries 1 mark.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q2.** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the role of a **Wheelchair-Mounted Robotic Arm (WMRA)** in rehabilitation.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Wheelchair-Mounted Robotic Arm (WMRA)** is a robotic manipulator rigidly fixed to the chassis or armrest of a powered wheelchair. It functions as a **prosthetic extension of the user's reach**, performing manipulative tasks in the workspace around the wheelchair.

**Key roles** (1 mark each):
- **ADL Assistance:** Enables eating, drinking, opening doors, picking dropped objects — tasks impossible without upper-limb function.
- **Mobility + Manipulation Co-location:** Combines mobility and manipulation in a single integrated system, eliminating the need to transfer to a workstation.
- **User Independence & Dignity:** Reduces reliance on a caregiver, enhancing quality of life and self-esteem.
- **Vocational Use:** Allows office work (paper handling, phone answering) and light industrial work.

**Examples:** Manus ARM (Exact Dynamics), Raptor (Kinova), JACO (Kinova).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice 1]`

> **Q (A).** (a) With the aid of a labelled block diagram, describe the **five major subsystems** of a rehabilitation robotic manipulator. (7 marks)
>
> (b) A **2-DOF planar manipulator** has link lengths $L_1 = 0.5$ m and $L_2 = 0.4$ m. Set up the **Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) parameter table** and derive the **forward kinematics equations** for the end-effector position $(x, y)$. (7 marks)

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

The five major subsystems of a rehabilitation robotic manipulator are:

1. **Mechanical Subsystem — 1 mark**
   The kinematic chain consisting of links, revolute (R) and prismatic (P) joints, and the end-effector (gripper). For upper-limb mimicry, an **anthropomorphic 6-DOF chain** is used (3 shoulder/elbow DOFs + 3 wrist DOFs + 1 gripper DOF = 7 effective DOF).

2. **Actuation Subsystem — 1 mark**
   **DC servo motors** or **brushless DC (BLDC) motors** with **harmonic-drive** or **planetary gearheads** providing high torque at low speed. Each joint has its own motor-encoder assembly.

3. **Sensing Subsystem — 1 mark**
   - **Position:** Optical/magnetic encoders at each joint.
   - **Force:** Strain-gauge-based force-torque sensor at the wrist.
   - **Tactile:** Pressure-sensor array in the gripper fingers.
   - **Proximity:** IR / ultrasonic sensors for collision avoidance.

4. **Control Subsystem — 2 marks**
   The microcontroller / DSP executes **trajectory planning** and **real-time control**. The dominant control law is the **Computed-Torque Controller**:
   $$\tau = M(\theta)\ddot{\theta} + C(\theta,\dot{\theta})\dot{\theta} + G(\theta) + J^T F_{ext}$$
   It computes the torque at each joint by inverting the manipulator dynamics, achieving high tracking accuracy.

5. **Human-Machine Interface (HMI) Subsystem — 2 marks**
   Converts the user's intent into robot commands. Modalities include:
   - **Joystick** (for users with residual hand function),
   - **Sip-and-puff** (pneumatic switch for users with high spinal-cord injury),
   - **Head-tracker** (IMU-based, for users with limited upper-limb function),
   - **Eye-tracker / EOG** (for users with locked-in syndrome).
   The HMI must be **adaptive** to the user's residual capability.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Set up the DH parameter table (2 marks):**

For a 2-DOF planar arm (motion in $x$–$y$ plane, both joints revolute, no offsets or twists):

| Link $i$ | $a_i$ (m) | $\alpha_i$ (rad) | $d_i$ (m) | $\theta_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.5 | 0 | 0 | $\theta_1$ |
| 2 | 0.4 | 0 | 0 | $\theta_2$ |

**Step 2 — Write the individual DH transformation matrices (2 marks):**

$$
^{0}T_1 = \begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_1 & -\sin\theta_1 & 0 & 0.5\cos\theta_1 \\
\sin\theta_1 & \cos\theta_1 & 0 & 0.5\sin\theta_1 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
^{1}T_2 = \begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 & 0 & 0.4\cos\theta_2 \\
\sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_2 & 0 & 0.4\sin\theta_2 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply $^{0}T_1 \cdot {^{1}T_2}$ to obtain $^{0}T_2$ (2 marks):**

Using the chained product $^{0}T_2 = {^{0}T_1} \cdot {^{1}T_2}$ and extracting the translation column:

**Step 4 — Final forward kinematics equations (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
x &= 0.5\cos\theta_1 + 0.4\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
y &= 0.5\sin\theta_1 + 0.4\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\end{aligned}
}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 Choice 2]`

> **Q (B).** (a) Explain the concept of a **Jacobian matrix** for a robotic manipulator. Derive the Jacobian for the 2-DOF planar arm whose forward kinematics are given by $x = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ and $y = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$. State the **singularity condition** for this arm. (7 marks)
>
> (b) Compare the **Manus ARM (Wheelchair-Mounted)** and the **DeVAR (Desktop Vocational Assistant Robot)** under the following heads: (i) Mounting and mobility; (ii) Typical user population; (iii) DOF and payload; (iv) Input modality; (v) Two key limitations. (7 marks)

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Concept of the Jacobian — 2 marks:**

The **Jacobian matrix** $J(\theta)$ of a robotic manipulator is a multi-variable function that maps joint-space velocities ($\dot{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$) to Cartesian-space velocities of the end-effector ($\dot{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$):

$$\dot{x} = J(\theta) \dot{\theta}, \quad J_{ij} = \frac{\partial x_i}{\partial \theta_j}$$

It encodes the **instantaneous kinematics** of the manipulator at the current configuration. The transpose of the Jacobian, $J(\theta)^T$, maps an end-effector force to the equivalent joint torques (virtual work principle):

$$\tau = J(\theta)^T F$$

The Jacobian is also used in **singularity analysis**, **velocity limit checking**, and **static force analysis**.

**Step 1 — Differentiate the position equations with respect to time (2 marks):**

Differentiating $x$ and $y$ with respect to $t$ using the chain rule:

$$
\dot{x} = -L_1\sin\theta_1 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)
$$

$$
\dot{y} = L_1\cos\theta_1 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)
$$

**Step 2 — Factor out $\dot{\theta}_1$ and $\dot{\theta}_2$ (1 mark):**

$$
\dot{x} = [-L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)]\dot{\theta}_1 + [-L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)]\dot{\theta}_2
$$

$$
\dot{y} = [L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)]\dot{\theta}_1 + [L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)]\dot{\theta}_2
$$

**Step 3 — Express in matrix form (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{
J(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix}
-L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\
L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
\end{bmatrix}
}
$$

**Step 4 — Derive singularity condition (1 mark):**

$$
\det(J) = L_1 L_2 \sin\theta_2
$$

Setting $\det(J) = 0$:

$$
\boxed{\sin\theta_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_2 = 0 \text{ or } \theta_2 = \pi}
$$

At $\theta_2 = 0$, the arm is fully extended; at $\theta_2 = \pi$, it is fully folded. In both cases, one DOF is lost.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Comparative Table — 1 mark per row, 2 marks for limitations:**

| Parameter | Manus ARM (Wheelchair-Mounted) | DeVAR (Desktop Vocational Assistant) |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Mounting and mobility** | Mounted on powered wheelchair; mobile with user | Stationary, fixed to an office desk or workstation |
| **(ii) Typical user population** | Wheelchair users (e.g., high-level SCI, muscular dystrophy, ALS) | Ambulatory users with severe upper-limb impairment working in office jobs |
| **(iii) DOF and payload** | 6–7 DOF; 1.5 kg payload | 4–6 DOF; 2–3 kg payload (office items) |
| **(iv) Input modality** | Joystick, sip-and-puff, head-tracker, switch | Keyboard, dedicated single-button switch, voice |

**Two key limitations — 2 marks (1 mark each):**

- **Manus ARM:** High cost (>$30k) and reduced workspace when the user is stationary; requires significant training; no autonomy (purely user-controlled).
- **DeVAR:** Not mobile (tethered to desk); cannot assist with non-office ADL tasks like eating or personal hygiene; limited reach outside desk surface.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "degrees of freedom" with "axes".** A Manus arm with 6 motors and a 3-finger gripper has **7 effective DOF** (because the gripper contributes 1 DOF for open/close). Many students write "6 DOF" and lose a mark.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the Jacobian transpose convention.** The mapping $\tau = J^T F$ is from **Cartesian force to joint torque**. Writing $J F$ (without transpose) is a **fatal sign error** that loses 2 marks in any derivation.
> 
> 3. **Skipping the singularity discussion.** When you derive the Jacobian for any arm, **always state** the singularity condition $\det(J) = 0$ explicitly. Examiners give 1–2 marks *just* for this interpretation.
> 
> 4. **In the comparative question**, students often write "Manus is better" or "DeVAR is better" — this is **subjective and loses marks**. Always present **objective criteria** (DOF, payload, cost, mobility) and then justify your conclusion.
> 
> 5. **In the DH table**, students frequently write $\theta_1$ in the $d_i$ column or vice versa. **DH convention is strict**: $a_i$ = link length, $\alpha_i$ = link twist, $d_i$ = link offset, $\theta_i$ = joint variable. Memorize this order.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These 15 Points Before Walking into the Exam Hall:**

- [ ] A **robotic manipulation aid** is an externally powered, computer-controlled device that restores upper-limb function for ADLs and vocational tasks.
- [ ] The **five major subsystems** are: Mechanical, Actuation, Sensing, Control, and HMI.
- [ ] Standard rehabilitation manipulators have **5–7 DOF**, with **6 DOF** being most common (Manus, JACO).
- [ ] **Payload capacity** for ADL manipulators is typically **1.5–5 kg**.
- [ ] **Reach (workspace radius)** is **0.6–1.2 m** from the mounting point.
- [ ] **DH parameters** are four: $a_i$, $\alpha_i$, $d_i$, $\theta_i$ — in that order.
- [ ] **Forward kinematics** uses the chained product: $^{0}T_n = \prod_{i=1}^{n} {^{i-1}T_i}$.
- [ ] **Jacobian** maps joint velocities to Cartesian velocities: $\dot{x} = J\dot{\theta}$.
- [ ] **Joint torque** is mapped from end-effector force by: $\tau = J^T F$.
- [ ] **Singularity** occurs when $\det(J) = 0$ — the manipulator loses DOF and joint velocities can become unbounded.
- [ ] For a 2-DOF planar arm, $\det(J) = L_1 L_2 \sin\theta_2$; singularities at $\theta_2 = 0, \pi$.
- [ ] **Computed-torque control law:** $\tau = M\ddot{\theta} + C\dot{\theta} + G + J^T F_{ext}$.
- [ ] **Manus ARM** = wheelchair-mounted, 6 DOF, 1.5 kg payload, joystick/sip-and-puff input.
- [ ] **DeVAR** = desktop, office-based, switch input, for vocational use.
- [ ] **Input modalities** must match user capability: joystick (hand), head-tracker (neck), sip-and-puff (breath), eye-tracker (gaze), single-switch (binary).

> [!TIP]
> **Pro Tip for Toppers:** In any 14-mark KTU answer, structure your response as **(1) Definition, (2) Labelled diagram, (3) Step-by-step derivation or comparative table, (4) Engineering significance, (5) Limitations**. This 5-part structure is what separates a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Therapeutic Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT753 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Therapeutic Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 4):** Therapeutic Robots form the applied-mechatronics core of modern neurorehabilitation. They are programmable, sensor-integrated mechatronic devices designed to deliver, quantify, and adapt physical therapy interventions for patients with motor impairments arising from stroke, spinal cord injury (SCI), traumatic brain injury (TBI), cerebral palsy, and musculoskeletal disorders.

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Therapeutic Robot** is a mechatronic device — comprising actuators, sensors, a real-time controller, and a software-driven therapeutic protocol — that physically interacts with a patient's limb or body segment to deliver controlled, repetitive, task-specific motor training. Unlike surgical or assistive robots, the goal of a therapeutic robot is **not** permanent functional replacement, but rather the **promotion of neuroplastic recovery** by delivering high-intensity, quantifiable, and patient-adaptive movement therapy.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, therapeutic robots are classified as **Class IIa/IIb Active Medical Devices** (per IEC 60601-1) that must operate under the supervision of a licensed physiotherapist or rehabilitation physician.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction to Remember (Frequently Asked in KTU Exams):**
> - **Therapeutic Robot** → Aims at *recovery* (temporary use, training-oriented).
> - **Assistive Robot** → Aims at *compensation* (permanent use, task-replacement).
> - **Rehabilitation Exoskeleton** → A *wearable subset* of therapeutic/assistive robots that augments or restores limb motion through rigid external structure.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> **The Tireless Physical Therapist**
>
> Imagine a human physiotherapist who:
> - Never gets fatigued, even after 1,000 repetitions.
> - Remembers the exact joint angle, velocity, and force of every single movement.
> - Adjusts resistance in real time based on how much effort *you* put in.
> - Generates a printed progress report at the end of every session.
>
