> That is exactly what a therapeutic robot does — it is a **quantifiable, tireless, adaptive therapy partner** that delivers dose-controlled movement practice, which is the single most evidence-backed driver of cortical re-mapping after neurological injury.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Therapeutic Robotics (Bolded for Memorization)

| Parameter | Typical Range | Clinical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Workspace Volume** | **0.05 – 0.25 m³** | Must match adult human limb reachable space |
| **Peak End-Effector Force** | **20 – 80 N** | Matches therapist-applied guidance force |
| **Position Resolution** | **≤ 0.5 mm** | Required for sub-millimetric neural feedback |
| **Bandwidth of Force Control** | **≥ 10 Hz** | Critical for realistic haptic rendering |
| **Backdrivability** | **Low inertia (< 2 kg)** | Patient safety — robot must yield instantly |
| **Session Dosage** | **600–1000 reps/day** | Drives Hebbian plasticity (Nudo et al.) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "THE Q-BAG":** A good therapeutic robot is **Q**uantifiable, **B**ackdrivable, **A**daptive, and **G**entle. If any one is missing, it fails clinical translation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Therapeutic Robots (Module 4 Pillar)

Therapeutic robots can be categorized along three orthogonal axes — *anatomical*, *mechanical*, and *control-philosophical*.

#### A. Anatomical Classification (Most Frequently Tested)

1. **Upper-Limb Therapeutic Robots**
   - **End-effector type:** InMotion ARM (MIT-Manus), ARMEO Power, Burt (Barrett Upper-extremity Robotic Trainer).
   - **Exoskeleton type:** ARMEO Spring/Power, T-WREX, ABLE (Assistive Biofeedback Limbs Exerciser).
2. **Lower-Limb Therapeutic Robots**
   - **Gait Trainers:** Lokomat (Hocoma), LokoHelp, ALEX (Active Leg Exoskeleton).
   - **Treadmill-based end-effector:** Gait Trainer GT I/II, ReoAmbulator.
   - **Overground exoskeletons:** Ekso GT, ReWalk, Indego (used therapeutically in clinics).
3. **Ankle & Hand Robots**
   - Anklebot, Rutgers Ankle, Haptic Knob, Hand-of-Hope.
4. **Whole-Body / Multi-joint**
   - BALCOR (Balance Control Robot), String-Man.

#### B. Mechanical Architecture Classification

| Architecture | Description | Example | KTU Buzzword |
|---|---|---|---|
| **End-effector** | Robot attached only to distal segment (hand/foot) | MIT-Manus | "Distal attachment" |
| **Exoskeleton** | Rigid links aligned with human skeletal segments | ARMEO Power, Lokomat | "Skeletal alignment" |
| **Cable-driven** | Bowden-cable actuation for low inertia | String-Man, CAPE | "Remote actuation" |
| **Series-elastic / Variable-impedance** | Springs in series with motor | ANYexo, ARTHuR | "Intrinsic compliance" |

#### C. Control Philosophy Classification (High-Yield)

- **Position Control** — robot dictates trajectory; used in passive mobilization (PROM).
- **Force / Impedance Control** — robot controls mechanical impedance $\mathbf{Z}(s)$.
- **Assist-As-Needed (AAN)** — robot provides force *only* when patient deviates from a desired path.
- **Challenge-Point Control** — robot increases difficulty as patient improves (Guadagnoli framework).
- **EMG-triggered Control** — robot activates only on detection of volitional EMG.

### 2.2 The Impedance Control Framework (The Heart of Therapeutic Robotics)

Hogan (1985) impedance control is the foundational paradigm. The robot behaves as a programmable mass-spring-damper between itself and the patient:

$$F_{robot}(t) = M_d(\ddot{x}_d - \ddot{x}) + B_d(\dot{x}_d - \dot{x}) + K_d(x_d - x)$$

Where:
- $F_{robot}$ = force exerted by robot on patient limb
- $M_d, B_d, K_d$ = desired virtual **mass, damping, and stiffness** (the "impedance parameters")
- $x_d, \dot{x}_d, \ddot{x}_d$ = desired trajectory (position, velocity, acceleration)
- $x, \dot{x}, \ddot{x}$ = actual measured state of patient's limb

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in therapy:** By tuning $M_d, B_d, K_d$, the therapist can dial the robot from "totally guiding" (high stiffness $K_d$) to "totally resistive" (negative damping $B_d < 0$, used in strength training) — all on the same hardware.

### 2.3 Assist-As-Needed (AAN) Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

1. Define a **virtual tunnel** around the desired trajectory $x_d(t)$ of radius $r_{tunnel}$.
2. Measure actual patient position $x(t)$.
3. Compute lateral deviation $e(t) = \lVert x(t) - x_d(t) \rVert$.
4. **If** $e(t) \leq r_{tunnel}$: apply **zero** guidance force (patient is on-track → let them work).
5. **If** $e(t) > r_{tunnel}$: apply restoring force $F_{AAN} = K_{AAN} \cdot (e(t) - r_{tunnel})$ directed back toward the path.
6. Update $K_{AAN}$ over sessions based on success rate (machine-learning-driven personalization).

This single algorithm is responsible for the "magic" of modern robots like the Lokomat and Armeo — it prevents **slacking** (the patient letting the robot do all the work), which is critical for genuine cortical re-mapping.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Robot Impedance | $F = M_d \ddot{e} + B_d \dot{e} + K_d e$ | Force vs. deviation behavior | $N$ |
| Admittance (inverse) | $x = Y(s) \cdot F$, $Y(s) = \dfrac{1}{M_d s^2 + B_d s + K_d}$ | Position response to force | $m/N$ |
| AAN guidance force | $F_{AAN} = K_{AAN}(e - r_{tunnel})$ for $e > r_{tunnel}$ | Force only when needed | $N$ |
| Forward Kinematics (n-DOF) | $x = f(q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n)$ | Joints $\to$ Cartesian | $m$ |
| Jacobian transpose | $\tau = J^T F$ | Cartesian force $\to$ joint torque | $Nm$ |
| Series-elastic stiffness | $k_{SEA} = \dfrac{\tau_{motor}}{\Delta\theta_{spring}}$ | Physical compliance element | $Nm/rad$ |
| Repetition dose | $D = N_{reps} \times T_{session}$ | Plasticity-driving dosage | reps · min |
| EMG threshold | $EMG_{RMS} > \mu_{rest} + 3\sigma_{rest}$ | Volitional intent trigger | $mV$ |
| Backdrive friction limit | $F_{bd} \leq 5\% \; F_{max}$ | Safety metric | $N$ |
| Workspace sphere | $V = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r_{reach}^3$ | Anatomical fit check | $m^3$ |

> **Important KTU Constraint:** In the markdown table above, every absolute-value-style notation uses `\vert` or `\mid` — never the vertical bar `|` character — to keep the table parser intact.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Therapeutic robots have moved from research labs (MIT, ETH Zurich, University of Twente) into over **3,500 clinical installations worldwide** (Hocoma alone reports 800+ Lokomats). The economic driver is the **dose-response curve of neuroplasticity** — human therapy typically delivers 30–50 reps/session, while robot-aided therapy delivers 600–1,000 reps/session, accelerating the time to functional recovery by 30–40% in sub-acute stroke (VEJOVET trial, 2020).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the 1-DOF Impedance Control Law

We start from Newton's second law applied to the patient-robot coupled system along a single Cartesian axis $x$. The patient applies an active neuromuscular force $F_{patient}$, the robot applies $F_{robot}$, and the limb is modeled as a second-order mechanical system with intrinsic impedance $M_p, B_p, K_p$.

**Step 1 — Write the coupled dynamic equation:**

$$M_p \ddot{x} + B_p \dot{x} + K_p x = F_{patient} + F_{robot}$$

**Step 2 — Define the tracking error:**

$$e(t) = x_d(t) - x(t)$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate the error twice to relate accelerations:**

$$\ddot{x} = \ddot{x}_d - \ddot{e}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the coupled equation:**

$$M_p (\ddot{x}_d - \ddot{e}) + B_p (\dot{x}_d - \dot{e}) + K_p (x_d - e) = F_{patient} + F_{robot}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the robot force required to impose a *desired* impedance $M_d \ddot{e} + B_d \dot{e} + K_d e$ on the error dynamics:**

$$F_{robot} = (M_p \ddot{x}_d + B_p \dot{x}_d + K_p x_d) - (M_d \ddot{e} + B_d \dot{e} + K_d e) - F_{patient} + (M_p - M_d)\ddot{e} + (B_p - B_d)\dot{e} + (K_p - K_d)e$$

**Step 6 — Under the *matched-impedance* assumption $M_d = M_p, B_d = B_p, K_d = K_p$, the error terms cancel and the control law reduces to the clean form:**

$$F_{robot}(t) = M_d(\ddot{x}_d - \ddot{x}) + B_d(\dot{x}_d - \dot{x}) + K_d(x_d - x)$$

This is the **canonical impedance control law** taught in every KTU rehabilitation-engineering module and used as the basis of Armeo, Lokomat, and InMotion controllers.

### 3.2 Derivation of the AAN Tunnel Force

We begin with the deviation magnitude:

$$e(t) = \lVert x(t) - x_d(t) \rVert_2$$

**Step 1 — Define the tunnel threshold $r_{tunnel}$ (typically 5–20 mm in upper-limb robots).**

**Step 2 — Piecewise control law:**

$$F_{AAN}(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & e(t) \leq r_{tunnel} \\ K_{AAN}\,\bigl(e(t) - r_{tunnel}\bigr)\,\hat{n}(t), & e(t) > r_{tunnel} \end{cases}$$

Where $\hat{n}(t) = \dfrac{x_d(t) - x(t)}{\lVert x_d(t) - x(t) \rVert}$ is the unit normal vector pointing from the patient to the path.

**Step 3 — Stability check via Lyapunov candidate:**

Choose $V = \tfrac{1}{2}K_{AAN} e^2 \geq 0$.

Then:

$$\dot{V} = K_{AAN}\, e\, \dot{e} = -K_{AAN}\, e \, \dot{x}_{patient} \cdot \hat{n}$$

If the patient moves in the direction of decreasing $e$ (i.e., $\dot{x}_{patient}\cdot\hat{n} < 0$), then $\dot{V} < 0$ and the system is **asymptotically stable** — meaning the patient is gently guided back without ever being "dragged."

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Common KTU Exam Question

| Robot | Anatomical Target | Mechanism | Control Strategy | Key Sensor | KTU Tag |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MIT-Manus / InMotion ARM** | Shoulder + Elbow | 2-DOF planar end-effector | Impedance + AAN | Force/torque, encoder | Upper, planar |
| **ARMEO Power** | Whole upper limb | 6-DOF arm exoskeleton | Self-initiated, AAN | Angular, grip force | Upper, exoskeleton |
| **Lokomat** | Hip + Knee + Ankle | Bilateral gait orthosis | Position-controlled + AAN | Force sensors in joints | Lower, gait |
| **Gait Trainer GT II** | Gait | End-effector foot-plates | Position + impedance | Foot-plate force | Lower, end-effector |
| **ReWalk / Ekso** | Gait | Overground exoskeleton | Finite-state machine | Tilt, force, EMG | Lower, overground |
| **Anklebot** | Ankle | 2-DOF parallel | Impedance | Back-drivable encoders | Single-joint |
| **STRING-MAN** | Whole-body | Cable-suspended | Admittance | Cable tension | Multi-axis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic Notes for the Table:** Sensor data is fused at 100–1000 Hz. Each row maps onto a *different* KTU Course Outcome: CO3 (robotics design), CO4 (control), CO5 (clinical translation).

### 3.4 Quantitative Worked Example — Lokomat AAN Force

A Lokomat user is post-stroke, treadmill speed 2 km/h, desired knee trajectory $x_d(t) = 0.3\sin(2\pi t)$ m. At $t = 0.25$ s, measured position $x = 0.18$ m. Tunnel radius $r_{tunnel} = 0.015$ m, $K_{AAN} = 800$ N/m.

**Step 1 — Compute desired position at $t = 0.25$ s:**

$$x_d(0.25) = 0.3 \sin(0.5\pi) = 0.3 \times 1.0 = 0.30 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute deviation:**

$$e = \vert 0.18 - 0.30 \vert = 0.12 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Check tunnel condition:**

$$0.12 > 0.015 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{AAN active}$$

**Step 4 — Compute AAN force:**

$$F_{AAN} = 800 \times (0.12 - 0.015) = 800 \times 0.105 = 84 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check (clinical limit):**

$$F_{AAN} = 84 \text{ N} < 100 \text{ N safety clamp} \;\checkmark$$

This 84 N is comparable to a human therapist's elbow-correction force, confirming the physical plausibility of the result.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Therapeutic Robot System

The following Mermaid block diagrams the complete signal flow from patient intent to robot actuation. **All node IDs are alphanumeric-prefixed to avoid reserved-keyword collisions; all labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text with no markdown formatting inside double quotes.**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[PATIENT LIMB] --> A2[LIMB STATE - POSITION VELOCITY FORCE]
    A2 --> B1[ROBOT END EFFECTOR / EXOSKELETON JOINT]
    B1 --> B2[ENCODER + FORCE TORQUE SENSOR]
    B2 --> C1[STATE ESTIMATOR - KALMAN FILTER]
    C1 --> C2[ERROR COMPUTATION - e = xd MINUS x]
    C2 --> D1[THERAPY CONTROLLER CORE]
    D3[THERAPY PROTOCOL LIBRARY] --> D1
    D4[PATIENT DATABASE - EMG HISTORY SCORES] --> D1
    D1 --> D2[ASSIST AS NEEDED FORCE OUTPUT]
    D2 --> E1[SAFETY SUPERVISOR - FORCE VELOCITY LIMITS]
    E1 --> F1[LOW LEVEL MOTOR DRIVER]
    F1 --> G1[BRUSHLESS DC OR SERIES ELASTIC ACTUATOR]
    G1 --> B1
    D1 --> H1[GRAPHICAL USER INTERFACE - THERAPIST DASHBOARD]
    H1 --> I1[PROGRESS REPORT - PDF CSV EXPORT]
```

### 4.2 State-Machine of AAN Control

This diagram captures the discrete control states a typical upper-limb therapeutic robot cycles through during a single reach-to-grasp trial.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S1_INIT
    S1_INIT --> S2_TRIAL_START: Therapist Presses Start
    S2_TRIAL_START --> S3_TRAJECTORY_GUIDE: Patient Unable To Initiate
    S3_TRAJECTORY_GUIDE --> S4_ACTIVE_MOVE: Volitional EMG Detected
    S4_ACTIVE_MOVE --> S5_AAN_ASSIST: Deviation Greater Than Tunnel
    S4_ACTIVE_MOVE --> S6_FREE_MOVE: Deviation Within Tunnel
    S5_AAN_ASSIST --> S4_ACTIVE_MOVE
    S6_FREE_MOVE --> S7_TARGET_REACHED
    S3_TRAJECTORY_GUIDE --> S7_TARGET_REACHED
    S7_TARGET_REACHED --> S8_SCORE_UPDATE
    S8_SCORE_UPDATE --> S2_TRIAL_START
    S8_SCORE_UPDATE --> S9_SESSION_END: Dose Achieved
    S9_SESSION_END --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Anatomical Targeting Map (Conceptual Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[UPPER LIMB] --> L11[Armeo Power]
    L1 --> L12[InMotion ARM]
    L1 --> L13[Hand of Hope]
    L2[LOWER LIMB] --> L21[Lokomat]
    L2 --> L22[Gait Trainer]
    L2 --> L23[ReWalk Ekso Indego]
    L3[SINGLE JOINT] --> L31[Anklebot]
    L3 --> L32[Rutgers Knee]
    L4[TRUNK AND BALANCE] --> L41[BALCOR]
    L4 --> L42[STRING MAN]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Legend (For Examiner Readability)

| Block Prefix | Subsystem | Real Hardware Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| A | Patient Domain | Human limb, EMG electrodes |
| B | Mechanical Interface | Exoskeleton, end-effector |
| C | Sensing & Estimation | Encoders, F/T sensors, Kalman filter |
| D | High-Level Control | PC-based controller, therapy protocol DB |
| E | Safety Layer | Watchdog, ISO 13849 PL d circuitry |
| F | Drive Electronics | PWM amplifiers, current loop |
| G | Actuator | BLDC, SEA, pneumatic muscle |
| H, I | HMI & Reporting | Touchscreen GUI, DICOM export |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** When the exam asks for a "block diagram of a therapeutic robot," the examiner expects *at minimum*: Patient → Sensor → Controller → Actuator → Patient (closed loop). The five blocks above (Sensors, Controller, Safety, Driver, Actuator) earn full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEBMT753]**
**"Differentiate between therapeutic robots, assistive robots, and rehabilitation exoskeletons. Give one clinical example of each."** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer (Examiner-Key Wording):**
- **Therapeutic Robot:** A mechatronic device used *temporarily* during the recovery phase to deliver high-intensity, repetitive, task-specific motor training. The goal is *neuroplastic recovery*, not permanent replacement. *Example:* InMotion ARM for post-stroke shoulder/elbow therapy.
- **Assistive Robot:** A device used *permanently* by a person with disability to *compensate* for lost function in activities of daily living. *Example:* Manus ARM (assistive) — a powered wheelchair-mounted robotic arm for eating and drinking.
- **Rehabilitation Exoskeleton:** A *wearable subset* of robots, structurally aligned with the human skeleton, that *augments or restores* limb motion through rigid external frames. *Example:* Lokomat — a bilateral hip-knee exoskeleton for gait retraining in incomplete SCI.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Key:** Award 1 mark for each *correct definition* and 1 mark for the *correct example*. Writing the example without the definition gets only 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEBMT753]**
**"Explain the term 'Assist-As-Needed' (AAN) control in therapeutic robotics. Why is it preferred over pure position control?"** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
**AAN control** is an adaptive control paradigm in which the robot provides corrective force to the patient *only* when the patient's actual trajectory deviates from a desired reference path beyond a predefined tunnel radius. The control law is:

$$F_{AAN} = \begin{cases} 0, & e \leq r_{tunnel} \\ K_{AAN}(e - r_{tunnel}), & e > r_{tunnel} \end{cases}$$

**Why preferred over pure position control:** Pure position control "drags" the patient through the trajectory, encouraging *slacking* (the patient becomes a passive passenger). AAN preserves patient *engagement* and *volitional effort*, which are essential for **Hebbian plasticity** ("neurons that fire together wire together"). Clinical evidence (VEJOVET, 2020) shows AAN-trained patients retain functional gains 6 months post-discharge, while position-controlled patients regress.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Key:** Formula earns 1 mark, "slacking/preventing slacking" earns 1 mark, Hebbian/clinical-evidence reference earns 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with KTU Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern Note:** Module-4 Part-B questions carry 14 marks and offer an internal choice (either OR). Sub-parts are typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a generic upper-limb therapeutic robot. Identify and describe each block. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Derive the impedance control law $F_{robot} = M_d(\ddot{x}_d - \ddot{x}) + B_d(\dot{x}_d - \dot{x}) + K_d(x_d - x)$ starting from the coupled patient-robot dynamic equation. Show the matched-impedance simplification step clearly. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

| Block | Name | Function | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Patient Domain | Source of volitional motor intent; physical attachment of limb to robot | 1 |
| 2 | Mechanical Interface | End-effector or exoskeleton that physically constrains the limb | 1 |
| 3 | Sensor Suite | Encoders (position), F/T sensors (force), EMG (intent) | 1 |
| 4 | State Estimator | Kalman filter to denoise sensor data and estimate full state | 1 |
| 5 | Therapy Controller | Computes reference trajectory, runs AAN algorithm, adapts gains | 1 |
| 6 | Safety Supervisor | Enforces ISO 13849 force/velocity limits; emergency stop | 1 |
| 7 | Actuator & Driver | BLDC motor + amplifier executing the controller's command | 0.5 |
| 8 | HMI / Reporting | Therapist interface, progress logs, DICOM export | 0.5 |

(Any equivalent block diagram with clear arrow directions and labels = full 7 marks. Arrows must be closed-loop — examiner deducts 1 mark if the loop is open.)

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark) — Write the coupled dynamic equation:**

$$M_p \ddot{x} + B_p \dot{x} + K_p x = F_{patient} + F_{robot}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark) — Define tracking error:**

$$e(t) = x_d(t) - x(t)$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark) — Differentiate twice:**

$$\ddot{x} = \ddot{x}_d - \ddot{e}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark) — Substitute and rearrange:**

$$F_{robot} = M_p \ddot{x}_d + B_p \dot{x}_d + K_p x_d - (M_p \ddot{e} + B_p \dot{e} + K_p e) - F_{patient}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark) — Apply the matched-impedance assumption $M_d = M_p$, $B_d = B_p$, $K_d = K_p$:**

$$F_{robot} = M_d(\ddot{x}_d - \ddot{x}) + B_d(\dot{x}_d - \dot{x}) + K_d(x_d - x) - F_{patient}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark) — Note that if $F_{patient}$ is treated as an external disturbance and absorbed into the feedback term (decoupled assumption):**

$$F_{robot}(t) = M_d(\ddot{x}_d - \ddot{x}) + B_d(\dot{x}_d - \dot{x}) + K_d(x_d - x)$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark) — Physical interpretation:**
- $K_d$ = stiffness → how strongly the robot pulls the patient back to the path.
- $B_d$ = damping → how smoothly it absorbs velocity mismatch.
- $M_d$ = inertia → how "heavy" the robot feels to the patient.

Tuning these three parameters allows the same robot to act as a guide, a coach, or a resistor.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the Assist-As-Needed (AAN) control algorithm with the piecewise force equation. Show, using a Lyapunov function, that the system is asymptotically stable when the patient moves toward the desired path. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Compare the end-effector and exoskeleton architectures of upper-limb therapeutic robots. Mention two clinical examples of each and state two advantages and two disadvantages of each. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1.5 Marks) — Define deviation and tunnel:**

$$e(t) = \lVert x(t) - x_d(t) \rVert, \quad r_{tunnel} = \text{constant (typically 5–20 mm)}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 Marks) — Piecewise control law:**

$$F_{AAN}(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & e \leq r_{tunnel} \\ K_{AAN}\,\bigl(e - r_{tunnel}\bigr)\,\hat{n}, & e > r_{tunnel} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark) — Lyapunov candidate:**

$$V(e) = \tfrac{1}{2}K_{AAN}\,e^2 \;\geq\; 0$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark) — Differentiate:**

$$\dot{V} = K_{AAN}\,e\,\dot{e} = -K_{AAN}\,e\,\dot{x}_{patient}\cdot\hat{n}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark) — Show stability:**
If patient velocity has a component opposing the deviation, $\dot{x}_{patient}\cdot\hat{n} < 0$, so $\dot{V} < 0$. By Lyapunov's direct method, the system is **asymptotically stable** at $e = 0$.

**Step 6 (1 Mark) — Clinical implication:**
The AAN controller does not "pull" the patient — it only "catches" them when they err, preserving volitional effort and enabling cortical re-mapping.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

| Feature | End-Effector | Exoskeleton | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Attachment Point** | Distal segment only (hand) | Multiple joints (shoulder, elbow, wrist) | 1 |
| **Examples** | InMotion ARM (MIT-Manus), ARM Guide | ARMEO Power, ABLE, T-WREX | 1 |
| **Mechanical Complexity** | Low (1–2 actuators) | High (5–7 actuators) | 1 |
| **Alignment Issue** | Misalignment possible | Kinematically matched to skeleton | 1 |
| **Patient Comfort** | Easy don/doff | Heavier, longer setup | 0.5 |
| **Workspace Coverage** | Limited planar 2D | Full 3D reaching space | 0.5 |
| **Force Application** | Net endpoint force | Distributed joint torques | 0.5 |
| **Clinical Use** | Sub-acute, distal weakness | Chronic, multi-joint involvement | 0.5 |
| **Advantage 1** | Simple, safe, low cost | Better selectivity, can resist at any joint | 0.5 |
| **Advantage 2** | No skeletal alignment needed | Can isolate joint-specific impairment | 0.5 |
| **Disadvantage 1** | Cannot isolate single joint | Misalignment causes skin pressure, pain | 0.5 |
| **Disadvantage 2** | Cannot apply resistive torque at proximal joints | High cost (₹25L–1Cr) | 0.5 |

(Examiner scales proportionally; full table filled = 7 marks. Missing rows = proportional deduction.)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on this topic — read carefully:**
>
> 1. **Confusing therapeutic vs. assistive robots** — A Lokomat is *therapeutic*; a Manus ARM is *assistive*; a ReWalk used at home is *assistive*, but ReWalk used in a clinic for gait retraining is *therapeutic*. Many students write the wrong category. **Penalty: 1–2 marks per question.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to mention SLACKING** — When explaining AAN vs. position control, students often describe AAN correctly but omit the word *"slacking"* or *"patient engagement."* This is the examiner's keyword for full marks. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Sign errors in the impedance derivation** — The classic error is writing $F = M_d\ddot{x} + B_d\dot{x} + K_d x$ (no deviation). Always express force in terms of **error** $(x_d - x)$. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
>
> 4. **Open-loop block diagrams** — Drawing the patient → sensor → controller → actuator chain without a *feedback arrow back to the sensor* is an instant **−1 mark**.
>
> 5. **Units in numerical problems** — Students often write $F = 84$ without the unit "N." KTU examiners *do* deduct for missing SI units in 14-mark numericals. **Penalty: 0.5 mark per missing unit.**
>
> 6. **Forgetting ISO/IEC 60601-1** — Therapeutic robots are **active medical devices**. Any answer that classifies them as "general machinery" loses a mark on CO5 (professional ethics/safety).
>
> 7. **Conflating Lokomat with ReWalk** — Lokomat = *treadmill-based* exoskeleton for in-clinic gait training. ReWalk = *overground* exoskeleton for community ambulation. They are **not** the same device.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this and stick it on your wall the night before the exam.**

- [x] A **Therapeutic Robot** is a mechatronic device for *temporary* recovery-phase training; an **Assistive Robot** is for *permanent* functional compensation.
- [x] The **canonical impedance law** is $F = M_d(\ddot{x}_d - \ddot{x}) + B_d(\dot{x}_d - \dot{x}) + K_d(x_d - x)$ — write it in terms of **error**, never raw $x$.
- [x] **AAN** stands for **Assist-As-Needed** — zero force inside the tunnel, restoring force outside.
- [x] **Hogan (1985)** is the inventor of impedance control — name-drop him in derivations.
- [x] **Series-Elastic Actuators (SEA)** are the gold standard for therapeutic robots because they store transient energy in a spring, giving intrinsic force-control bandwidth and impact safety.
- [x] Key clinical examples to memorize: **MIT-Manus, ARMEO Power, Lokomat, Gait Trainer, ReWalk, Anklebot, String-Man, BALCOR**.
- [x] Therapeutic robots are **Class IIa/IIb** medical devices under **IEC 60601-1** and must be **ISO 13849 PL d** compliant for safety circuits.
- [x] Clinical dose target: **600–1,000 repetitions per session** to drive Hebbian plasticity.
- [x] Backdrivability (low intrinsic friction) is **non-negotiable** for patient safety.
- [x] The Lyapunov stability proof for AAN uses $V = \tfrac{1}{2}K_{AAN}e^2$.
- [x] Always mention **slacking prevention** when contrasting AAN with position control.
- [x] Lokomat = treadmill; ReWalk = overground. **Do not mix them up.**
- [x] End-effector robots attach distally; exoskeletons attach at every joint — they have **opposite** misalignment profiles.
- [x] KTU exam mantra: **"Quantifiable, Backdrivable, Adaptive, Gentle = THE Q-BAG."**

---

> **End of Module 4 — Therapeutic Robots (PEBMT753 | KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> *Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 standards. Aligned with Course Outcomes CO3 (Design), CO4 (Control), and CO5 (Clinical Translation).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Telecommunications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT753 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Telecommunications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Telecommunications in Rehabilitation Engineering** is the discipline that applies electronic communication, signal processing, and information transmission technologies to enable persons with **sensory (hearing/visual), speech, motor, or cognitive impairments** to *send, receive, and interpret* information across distance, in a manner functionally equivalent to unimpaired communication.

The term encompasses a broad spectrum of assistive devices, from low-tech **communication boards** to high-tech **Cochlear Implants, TTY/TDD terminals, Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) devices, and Video Relay Services (VRS)**. The goal is *functional communication equivalence* — not merely amplification, but the restoration of bi-directional information flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Categories of Rehabilitation Telecommunications Devices (KTU Syllabus Tag):**
> 1. **Assistive Listening Devices (ALDs)** — Hearing Aids, Cochlear Implants, FM Systems, Loop Systems
> 2. **Text-Based Devices** — TTY/TDD, ASCII terminals
> 3. **Augmentative & Alternative Communication (AAC)** — Speech Generating Devices (SGDs), Picture Boards
> 4. **Video/Internet-Based Systems** — VRS, VRI, IP-Relay, CapTel
> 5. **Alerting/Signaling Devices** — Visual & Tactile alerting systems

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are in a *very noisy factory* trying to talk to a friend on the other side of the building. You cannot speak louder than the noise; your friend cannot hear soft sounds. What do you do?

- You could **write letters** (analogous to **TTY/TDD**).
- You could use a **loudspeaker + microphone walkie-talkie** (analogous to **FM Loop System**).
- You could **type a message that becomes speech on their end** (analogous to **AAC / Speech Generating Device**).
- You could **turn on a flashing light instead of a doorbell** (analogous to a **visual alert**).
- You could **show a picture of what you need** (analogous to a **communication board**).

Rehabilitation telecommunications is essentially that — engineering equivalent pathways around the broken link in the communication chain. If the *ear* is broken, we route sound around it (a hearing aid or cochlear implant). If the *voice* is broken, we replace text-to-speech or a picture board. If the *hand* is broken, we switch to voice-recognition, sip-and-puff, or eye-tracking.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember (in bold) for KTU exams:**
> - **Speech frequency band: 300 Hz to 3400 Hz** (telephone channel standard, ITU-T)
> - **Full audio band for music/hearing aids: 20 Hz to 20 kHz**
> - **Hearing Aid Typical Gain: 30 to 50 dB**
> - **Maximum Power Output (MPO): 110 to 130 dB SPL**
> - **TTY Baud Rate: 45.45 baud (Baudot code)**
> - **HAC Standard: TIA-1083**
> - **FCC Mandate: ≥ 50% of phones must be Hearing Aid Compatible (HAC)**

### 1.3 Why Telecommunications is Central to Rehabilitation

A person with hearing loss is not "dumb" — they are *information-deprived*. The ear is a *telecommunications channel*. When it fails, the brain receives a degraded or absent signal. The rehabilitation engineer's role is to design a *better channel* — a **man-made telecommunications system** that bypasses the broken biological link.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** Rehabilitation Engineering views the **Human Body as a Communication System**:
> - **Transducer (Ear / Eye)** → **Channel (Auditory nerve / Optical nerve)** → **Processor (Brain)** → **Transducer (Vocal cords / Hand)**
> When any part of this chain is impaired, the rehabilitation engineer designs a *substitute* or *augmentation* path.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Rehabilitation Telecommunication Devices

The KTU syllabus organizes these devices into a clear taxonomy. Mastering this taxonomy is essential for 7- and 14-mark questions.

#### A. By User Impairment Type

| Impairment | Device Class | Example Devices |
|---|---|---|
| **Hearing Loss** | Assistive Listening Devices (ALDs) | Behind-The-Ear (BTE) hearing aid, FM system, Induction loop |
| **Profound / Total Deafness** | Sensory Substitution | TTY/TDD, VRS, CapTel, Cochlear implant |
| **Speech Loss (Aphonia, Dysarthria)** | Augmentative & Alternative Communication (AAC) | Speech Generating Device (SGD), picture board |
| **Combined Speech + Hearing** | Dual-Sensory Devices | TTY + VCO (Voice Carry Over) |
| **Motor Disability** | Switch-Interface AAC | Sip-and-puff switch, eye-gaze AAC, head-tracking AAC |

#### B. By Signal Modality

| Modality | Information Carrier | Typical Device |
|---|---|---|
| **Auditory (Acoustic)** | Amplified sound waves | Hearing aid, personal amplifier |
| **Tactile (Cutaneous)** | Vibration on skin | Tactile alerting, Tadoma method |
| **Visual (Optical)** | Light, text on screen | Visual alert, CapTel captioning |
| **Electrophysiological** | Electrical stimulation of nerve | Cochlear implant, auditory brainstem implant |
| **Textual/Symbolic** | Letters, pictograms | Communication board, AAC |

### 2.2 The Universal Rehabilitation Communication Pipeline

Every rehabilitation telecommunications device, no matter how complex, can be modeled as a **five-stage signal processing chain**:

1. **Input Transducer** — Microphone, camera, switch, or eye-tracker
2. **Signal Conditioning** — Amplification, filtering, noise reduction
3. **Signal Processing / Coding** — DSP, encryption, modulation, feature extraction
4. **Output Transducer** — Speaker (loudspeaker), display, vibrator, electrode array
5. **Feedback / Verification** — Indicator LEDs, screen echoes, tactile confirmation

### 2.3 Critical Specifications to Memorize

#### A. Hearing Aid Specifications

| Parameter | Typical Range | Unit | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequency Response** | **200 to 6000** (BTE) | Hz | Covers speech band |
| **Peak Gain (SSPL90)** | **30 to 70** | dB | Mild to severe loss |
| **Maximum Power Output** | **110 to 130** | dB SPL | MPO limiter protects residual hearing |
| **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** | **< 3 to 5** | % | Quality metric |
| **Equivalent Input Noise (EIN)** | **< 25 to 30** | dB SPL | Lower is better |
| **Battery Current** | **0.5 to 3** | mA | Battery life driver |
| **Number of Channels** | **2 to 48** | — | Digital hearing aid |

#### B. TTY / TDD Specifications

| Parameter | Value | Note |
|---|---|---|
| **Code** | **5-bit Baudot (ITA-2)** | Originates from telegraph era |
| **Baud Rate** | **45.45 baud** | Standard US TDD |
| **Frequency (Originate)** | **1275 Hz (mark) / 1075 Hz (space)** | FSK modulation |
| **Frequency (Answer)** | **2225 Hz (mark) / 2025 Hz (space)** | FSK modulation |
| **Bits per Character** | **7.5** (1 start + 5 data + 1.5 stop) | Bit-serial transmission |
| **Characters per Second** | **≈ 6.06** | Real-world typing speed |
| **Standard** | **EIA/TIA-825 (US), ITU-T V.18** | Compliance |

#### C. AAC / SGD Specifications

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Vocabulary Capacity** | **8 to 20,000+ symbols** | Stored in device |
| **Synthesis Methods** | **Concatenative / Formant / Articulatory** | Quality trade-off |
| **Switch Access Latency** | **< 100 ms** | Critical for fluent AAC |
| **Output Modes** | **Synthesized speech / text display** | Multi-modal |

### 2.4 Engineering Trade-offs (Why "More" is Not Always Better)

- **High Gain ≠ Good Hearing Aid:** Excess gain causes *acoustic feedback* (whistling) and *recruitment discomfort*.
- **More Channels ≠ Better Hearing Aid:** Channel count must match the audiogram resolution; over-processing distorts speech cues.
- **Higher Text Rate ≠ Better TTY:** TDDs are intentionally *slow* (45.45 baud) for compatibility with the acoustic coupler of analog telephone handsets.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula** a KTU 2024 student is expected to know for this topic. Memorize the variables and units.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} \right)$ | Voltage gain in decibels (electrical) | $V$ in volts (V) |
| 2 | $G_{acoustic} = SPL_{out} - SPL_{in}$ | Acoustic gain of hearing aid (dB) | $SPL$ in dB SPL |
| 3 | $SPL_{out} = SPL_{in} + G_{acoustic}$ | Output sound pressure level | dB SPL |
| 4 | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} \right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | $P$ in watts (W) |
| 5 | $T_{char} = \dfrac{n_{bits}}{R_{baud}}$ | Time per character (TTY) | $T$ in seconds, $R$ in baud |
| 6 | $\text{CPS} = \dfrac{R_{baud}}{n_{bits}}$ | Characters per second | dimensionless |
| 7 | $BW = f_{H} - f_{L}$ | Channel bandwidth (Hz) | $f$ in Hz |
| 8 | $C = BW \cdot \log_{2}\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Shannon–Hartley channel capacity | $C$ in bits/s |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Efficiency | percentage |
| 10 | $L_{I} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{I_{out}}{I_{in}} \right)$ | Current gain (dB) | $I$ in amperes (A) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Do not confuse** the *voltage* formula (factor 20) with the *power* formula (factor 10). This is a **classic KTU pitfall** — examiners love to test it.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hearing Aid Acoustic Gain from Circuit Parameters

A hearing aid can be modeled as a cascade of three functional blocks: a **microphone** (acoustic-to-electric transducer), an **amplifier** (with voltage gain $A_v$), and a **receiver** (electric-to-acoustic transducer). The total acoustic gain must be derived step-by-step.

**Step 1 — Define the microphone sensitivity ($S_m$)**
The microphone converts an incident sound pressure $p_{in}$ (in Pascal) into a voltage $V_m$:

$$S_m = \frac{V_m}{p_{in}} \quad [\text{units: V/Pa}]$$

Common hearing aid microphones have sensitivities of the order of **-46 dB V/Pa = 5 mV/Pa** (re 1 V/Pa).

**Step 2 — Apply the amplifier voltage gain**
The amplifier multiplies the microphone voltage by a dimensionless factor $A_v$:

$$V_{out,elec} = A_v \cdot V_m = A_v \cdot S_m \cdot p_{in}$$

**Step 3 — Define the receiver sensitivity ($S_r$)**
The receiver converts an applied voltage into a sound pressure level. It is usually specified as a *transfer function* in **dB SPL per volt** at a reference distance (e.g., 1 cm or via a 2-cc coupler):

$$p_{out} = S_r \cdot V_{out,elec} = S_r \cdot A_v \cdot S_m \cdot p_{in}$$

**Step 4 — Express in dB**
Converting pressures to dB SPL (using reference $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$):

$$SPL_{out} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{S_r \cdot A_v \cdot S_m \cdot p_{in}}{p_0}\right)$$

Splitting the logarithm:

$$SPL_{out} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{in}}{p_0}\right) + 20 \log_{10}(S_m) + 20 \log_{10}(A_v) + 20 \log_{10}(S_r)$$

**Step 5 — Identify the acoustic gain**
Since $SPL_{in} = 20 \log_{10}(p_{in}/p_0)$, the acoustic gain is therefore:

$$G_{acoustic} = SPL_{out} - SPL_{in} = 20\log_{10}(S_m) + 20\log_{10}(A_v) + 20\log_{10}(S_r)$$

The result is the **sum of the dB microphone sensitivity, dB voltage gain, and dB receiver sensitivity**.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A hearing aid has microphone sensitivity $S_m = 5$ mV/Pa, amplifier voltage gain $A_v = 200$, and receiver sensitivity $S_r = 100$ Pa/V (all referenced to a 2-cc coupler). For an input of $p_{in} = 0.02$ Pa (= 60 dB SPL speech), find the output SPL.

**Step 1 — Microphone output voltage**

$$V_m = S_m \cdot p_{in} = (5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (0.02) = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} = 0.1 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 2 — Amplifier output voltage**

$$V_{out,elec} = A_v \cdot V_m = 200 \cdot 0.1 \text{ mV} = 20 \text{ mV} = 0.02 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Receiver output pressure**

$$p_{out} = S_r \cdot V_{out,elec} = 100 \cdot 0.02 = 2 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to dB SPL**

$$SPL_{out} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(100{,}000) = 20 \cdot 5 = 100 \text{ dB SPL}$$

**Step 5 — Acoustic gain**

$$G_{acoustic} = 100 - 60 = 40 \text{ dB}$$

This is well within the typical 30-50 dB range for a Behind-The-Ear (BTE) hearing aid. ✅

### 3.3 Derivation: TTY / TDD Character Rate

**Step 1 — Bit timing**
At a baud rate $R_{baud}$ (bits per second), the duration of one bit is:

$$T_{bit} = \frac{1}{R_{baud}}$$

For a standard TDD, $R_{baud} = 45.45$ baud, so:

$$T_{bit} = \frac{1}{45.45} \approx 22 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Bits per character**
Baudot code uses 1 start + 5 data + 1.5 stop = 7.5 bits per character.

**Step 3 — Time per character**

$$T_{char} = 7.5 \cdot T_{bit} = 7.5 \cdot 22 \text{ ms} = 165 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Characters per second**

$$\text{CPS} = \frac{1}{T_{char}} = \frac{1}{0.165} \approx 6.06 \text{ characters/second}$$

This slow rate is the price paid for **compatibility with acoustic couplers** strapped to analog telephone handsets — the *telephone channel* (300–3400 Hz) cannot support faster FSK without errors.

### 3.4 Derivation: Shannon-Hartley Limit Applied to Telephone Channel

A rehabilitation engineer may ask: *What is the maximum data rate we can push through a phone line to send text-based AAC data?*

**Step 1 — Bandwidth of telephone channel**

$$BW = 3400 - 300 = 3100 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Assume SNR = 30 dB (typical clean landline)**

$$\frac{S}{N} = 10^{30/10} = 1000$$

**Step 3 — Apply Shannon–Hartley theorem**

$$C = BW \cdot \log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) = 3100 \cdot \log_{2}(1001)$$

$$\log_{2}(1001) = \frac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} \approx \frac{3.0004}{0.3010} \approx 9.967$$

$$C = 3100 \cdot 9.967 \approx 30{,}900 \text{ bits/second}$$

This is *why* modern text-over-phone services (V.18, V.21, V.23 modems) can use ASCII at 300 baud or 1200 baud — comfortably within the Shannon limit — while old Baudot TTYs are stuck at 45.45 baud for *acoustic-coupler compatibility*, not channel capacity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Digital Hearing Aid (DSP-Based)

The following Mermaid diagram captures the *entire signal chain* of a modern digital hearing aid, including the *feedback canceller* and *MPO limiter* — both critical subsystems examined frequently in KTU papers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Mic[Microphone - Acoustic to Electric] --> Preamp[Pre Amplifier]
    Preamp --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> PreProc[Pre Processing - Wind Noise Reduction]
    PreProc --> Filterbank[Multi Channel Filterbank]
    Filterbank --> Comp[Compression Amplifier AGC]
    Comp --> NR[Noise Reduction Spectral Subtraction]
    NR --> FB[Feedback Canceller]
    FB --> MPO[MPO Limiter]
    MPO --> DAC[Digital to Analog Converter]
    DAC --> Rec[Receiver - Electric to Acoustic]
    Rec --> Ear[Ear Canal of User]
    MPO --> OutLev[Output Level Indicator LED]
    Filterbank --> ChannelCtrl[Channel Wise Gain Control]
    ChannelCtrl --> Comp
    PreProc --> BattMon[Battery Monitor]
    BattMon --> BattLED[Low Battery Alert]
```

**Reading the diagram (left → right):** Sound enters the microphone, is digitized, processed in 2 to 48 frequency channels, compressed to fit the user's dynamic range, denoised, feedback-cancelled, limited to a safe MPO, converted back to analog, and delivered to the ear canal.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — TTY / TDD Communication System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    UserA[User A - Deaf - Keyboard Input] --> KeyA[ASCII or Baudot Keyboard Encoder]
    KeyA --> ModA[FSK Modulator]
    ModA --> LineA[Acoustic Coupler or Direct Connect]
    LineA --> PSTN[Public Switched Telephone Network]
    PSTN --> LineB[Acoustic Coupler or Direct Connect]
    LineB --> DemodB[FSK Demodulator]
    DemodB --> DecB[Baudot Decoder]
    DecB --> DispB[Character Display or Printer]
    DispB --> UserB[User B - Reads Text]
    UserB --> KeyB[Keyboard Encoder]
    KeyB --> ModB[FSK Modulator]
    ModB --> LineB
    LineB --> PSTN
    PSTN --> LineA
    LineA --> DemodA[FSK Demodulator]
    DemodA --> DecA[Decoder]
    DecA --> DispA[Display or Printer]
    DispA --> UserA
```

**Key insight:** TTY/TDD is a *half-duplex* system. Both users must take turns — they cannot "talk" simultaneously because the channel is shared.

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    User[User with Speech Impairment] --> Switch[Access Method - Switch, Touch, Eye Gaze, Sip Puff]
    Switch --> Sel[Symbol or Letter Selection]
    Sel --> Lang[Language Model and Word Prediction]
    Lang --> Buf[Message Buffer]
    Buf --> Out1[Text Display LCD]
    Buf --> Out2[Speech Synthesizer Text to Speech]
    Buf --> Out3[Stored Phrases and Hot Keys]
    Out2 --> Spk[Speaker Output to Listener]
    Out1 --> List[Listener or Communication Partner]
    Out3 --> Spk
    Sel --> ML[Machine Learning Predictor]
    ML --> Lang
    Buf --> Store[Message History and Storage]
    Store --> USB[USB or Cloud Backup]
```

**Why this matters:** AAC is the *fastest-growing* rehabilitation technology segment, and KTU 2024 examiners expect a labeled block diagram with at least **5 functional blocks**. A student who draws only "input → output" loses 4 to 5 marks.

### 4.4 Schematic — Frequency Allocation for TDD FSK

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Orig[Originate Mode - User A]
        OMark[1275 Hz - Mark - Binary 1]
        OSpace[1075 Hz - Space - Binary 0]
    end
    subgraph Ans[Answer Mode - User B]
        AMark[2225 Hz - Mark - Binary 1]
        ASpace[2025 Hz - Space - Binary 0]
    end
    subgraph Chan[Telephone Channel 300 to 3400 Hz]
        BW[Available Bandwidth 3100 Hz]
    end
    Orig --> Chan
    Ans --> Chan
```

The two pairs of frequencies (originate vs. answer) are offset by exactly **1000 Hz** to prevent the *echo* of the originating signal from being mistaken for the answer signal. The 200 Hz mark/space separation within each pair is the *FSK deviation* — wide enough to survive telephone-channel noise.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1. [3 Marks] Define *Telecommunications Device for the Deaf (TDD)*. Mention the standard baud rate and modulation scheme used.**

**Model Answer:**
A TDD (also called TTY) is a text-based telecommunication device that allows persons with hearing or speech impairments to communicate over standard telephone lines by typing messages. It uses **5-bit Baudot (ITA-2) code** at a **baud rate of 45.45 baud** with **Frequency Shift Keying (FSK)** modulation. The originate mode uses 1275 Hz (mark) and 1075 Hz (space), and the answer mode uses 2225 Hz and 2025 Hz. Standard: **EIA/TIA-825 / ITU-T V.18**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. [3 Marks] List any *three* differences between a hearing aid and a cochlear implant.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Hearing Aid | Cochlear Implant |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Acoustic** output (amplified sound) | **Electrical** output (stimulates auditory nerve directly) |
| 2 | For **mild-to-severe** hearing loss | For **severe-to-profound** hearing loss |
| 3 | **Non-invasive**, worn externally | **Surgically implanted** with external sound processor |
| 4 | Amplifies residual hearing | Bypasses damaged hair cells |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice: 14 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**

---

#### ✅ OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks] Explain the various categories of Telecommunication Devices used for persons with hearing impairment, with examples.**

**Model Solution:**

Persons with hearing impairment span a wide spectrum — from mild presbycusis to profound deafness — and no single device serves all. The rehabilitation engineer classifies their devices into **five categories**:

1. **Assistive Listening Devices (ALDs):** These *augment* residual hearing. They include:
   - **Personal amplifiers** — handheld microphone + headphones.
   - **FM Systems** — a transmitter microphone worn by the speaker, and a receiver worn by the listener; radio-frequency link (~72–216 MHz) carries clean speech to the listener.
   - **Induction Loop Systems** — a wire loop around a room generates a magnetic field picked up by the hearing aid's *T-coil (telecoil)*. Standard: **IEC 60118-4**.

2. **Hearing Aids:** Three form factors — **BTE (Behind-The-Ear), ITE (In-The-Ear), CIC (Completely-In-Canal)**. Modern digital aids use DSP, multi-channel compression, directional microphones, and feedback cancellation.

3. **Cochlear Implants:** For severe-to-profound loss where hearing aids are insufficient. External microphone → sound processor → **transcutaneous RF link** → implanted electrode array in the **cochlea** (typically 16–22 electrodes). The electrodes directly stimulate the auditory nerve fibers.

4. **Text-Based Devices (TTY/TDD):** Discussed in Q1 above — Baudot-coded FSK over the PSTN at 45.45 baud.

5. **Video and Internet-Based Services:**
   - **Video Relay Service (VRS):** A sign-language interpreter relays the call between Deaf (signing on video) and Hearing (voice) parties.
   - **CapTel (Captioned Telephone):** A live stenographer/transcription engine captions the caller's speech in real time.
   - **IP-Relay / VCO / HCO:** Text or voice carry-over services over IP.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often *omit VRS and CapTel* and list only hearing aids and TTYs. The 2024 syllabus explicitly mentions *"modern telecommunication aids"*, so **VRS, CapTel, and IP-Relay are compulsory** for full marks.

---

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a *digital hearing aid*. List its key specifications.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1)

**Working Principle:**

The microphone converts the incident acoustic pressure into a small analog voltage (sensitivity ≈ -46 dB V/Pa). This signal is pre-amplified and digitized by an **ADC** (typically 16-bit, sampling at 16–48 kHz). The DSP core performs the following in real time:

1. **Pre-processing:** Wind-noise detection and reduction, adaptive filtering of low-frequency rumble.
2. **Filterbank analysis:** The signal is split into 2 to 48 frequency bands (e.g., 4 kHz-wide channels).
3. **Multi-channel compression:** Each band has its own *compression ratio* and *compression threshold* matched to the user's audiogram — a technique called **WDRC (Wide Dynamic Range Compression)**.
4. **Noise reduction:** Spectral subtraction or modulation-based detection; speech is preserved, noise is attenuated.
5. **Feedback cancellation:** An adaptive filter (NLMS algorithm) estimates the acoustic feedback path and subtracts it, eliminating whistle.
6. **MPO limiting:** The output is hard-limited to the user's **Uncomfortable Level (UCL)** — typically 110–130 dB SPL — protecting residual hearing.
7. The processed digital signal is converted back to analog (DAC) and delivered through the receiver into the ear canal.

**Key Specifications:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Frequency Response | **200 Hz to 6 kHz** (BTE) |
| Peak Gain | **40–70 dB** |
| MPO | **110–130 dB SPL** |
| THD | **< 3%** |
| EIN | **< 25 dB SPL** |
| Battery | **Zinc-air, ~5–14 days life** |
| Channels | **2 to 48** |

---

#### ✅ OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks] Explain *Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC)* systems. List the main types with examples.**

**Model Solution:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AAC is the set of tools and strategies used by individuals who cannot meet their communication needs through speech alone.** It is the cornerstone of rehabilitation for persons with **cerebral palsy, ALS (Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis), autism, aphasia, laryngectomy, and traumatic brain injury**.

**Two main categories:**

**A. Aided AAC** — requires an external tool:
- **Low-tech:** Communication boards with picture symbols (PECS — Picture Exchange Communication System), alphabet boards, E-Tran frames.
- **High-tech:** Speech-Generating Devices (SGDs), tablet-based AAC apps (e.g., Proloquo2Go, TouchChat), eye-gaze systems (Tobii), head-tracking systems.

**B. Unaided AAC** — uses only the body:
- **Gestures**, manual signs (ASL/ISL), finger-spelling, natural gestures.

**Components of a modern AAC system** (refer Section 4.3 Mermaid diagram):

1. **Access method** — direct touch, switch (single or two-switch scanning), head-tracking, eye-gaze, sip-and-puff.
2. **Vocabulary** — stored as symbols, words, and phrases. Organized by category, frequency, or **Minspeak (semantic compaction)**.
3. **Language model / Word prediction** — accelerates typing; word-prediction engines like **WordQ** or **Predictable**.
4. **Output modalities:**
   - **Synthesized speech** (text-to-speech, TTS) using formant, concatenative, or unit-selection synthesis.
   - **Text display** for partners to read.
   - **Stored phrases** for one-touch retrieval.
5. **Memory / Storage** — for message history and personalized phrases.

**Example devices:** Tobii Dynavox, PRC Accent, Liberator devices, Smartbox, Eye-Gaze Edge.

---

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks] Draw the block diagram of a *TTY/TDD* system and explain its working. Mention any five specifications.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2)

**Working Principle:**

A TTY is a *text-over-voice-line* device. Each user has a keyboard, a small character display (LED or LCD), and a modem (FSK modulator/demodulator). The connection to the PSTN can be either:

- **Direct connect** (a cable plugs into the phone wall jack), or
- **Acoustic coupler** (rubber cups that hold the telephone handset, with speaker/microphone inside).

**Transmission steps (User A → User B):**

1. User A types a character on the keyboard.
2. The Baudot encoder converts the character to a 5-bit code (or ASCII in newer TTYs).
3. The FSK modulator generates 1275 Hz (mark = 1) or 1075 Hz (space = 0) tones.
4. These tones are sent through the telephone line (PSTN).
5. At User B's end, the FSK demodulator converts tones back to bits.
6. The Baudot decoder maps the 5-bit code to a character, displayed on User B's screen.

For the **reverse direction** (B → A), the answer-mode frequencies (2225/2025 Hz) are used to avoid confusion with echo of the originate signal.

**Five Specifications:**

| # | Specification | Value |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Code | 5-bit Baudot (ITA-2) |
| 2 | Baud rate | 45.45 baud |
| 3 | Originate FSK | 1275 / 1075 Hz |
| 4 | Answer FSK | 2225 / 2025 Hz |
| 5 | Characters per second | ~6.06 CPS |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often draw the diagram as "keyboard → telephone" without showing the **modulator, demodulator, encoder, decoder, and acoustic coupler**. Each of these five blocks carries 1–2 marks. Missing blocks = 3 to 5 marks lost.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (read 5 minutes before exam):**

- ⭐ **Telecommunications in rehabilitation** = use of communication technology to bypass or augment impaired sensory/motor channels.
- ⭐ **Five device categories:** ALDs, Hearing Aids/Cochlear Implants, AAC, Text-based (TTY/TDD), Video/Internet-based (VRS, CapTel).
- ⭐ **Hearing aid acoustic gain** = $SPL_{out} - SPL_{in}$ (in dB). Typical: 30–50 dB. MPO: 110–130 dB SPL.
- ⭐ **MPO limiter** is a *safety feature* preventing over-amplification that could damage residual hearing.
- ⭐ **Digital hearing aid** uses ADC → DSP → DAC chain with multi-channel WDRC compression.
- ⭐ **TTY/TDD** uses 5-bit Baudot at **45.45 baud**, FSK with 1275/1075 Hz (originate) and 2225/2025 Hz (answer).
- ⭐ **TTY is half-duplex** — both users alternate; baud rate is intentionally slow for acoustic-coupler compatibility.
- ⭐ **AAC** = Aided (low-tech boards, high-tech SGDs) and Unaided (signs, gestures). Components: access method, vocabulary, language model, output, memory.
- ⭐ **Cochlear implant** = surgical; bypasses damaged hair cells; stimulates auditory nerve directly via electrode array.
- ⭐ **VRS** = sign-language interpreter over video link; **CapTel** = real-time captions; **IP-Relay** = text over IP.
- ⭐ **Gain formulas:** Voltage/current use factor **20**; Power uses factor **10**. Never mix them.
- ⭐ **SNR formula:** $10 \log_{10}(P_s/P_n)$. Speech intelligibility falls sharply when SNR < 0 dB.
- ⭐ **Shannon–Hartley:** $C = BW \cdot \log_2(1 + S/N)$. Phone channel (3.1 kHz, 30 dB SNR) → ~31 kbps capacity.
- ⭐ **HAC (Hearing Aid Compatible)** standard: **TIA-1083**; FCC requires ≥ 50% of phones HAC-compliant.
- ⭐ **Speech band:** **300 Hz to 3400 Hz** (telephone); **20 Hz to 20 kHz** (full audio).
- ⭐ **Always draw block diagrams** with *5+ labeled blocks* for full marks on Part B 14-mark questions.
- ⭐ **Examiner's "must include" terms** in any answer: transducer, signal processing, bandwidth, SNR, FSK, Baudot.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT753 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Computers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Rehabilitation Engineering**, **Computers** refers to the systematic application of computer hardware, software, and peripheral technologies as **Assistive Technology (AT)** devices to mitigate functional limitations imposed by physical, sensory, cognitive, or communication disabilities. The computer, when properly configured with adaptive peripherals and specialized software, functions as a universal enabler — restoring or augmenting a user's independence in **Education, Employment, Communication, Mobility, and Activities of Daily Living (ADL)**.

The KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes computer-based rehabilitation technologies along three functional axes:
- **Input Adaptation** — Alternative input modalities (e.g., switches, eye-gaze, sip-and-puff)
- **Output Adaptation** — Alternative output modalities (e.g., speech synthesis, Braille displays)
- **Software Adaptation** — Accessibility middleware, AAC software, cognitive orthotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *A computer in rehabilitation is a multipurpose electronic digital device whose I/O channels are reconstituted through adaptive hardware and software to compensate for sensorimotor, cognitive, or communicative impairments.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Universal Remote Control" Analogy**
>
> Imagine the modern computer as a **Swiss Army Knife**. For an able-bodied user, it is a productivity tool. For a person with a disability, the *same* computer becomes a **voice** (when they cannot speak), a **hand** (when they cannot grasp), a **pair of eyes** (when they cannot see), and a **memory assistant** (when cognitive load becomes overwhelming). The hardware stays identical; only the *human–machine interface* is rewired. In essence, **rehabilitation computing is the art of routing information around the broken pathways of the human body.**

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standards

- **Standard Refresh Rate** of typical visual displays: **60 Hz – 144 Hz**
- **Speech Synthesis Latency** acceptable for AAC: **< 200 ms**
- **Braille Display Cell Resolution**: **6-dot** or **8-dot** cells
- **Eye-Tracker Sampling Frequency**: **30 Hz – 1000 Hz**
- **Switch Activation Force (Light-touch)**: **0.1 N – 0.5 N**
- **WCAG 2.1 Compliance Level**: **AA** (minimum for rehabilitation contexts)
- **AAC Typing Rate (Target)**: **≥ 5 words per minute (WPM)**

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently test whether students understand the **difference between assistive technology and rehabilitation engineering** — assistive technology is the *tool*, rehabilitation engineering is the *system-level design discipline* that delivers it.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of a Rehabilitation Computer System

A rehabilitation-oriented computer system consists of **three coupled layers**:

1. **Core Computing Layer** — Standard CPU, RAM, storage, OS (Windows/macOS/Linux/Android).
2. **Adaptive I/O Layer** — Replaces or augments the keyboard, mouse, monitor, and speaker.
3. **Application Layer** — Specialized software (AAC, screen readers, cognitive trainers, VR rehab).

### 2.1.1 Input Adaptation Subsystem

| Input Device | Target Disability | Operating Principle |
|--------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Single Switch** | Severe motor impairment | Binary ON/OFF; scanning software iterates options |
| **Sip-and-Puff Pneumatic Switch** | Quadriplegia (C1–C4) | Air pressure differential → digital signal |
| **Head Tracker (IR/Ultrasonic)** | Limited hand control | Positional change of head → mouse cursor |
| **Eye-Gaze Tracker** | ALS, locked-in syndrome | Corneal reflection (Purkinje image) → fixation point |
| **Adaptive Keyboard** | Limited dexterity | Reduced key count, keyguard overlay, slowed repeat |
| **Voice Recognition Input** | Repetitive strain, blindness | MFCC features → HMM/DNN → text |

### 2.1.2 Output Adaptation Subsystem

| Output Device | Target Disability | Operating Principle |
|---------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Screen Reader (JAWS/NVDA)** | Blindness / low vision | TTS engine reads DOM/text buffer |
| **Refreshable Braille Display** | Deaf-blindness | Piezoelectric pins raise/lower dot patterns |
| **Speech Synthesizer** | Aphonia, post-laryngectomy | Text-to-Speech (TTS) pipeline |
| **AAC Speech-Generating Device (SGD)** | Non-verbal autism, cerebral palsy | Symbolic/icon-based language generation |
| **Large-Print / High-Contrast Display** | Low vision | OS-level magnification (e.g., 200% scaling) |

## 2.2 Theoretical Framework — Fitts's Law in Adaptive Input

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fitts's Law** is the foundational human–computer interaction model for evaluating the efficiency of an adaptive input device.

The law states that the **Index of Difficulty (ID)** of a target acquisition task is logarithmically related to the distance and size of the target:

$$
ID = \log_2\left(\frac{A}{W} + 1\right)
$$

Where:
- $ID$ = Index of Difficulty (in **bits**)
- $A$ = Distance from cursor to target centre
- $W$ = Width of the target along the axis of motion

The **Movement Time (MT)** is given by the linear prediction:

$$
MT = a + b \cdot ID = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{A}{W} + 1\right)
$$

Where $a$ and $b$ are empirically derived constants (in **seconds** and **seconds/bit** respectively). For a standard mouse, $a \approx 0.05$ s, $b \approx 0.1$ s/bit. For **eye-gaze trackers**, $b$ rises to **0.3 – 0.5 s/bit** because of the Midas-touch problem and dwell-time selection errors.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Fitts's Law allows a rehabilitation engineer to *quantify* the efficiency of an adaptive pointing device relative to a standard mouse — an important design metric that KTU examiners may ask you to compute.

## 2.3 Throughput Formulation for AAC

For **Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC)**, the rate at which a user can compose a message is modeled as:

$$
T = \frac{\text{Information Transmitted (bits)}}{\text{Time Elapsed (s)}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2(P_i^{-1})}{\Delta t}
$$

Where $P_i$ is the probability of the $i$-th symbol selected and $N$ is the number of selections. Average conversational AAC throughput is **5–15 WPM**, versus **150–200 WPM** for natural speech.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates all high-yield equations. Note the use of `\vert` instead of the vertical bar `|` to maintain markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Fitts's Index of Difficulty | $ID = \log_2\left(\frac{A}{W} + 1\right)$ | bits | Input device evaluation |
| 2 | Fitts's Movement Time | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{A}{W} + 1\right)$ | seconds | Task time prediction |
| 3 | Throughput (Information) | $T = \frac{\sum \log_2(P_i^{-1})}{\Delta t}$ | bits/s | AAC speed analysis |
| 4 | Effective Resolution | $R_{eff} = \frac{N_{pixels}}{M_{mag}}$ | pixels/cycle | Magnification design |
| 5 | Speech Intelligibility (CST) | $S_i = 1 - \frac{N_{errors}}{N_{words}}$ | dimensionless | TTS assessment |
| 6 | Switch Scan Rate | $f_s = \frac{1}{t_{dwell}}$ | Hz | Scanning AAC design |
| 7 | Eye-Tracker Accuracy | $\epsilon = \sqrt{(x_t - x_e)^2 + (y_t - y_e)^2}$ | degrees of visual angle | Gaze-based input |
| 8 | Shannon Entropy (Vocabulary) | $H = -\sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | bits/symbol | AAC vocabulary optimization |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **Index of Performance (IP)** with **Throughput (TP)**. Throughput is measured empirically in bits/s, while Index of Performance is the slope $b^{-1}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Shannon's Channel Capacity to AAC Throughput

This derivation shows how AAC message composition rate emerges from **information theory**.

**Step 1 — Define the Information Content of a Single Symbol**
By Shannon (1948), the self-information of a symbol $x_i$ drawn from alphabet $\mathcal{A}$ is:

$$
I(x_i) = -\log_2 P(x_i) \quad [\text{bits}]
$$

This row converts probability to log-domain information measure for a single utterance.

**Step 2 — Define the Average Information per Symbol (Entropy)**
For a memoryless source emitting $k$ distinct symbols, the expected information per symbol is the **Shannon Entropy**:

$$
H(\mathcal{A}) = -\sum_{i=1}^{k} P(x_i) \log_2 P(x_i) \quad [\text{bits/symbol}]
$$

This row aggregates the per-symbol self-information weighted by the symbol probabilities to obtain the average.

**Step 3 — Couple to Channel Throughput**
If a user takes $\Delta t$ seconds to emit $N$ symbols (including selection latency), the total information transmitted is:

$$
I_{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{N} I(x_j) = N \cdot H(\mathcal{A}) \quad \text{(if symbols are equiprobable)}
$$

This row applies the additive property of information across a sequence of $N$ selections.

**Step 4 — Express Throughput in Bits/Second**
By definition of channel capacity $C$:

$$
C = \frac{I_{total}}{\Delta t} = \frac{N \cdot H(\mathcal{A})}{\Delta t} \quad [\text{bits/s}]
$$

This row normalizes total information by elapsed time to yield the throughput.

**Step 5 — Convert to Words-Per-Minute for AAC Context**
Assuming an average English word length of $\overline{L} = 5$ characters and entropy per character $H_c \approx 1.5$ bits/char, the bits per word are $H_w = \overline{L} \cdot H_c$. Therefore:

$$
WPM = \frac{C \cdot 60}{H_w} = \frac{60 \cdot C}{5 \cdot 1.5} = 8 \cdot C
$$

This row performs the final unit conversion from bits/s to the KTU-friendly metric of WPM.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Fitts's Law for an Adaptive Head-Tracker

**Problem Statement:**
A rehabilitation engineer evaluates a head-tracking mouse against a standard mouse. The user must click a target of width $W = 80$ pixels located at a distance $A = 320$ pixels.

**Given Constants (Head Tracker):** $a = 0.18$ s, $b = 0.42$ s/bit
**Given Constants (Standard Mouse):** $a = 0.05$ s, $b = 0.10$ s/bit

**Step 1 — Compute the Index of Difficulty**

$$
ID = \log_2\left(\frac{A}{W} + 1\right) = \log_2\left(\frac{320}{80} + 1\right) = \log_2(4 + 1) = \log_2(5)
$$

Since $\log_2(5) \approx 2.3219$ bits, we have $ID \approx 2.32$ bits.

This row substitutes numerical values into the Fitts's Law ID formula.

**Step 2 — Movement Time for Standard Mouse**

$$
MT_{mouse} = 0.05 + 0.10 \cdot 2.3219 = 0.05 + 0.2322 = 0.2822 \; \text{s}
$$

This row plugs the standard mouse coefficients into the linear movement time equation.

**Step 3 — Movement Time for Head Tracker**

$$
MT_{head} = 0.18 + 0.42 \cdot 2.3219 = 0.18 + 0.9752 = 1.1552 \; \text{s}
$$

This row plugs the head tracker coefficients into the same model to obtain a slower movement time.

**Step 4 — Performance Ratio**

$$
\eta = \frac{MT_{mouse}}{MT_{head}} = \frac{0.2822}{1.1552} \approx 0.244
$$

This row computes the dimensionless efficiency ratio between the two modalities.

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation**
The head tracker is **~24.4% as fast as a standard mouse** for this task. This is *clinically acceptable* because the user with C4 tetraplegia would otherwise have **0% access** to a graphical interface. The rehabilitation gain is not raw speed — it is the *enablement of function*.

> [!TIP]
> KTU 14-mark questions often ask for both a numerical result **and** its clinical interpretation. Always end with a one-line design implication.

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Input Modalities

| Modality | Effective Throughput (bits/s) | Latency (ms) | Fatigue Index | Best Use Case |
|----------|-------------------------------|--------------|---------------|---------------|
| Standard Mouse | 30 – 45 | 16 | Low | Baseline |
| Head Tracker | 12 – 20 | 30 – 60 | Medium | High-level SCI |
| Eye-Gaze Tracker | 15 – 30 | 50 – 100 | Low | ALS, locked-in |
| Single-Switch Scanning | 4 – 10 | 200 – 800 | Low | Profound motor limitation |
| Voice Input (DNN) | 25 – 40 | 200 – 500 | Low | Visual impairment, RSI |
| Sip-and-Puff | 6 – 12 | 100 – 200 | Medium | Quadriplegia (C1–C3) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **fatigue index** is a qualitative ordinal scale: Low, Medium, High. KTU exam answers should never present a quantitative value for it without source citation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Rehabilitation Computer System

The diagram below captures the *signal-flow* and *information routing* of a rehabilitation computer system. It is intentionally abstracted from physical layout to satisfy the **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard** for complex systems.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph inputLayer[Adaptive Input Layer]
        A1[Single Switch] --> A2[Scanning Controller]
        A3[Sip-and-Puff Sensor] --> A2
        A4[Head Tracker IR] --> A5[Cursor Emulator]
        A6[Eye Gaze Sensor] --> A5
        A7[Adaptive Keyboard] --> A5
        A8[Voice Input Mic] --> A5
    end

    A2 --> B1[OS Event Bus]
    A5 --> B1
    A8 --> B1

    B1 --> C1[Core Computing Layer - CPU RAM OS]

    C1 --> D1[Application Software]
    D1 --> D2[Screen Reader]
    D1 --> D3[AAC Communicator]
    D1 --> D4[Cognitive Trainer]
    D1 --> D5[VR Rehab Engine]

    C1 --> outputLayer[Adaptive Output Layer]
    outputLayer --> E1[Refreshable Braille Display]
    outputLayer --> E2[Speech Synthesizer TTS]
    outputLayer --> E3[High Contrast Magnified Display]
    outputLayer --> E4[Environmental Control Bus]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The user's *physical intent* (a switch closure, a head motion, a gaze fixation, a phoneme) is captured in the **Adaptive Input Layer**, normalized into standardized OS events by the **Cursor Emulator / Scanning Controller**, processed by the **Core Computing Layer**, and finally re-materialized through the **Adaptive Output Layer** in a form the user can perceive.

## 4.2 Signal Processing Chain for a Voice-Activated Rehabilitation Input

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Acoustic Pressure - Microphone] --> F2[Anti-aliasing Filter]
    F2 --> F3[ADC Sampling fs 16 kHz]
    F3 --> F4[MFCC Feature Extraction]
    F4 --> F5[Acoustic Model DNN or HMM]
    F5 --> F6[Language Model Decoder]
    F6 --> F7[Text Command Output]
    F7 --> F8[OS Command Dispatcher]
    F8 --> F9[Wheelchair or Appliance Actuator]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** The **MFCC (Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficient)** stage mimics the human cochlea's logarithmic frequency resolution. This is why DNN-based speech recognizers are robust even in noisy rehabilitation environments (e.g., open hospital wards).

## 4.3 AAC Symbol-Selection State Machine

The state machine below shows how a row-column scanning AAC device navigates a dynamic symbol grid.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> RowScan: Switch Activated Row 1
    Idle --> RowScan: Switch Activated Row 2
    Idle --> RowScan: Switch Activated Row 3
    RowScan --> ColScan: Row Selected
    ColScan --> Speak: Column Selected
    Speak --> Idle: Symbol Synthesized
    ColScan --> RowScan: Timeout No Selection
    Speak --> RowScan: Next Symbol Requested
```

> [!TIP]
> The `stateDiagram-v2` syntax is preferred over `flowchart` when modeling **discrete-time selection logic** because Mermaid's compiler handles state labels more reliably than nested conditional arrows.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define an Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) device. List any two categories of AAC with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
AAC refers to methods, devices, and systems that supplement or replace natural speech/writing for individuals with communication impairments.

1. **Aided AAC** — Requires an external tool.
   *Example:* Picture Exchange Communication System (PECS), speech-generating tablet with Proloquo2Go.
2. **Unaided AAC** — Uses only the body.
   *Example:* Manual sign language, gestures, finger-spelling.

> [!NOTE]
> A 3-mark answer requires: (1) definition — 1 mark, (2) categories with examples — 2 marks.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is Fitts's Law? State its significance in the design of adaptive pointing devices for rehabilitation.**

**Model Answer:**
Fitts's Law quantifies the relationship between the **time required to move to a target** and the **distance-to-target ratio**.

$$
MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{A}{W} + 1\right)
$$

**Significance in Rehabilitation:**
- It allows **objective benchmarking** of alternative input devices (head tracker, eye-gaze, sip-and-puff) against the standard mouse.
- It helps clinicians **prescribe** an appropriate input device based on the user's motor capacity.
- It guides UI designers to **enlarge targets** and **reduce distances**, increasing accessibility for users with motor impairment.

> [!TIP]
> 3-mark answers should ideally include the **formula + one engineering use-case**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students must answer **one full question from each module**. Each 14-mark question carries sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — July 2024]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a computer-based rehabilitation system**. Discuss the role of **adaptive input, adaptive output, and application software layers**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The functional architecture of a computer-based rehabilitation system consists of three coupled layers:

**1. Adaptive Input Layer:**
This layer captures the user's intent through non-standard modalities. Examples include single switches (operated by any voluntary muscle), sip-and-puff transducers (for C1–C3 quadriplegia), head-tracking cameras, eye-gaze sensors (for ALS), and adaptive keyboards with keyguards. The signal is normalized to standard OS events (mouse clicks, keystrokes).

**2. Core Computing Layer:**
This contains the standard CPU, RAM, storage, and operating system. It runs accessibility middleware (e.g., Windows Accessibility API, AT-SPI on Linux) that allows the OS to communicate bidirectionally with assistive applications.

**3. Adaptive Output Layer:**
This layer re-materializes processed information in forms the user can perceive — refreshable Braille displays (tactile), speech synthesizers (auditory), high-contrast magnified displays (visual), and environmental control buses (actuator control).

**4. Application Software Layer:**
Specialized software implements the rehabilitation function:
- **AAC Communicators** (e.g., Proloquo, TouchChat)
- **Screen Readers** (JAWS, NVDA, VoiceOver)
- **Cognitive Trainers** (e.g., Lumosity, Constant Therapy)
- **VR Rehab Engines** (e.g., SaeboVR, MindMaze)

**Block Diagram (representative):**

```
USER → ADAPTIVE INPUT → OS EVENT BUS → CORE COMPUTING → APPLICATION SOFTWARE
                                                                ↓
                                  ADAPTIVE OUTPUT ← STANDARD OS I/O
```

**(b)** Compute the **Index of Difficulty (ID)** and **Movement Time (MT)** for an eye-gaze tracker used by an ALS patient, given that the target is 400 pixels away and 60 pixels wide. Assume $a = 0.20$ s and $b = 0.35$ s/bit. Compare the result with a standard mouse. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute ID**

$$
ID = \log_2\left(\frac{A}{W} + 1\right) = \log_2\left(\frac{400}{60} + 1\right)
$$

$$
ID = \log_2(6.667 + 1) = \log_2(7.667) \approx 2.939 \text{ bits}
$$

This row substitutes the given pixel values into Fitts's ID formula.

**Step 2 — MT for Eye-Gaze Tracker**

$$
MT_{eye} = 0.20 + 0.35 \cdot 2.939 = 0.20 + 1.0287 = 1.229 \text{ s}
$$

This row plugs the eye-gaze coefficients into the movement-time equation.

**Step 3 — MT for Standard Mouse (using $a = 0.05$, $b = 0.10$)**

$$
MT_{mouse} = 0.05 + 0.10 \cdot 2.939 = 0.05 + 0.2939 = 0.344 \text{ s}
$$

This row computes the baseline standard-mouse time using conventional coefficients.

**Step 4 — Performance Ratio**

$$
\eta = \frac{MT_{mouse}}{MT_{eye}} = \frac{0.344}{1.229} \approx 0.280
$$

This row expresses the eye-gaze tracker as a fraction of standard-mouse speed.

**Step 5 — Interpretation**
The eye-gaze tracker achieves only **~28% of standard-mouse speed**, but for an ALS patient with no voluntary muscle control below the eyes, it is the **only viable input modality**. The rehabilitation gain is the *enablement of function*, not the optimization of speed.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Describe in detail the **different categories of assistive input devices** used in rehabilitation computing. Provide suitable examples for users with motor, visual, and auditory disabilities. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Category 1 — Motor-Disability Input Devices:**
- **Single-Switch Scanning:** A binary switch operated by any voluntary muscle (e.g., a finger, toe, or eyebrow twitch). Scanning software cycles through options. *Example:* AbleNet JellyBean switch with Grid 3 software.
- **Head-Trackers:** IR or ultrasonic sensors track head movement. *Example:* TrackerPro by Madentec.
- **Sip-and-Puff Switches:** Pneumatic transducers for C1–C3 quadriplegia. *Example:* Origin Instruments Sip-and-Puff.
- **Eye-Gaze Trackers:** Infrared cameras detect corneal reflection. *Example:* Tobii Dynavox I-13/I-16.

**Category 2 — Visual-Disability Input Devices:**
- **Refreshable Braille Keyboards/Terminals:** Tactile input combined with tactile output.
- **Voice Input (Speech-to-Text):** *Example:* Dragon NaturallySpeaking.
- **High-Contrast / Tactile Keyguards:** For users with low vision.

**Category 3 — Auditory-Disability Input Devices:**
- **Visual Alert Systems:** Decoded text on-screen for telephone ring, doorbell, fire alarm.
- **Cued Speech Interfaces:** Hand-shape cues overlaid on lip-reading.
- **Real-Time Captioning Software:** *Example:* Otter.ai, Google Live Caption.

**Category 4 — Cognitive-Disability Input Devices:**
- **Simplified Interfaces:** Picture-based launchers reducing cognitive load.
- **Pictographic AAC Keyboards:** *Example:* Widgit symbols, PCS (Picture Communication Symbols).

**(b)** An AAC user takes 8 seconds to compose a 4-word message using a row-column scanning device. The vocabulary has 200 distinct symbols, each used with equal probability. Calculate the **information throughput in bits/s** and convert to **words per minute (WPM)**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**
