**Step 1 — Compute Symbol Entropy (Equiprobable Case)**

$$
H = \log_2(k) = \log_2(200) \approx 7.644 \text{ bits/symbol}
$$

This row applies the equiprobable-sources formula for Shannon entropy.

**Step 2 — Compute Total Information per Word (assume $\overline{L} = 5$ characters/word)**

$$
H_w = 5 \cdot H_{char}
$$

Assuming each character maps to one symbol in the AAC grid and $H_{char} = H$ (uniform case):

$$
H_w = 5 \cdot 7.644 = 38.22 \text{ bits/word}
$$

This row converts per-symbol information into per-word information using average English word length.

**Step 3 — Compute Total Information Transmitted**

$$
I_{total} = 4 \text{ words} \cdot 38.22 \text{ bits/word} = 152.88 \text{ bits}
$$

This row multiplies the per-word information by the number of words to get the message total.

**Step 4 — Compute Throughput in Bits/s**

$$
C = \frac{I_{total}}{\Delta t} = \frac{152.88}{8} = 19.11 \text{ bits/s}
$$

This row normalizes the total information by elapsed time.

**Step 5 — Convert to WPM**

$$
WPM = \frac{C \cdot 60}{H_w} = \frac{19.11 \cdot 60}{38.22} = \frac{1146.6}{38.22} = 30.0 \text{ WPM}
$$

This row converts the throughput to the more intuitive WPM metric.

**Step 6 — Interpretation**
A throughput of **19.11 bits/s** and **30 WPM** is well above the typical 5–15 WPM AAC baseline, indicating an efficient scanning setup. This performance is achievable only with optimized dwell times, predictive word completion, and frequent vocabulary pruning.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Module:**
> 1. **Forgetting Units** — Fitts's ID must be in *bits*, MT in *seconds*. Many students omit units, losing 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing AAC Categories** — Unaided (body only) vs. Aided (external tool). Aided is further split into **Low-Tech** (PECS boards) and **High-Tech** (SGD tablets).
> 3. **Skipping the Clinical Interpretation** — KTU 2024 scheme demands OBE-style *design justification*. A 7-mark sub-question without a concluding engineering/clinical interpretation typically loses **1.5–2 marks**.
> 4. **Using `|` inside markdown tables** — This breaks table parsing. Always use `\vert` or `\mid`.
> 5. **Omitting Step-Wise Derivations** — Examiners award partial marks for **each valid algebraic step**. Skipping a transition forfeits 0.5–1 mark per skipped step.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Computers in Rehabilitation):**
>
> - ☐ A rehabilitation computer system has **three layers**: Adaptive Input, Core Computing, Adaptive Output (+ Application Software).
> - ☐ **Fitts's Law** is the gold-standard HCI model: $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(A/W + 1)$.
> - ☐ Higher $b$ coefficient ⇒ slower input device (head-tracker, eye-gaze have larger $b$ than mouse).
> - ☐ **AAC** = Augmentative and Alternative Communication; categories: **Unaided / Aided (Low-Tech / High-Tech)**.
> - ☐ **SGD** (Speech-Generating Device) is the canonical high-tech AAC output.
> - ☐ **Screen Readers** (JAWS, NVDA) are software, not hardware. **Refreshable Braille Displays** are hardware output.
> - ☐ **Eye-gaze tracking** is suitable for **ALS / locked-in syndrome** because residual eye movement is preserved longest.
> - ☐ **Sip-and-puff** is suitable for **C1–C3 quadriplegia** (no hand, no head control).
> - ☐ **Single-switch scanning** has the *lowest throughput* (4–10 bits/s) but the *lowest motor demand*.
> - ☐ **WCAG 2.1 AA** is the minimum accessibility standard referenced in KTU syllabi.
> - ☐ **Information throughput formula** for AAC: $C = N \cdot H(\mathcal{A}) / \Delta t$ in **bits/s**.
> - ☐ Typical AAC throughput: **5–15 WPM** vs. natural speech **150–200 WPM**.
> - ☐ **Eye-tracker accuracy** $\epsilon$ is measured in **degrees of visual angle**, not pixels.
> - ☐ **Dwell-time selection** is the most common source of Midas-touch errors in gaze-based AAC.
> - ☐ **Predictive word completion** (e.g., T9, SwiftKey) can **double** AAC throughput.
> - ☐ **VR rehab** is a *software application layer*, not a separate I/O channel.
> - ☐ Always convert Fitts's Law outputs to **bits** and **seconds** before quoting.
> - ☐ Always close derivations with a **clinical / engineering interpretation line** for full OBE marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Web Accessibility: Conceptual Model: Telecommunications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT753 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Web Accessibility: Conceptual Model: Telecommunications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Web Accessibility — Formal Academic Definition

**Web Accessibility** refers to the inclusive practice of ensuring that websites, web applications, and digital content are designed and developed in such a way that **all users — including people with disabilities — can perceive, understand, navigate, and interact** with the web effectively, on an equal basis with others.

According to the **World Wide Web Consortium (W3C)**, the international body governing web standards, web accessibility is governed by the **Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG)** — currently at version **WCAG 2.2 (published October 2023)**. These guidelines are built upon four foundational principles, universally abbreviated as **POUR**:

| Principle | Expansion | Core Intent |
|---|---|---|
| **P** | Perceivable | Information must be presentable to users in ways they can perceive. |
| **O** | Operable | Interface components and navigation must be operable. |
| **U** | Understandable | Information and UI operation must be understandable. |
| **R** | Robust | Content must be robust enough to be interpreted by a wide variety of agents, including assistive technologies. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Conceptual Model of Web Accessibility describes the layered interaction between **user agents, assistive technologies, content, and the user**, and how **telecommunications** acts as the *bridge layer* that enables remote accessibility services (e.g., relay services, captioned telephony, real-time text) to function over packet-switched and circuit-switched networks.

## 1.2 The Conceptual Model — An Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **multi-stakeholder international phone call** between three people:
- **Person A (Speaker):** Speaks only in English.
- **Person B (Deaf Person):** Reads text and signs.
- **Person C (Translator/Relay Operator):** Translates between spoken English and written text/sign language.

Now imagine this call must work over **landline, mobile, satellite, and internet** — and Person B must *also* see captions, transcripts, and synchronized visual cues on their laptop.

This is exactly the problem **Web Accessibility: Conceptual Model: Telecommunications** addresses.

The web content (Person A's speech) must be made **perceivable** to a user with sensory impairments (Person B), using a **telecommunications channel** (the phone network) and **relay/translation services** (Person C). The model formalizes this interaction.

## 1.3 Telecommunications in the Web Accessibility Context

In rehabilitation engineering, telecommunications plays three critical roles in web accessibility:

1. **Transport Layer Enablement:** Carrying accessible content (audio descriptions, captions, sign language video) over IP/3G/4G/5G/ISDN/PSTN networks.
2. **Service Layer Mediation:** Services like **TTY/TDD**, **Video Relay Service (VRS)**, **Speech-to-Speech (STS)**, **IP Relay**, and **Real-Time Text (RTT)** translate between modalities (text↔voice↔sign).
3. **Architectural Layer Integration:** The **W3C Web Accessibility Initiative (WAI)** explicitly defines how user agents, AT (Assistive Technology), and the web interact through the **Accessibility API** layer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard to Remember:** **Section 508** of the U.S. Rehabilitation Act, **EN 301 549** (European harmonized standard), and the **Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 (India)** all mandate that public-facing telecommunications and web services be accessible.

## 1.4 Core Terminology Reference

- **Assistive Technology (AT):** Hardware/software that increases/maintains functional capabilities of a person with disability (e.g., screen reader JAWS, NVDA, VoiceOver).
- **User Agent (UA):** Software that retrieves and presents web content (e.g., browsers, media players).
- **Accessibility API:** A bridge between web content and AT (e.g., **MSAA, UIA, ARIA, AT-SPI**).
- **Teletypewriter (TTY) / Telecommunications Device for the Deaf (TDD):** Pre-internet text communication device operating over analog phone lines using **Baudot code at 45.45 baud**.
- **Real-Time Text (RTT):** IP-based character-by-character text transmission, replacing TTY in modern 4G/5G networks.
- **Video Relay Service (VRS):** Real-time video interpretation between sign language users and voice callers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standardized Transmission Speed for TTY:** **45.45 baud** (bits per second) — this is the legacy baseline metric you must know.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The W3C Conceptual Model of Web Accessibility

The W3C defines a layered conceptual model showing how web accessibility is achieved. The telecommunications layer sits *between* the user and the content.

### Layered Architecture (Top-Down):

1. **User Layer:** The end user with diverse abilities, context, and preferences.
2. **Assistive Technology Layer:** Screen readers, screen magnifiers, Braille displays, sip-and-puff switches, eye-trackers.
3. **User Agent Layer:** Browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari) and media players.
4. **Accessibility API Layer:** A standardized interface exposing content semantics (e.g., WAI-ARIA, AXAPI, MSAA/UIA, AT-SPI on Linux, VoiceOver on iOS).
5. **Content Layer:** The actual web resource (HTML, CSS, JavaScript, SVG, multimedia).
6. **Telecommunications / Transport Layer:** The physical and logical channel delivering the content — fiber, copper, wireless, satellite — and protocol stack (TCP/IP, SIP, RTP, WebRTC).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A common question asks you to *identify the layer* where a specific accessibility barrier occurs. For example, missing alt-text is a **Content Layer** failure; an inaccessible CAPTCHA is a **User Agent / AT Layer** failure; and slow streaming of sign language video is a **Telecommunications Layer** failure.

## 2.2 Telecommunications Channels Used in Accessibility

| Channel | Bandwidth (Typical) | Accessibility Service | Modality Conversion |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PSTN (Analog)** | ~3.4 kHz | TTY/TDD (45.45 baud) | Text↔Tones |
| **ISDN** | 64 / 128 kbps | Video conferencing for sign language | Video↔Video |
| **3G / 4G LTE** | 2 Mbps – 100 Mbps | VRS, captioned video, RTT | Sign↔Voice |
| **5G URLLC** | <1 ms latency | Remote robotic rehabilitation, tactile haptics | Multi-modal |
| **Wi-Fi (802.11ax)** | ~9.6 Gbps | Local screen reader streaming, IP-Relay | Text↔Text |
| **Satellite** | Variable (256 kbps – 100 Mbps) | Rural/remote deaf-blind communication | Multi-modal |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since this module involves signal-channel relationships for accessibility data streams, the following equations are the **high-yield formulas** you must memorize for the university exam.

$$
\boxed{C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)}
$$

**Shannon–Hartley Theorem** — the maximum error-free data rate $C$ (in bits/second) over a channel of bandwidth $B$ (Hz) with signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$.

$$
\boxed{R_{\text{TTY}} = 45.45 \text{ baud (bits/second)}}
$$

**TTY/TDD Baud Rate** — fixed transmission speed for legacy text telephony.

$$
\boxed{L_{\text{RTT}} = N_{\text{chars}} \cdot t_{\text{per-char}}}
$$

**Real-Time Text Latency** — where $L_{\text{RTT}}$ is end-to-end latency, $N_{\text{chars}}$ is character count, $t_{\text{per-char}}$ is per-character transmission time. Standard: each character delivered within **~300 ms**.

$$
\boxed{B_{\text{VRS}} \approx 384 \text{ kbps (CIF) to 2 Mbps (720p)}}
$$

**VRS Minimum Bandwidth** — for sign language video to be intelligible, frame rate must be ≥ **25 fps**, resolution ≥ **CIF (352×288)**.

$$
\boxed{M = 8 \cdot f_s \cdot n_b}
$$

**Audio Data Rate** for streaming accessibility audio (e.g., audio descriptions), where $f_s$ is sampling frequency (Hz), $n_b$ is bits per sample, $M$ is bit rate (bps).

$$
\boxed{t_{\text{download}} = \dfrac{F \cdot 8}{B \cdot \eta}}
$$

**File Transfer Time** — $F$ = file size (bytes), $B$ = bandwidth (bps), $\eta$ = channel efficiency factor ($\eta \approx 0.8$ for TCP/IP).

### Symbol Reference Table

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $C$ | Channel capacity (Shannon limit) | **bits per second (bps)** |
| $B$ | Bandwidth | **Hertz (Hz)** |
| $S/N$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (dimensionless) | **dB or unitless ratio** |
| $R$ | Transmission rate (TTY) | **baud** |
| $L$ | Latency | **milliseconds (ms)** |
| $M$ | Media bit rate | **bps** |
| $\eta$ | Protocol efficiency factor | **dimensionless (0–1)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used to design **accessible broadband networks**, dimension **captioning servers**, and verify **WCAG Success Criterion 2.2.1 (Timing Adjustable)** and **1.2.x (Time-based Media)**. A rehabilitation engineer uses Shannon's formula to verify that the bandwidth provisioned for a hospital's VRS terminal is sufficient for sign-language clarity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Telecommunications × Accessibility Mapping

| Accessibility Need | Standard (WCAG / IEEE / ITU) | Telecommunication Technology | Engineering Metric |
|---|---|---|---|
| Blind users accessing web | WCAG 1.1.1 (Non-text Content) | TTS-over-VoIP; AST (Audible Screen Reader) | Audio bit rate $M \geq 64$ kbps (MP3); Latency $\leq 200$ ms |
| Deaf users (telephony) | ITU-T V.18; TIA-825-A | TTY (45.45 baud), RTT | Character latency $\leq 300$ ms |
| Hard-of-hearing (video) | WCAG 1.2.2 (Captions Prerecorded) | DVB subtitles, WebVTT over DASH | Caption sync offset $\leq \pm 500$ ms |
| Deaf-blind users | WCAG 1.1.1 + 1.2.3 | TTY + Braille display; ASCII over PSTN | Refresh rate: 80 cells/sec refresh for Braille |
| Sign language users | WCAG 1.2.6 (Sign Language Prerecorded) | VRS; SIP/SDP/RTP; H.264/AVC | 384 kbps CIF / 2 Mbps 720p @ 25 fps |
| Speech-impaired users | WCAG 1.2.x | STS (Speech-to-Speech), IP-Relay | Voice clarity $\geq 16$ kHz sample |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Bandwidth Required for VRS Session

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numerical):**
A rehabilitation center is establishing a **Video Relay Service (VRS)** link between a deaf user (sign language) and a hearing party. The VRS must use **CIF resolution (352×288)** at **25 frames per second**, with **H.264 codec (compression ratio ≈ 50:1, equivalent quality factor 0.5 bits/pixel for moderate motion)**. Compute the minimum channel bandwidth required.

### Step 1: Compute Raw Uncompressed Video Data Rate

The frame size in pixels is:
$$
352 \times 288 = 101{,}376 \text{ pixels/frame}
$$

At 25 frames/second, raw pixel throughput is:
$$
101{,}376 \times 25 = 2{,}534{,}400 \text{ pixels/second}
$$

### Step 2: Apply Bits-per-Pixel Factor

For H.264 standard quality (0.5 bits/pixel for moderate motion sign-language content):
$$
R_{\text{raw}} = 2{,}534{,}400 \times 0.5 = 1{,}267{,}200 \text{ bps}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Protocol Overhead

Real-world IP networks using RTP/UDP have overhead $\approx 20\%$ (IP/UDP/RTP headers), so efficiency $\eta \approx 0.8$:
$$
B_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{R_{\text{raw}}}{\eta} = \dfrac{1{,}267{,}200}{0.8} = 1{,}584{,}000 \text{ bps} \approx 1.58 \text{ Mbps}
$$

### Step 4: Verify Against Shannon's Limit

Given the link has bandwidth $B = 2$ MHz (a typical DSL/4G channel) and $S/N = 1000$ (30 dB):
$$
C = 2 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(1 + 1000) = 2 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(1001)
$$

Compute the log term:
$$
\log_2(1001) = \dfrac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \dfrac{3.0004}{0.3010} \approx 9.967
$$

Therefore:
$$
C \approx 2 \times 10^{6} \times 9.967 = 19.93 \text{ Mbps}
$$

### Step 5: Capacity Adequacy Check

$$
C = 19.93 \text{ Mbps} \quad \geq \quad B_{\text{required}} = 1.58 \text{ Mbps} \quad \checkmark
$$

The channel is **adequate** for VRS, with surplus capacity $\Delta C = 18.35$ Mbps (≈ 91% idle), allowing concurrent screen-reader audio streaming and text relay.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 12.6× margin between Shannon capacity and VRS demand is why VRS can be deployed cheaply on residential broadband. This is the kind of *back-of-the-envelope verification* a KTU examiner expects in 14-mark problems.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: TTY Character Transmission Time

**Problem Statement:** A TTY device transmits at **45.45 baud** using **5-bit Baudot code with 1 start bit and 1.5 stop bits**. Compute the time to transmit a 100-character message and verify it meets the **300 ms per character** WCAG-aligned real-time text benchmark.

### Step 1: Bits per Character

$$
N_{\text{bits}} = 1 \text{ (start)} + 5 \text{ (data)} + 1.5 \text{ (stop)} = 7.5 \text{ bits/char}
$$

### Step 2: Time per Character

$$
t_{\text{per-char}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{bits}}}{R} = \dfrac{7.5}{45.45} = 0.165 \text{ seconds/char} = 165 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 3: Total Message Time

$$
T_{\text{msg}} = 100 \times 0.165 = 16.5 \text{ seconds}
$$

### Step 4: Benchmark Verification

$$
t_{\text{per-char}} = 165 \text{ ms} < 300 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark
$$

The TTY is **adequate** for real-time text (per legacy standard). This is why TTY remains the de facto fallback in emergency services for the deaf.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to include the **start and stop bits** in Baudot calculations, leading to an incorrect 110 ms/char answer. Always include framing overhead.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Web Accessibility Conceptual Model with Telecommunications Layer

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **W3C Conceptual Model extended with the Telecommunications Layer**, as specified in the KTU 2024 Rehabilitation Engineering syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userA[User With Disability]:::user --> atA[Assistive Technology Layer]:::at
    userB[User Without Disability]:::user --> uaB[Standard User Agent]:::ua
    atA --> uaA[Accessible User Agent]:::ua
    uaA --> apiA[Accessibility API WAI-ARIA]:::api
    apiA --> contentA[Web Content HTML CSS JS SVG Media]:::content
    uaB --> contentB[Web Content Same Content]:::content
    contentA --> transA[Telecommunications Transport Layer]:::telecom
    contentB --> transA
    transA --> physA[Physical Channel Fiber 4G 5G WiFi Satellite PSTN]:::channel
    transA --> servA[Service Mediation TTY RTT VRS IP-Relay Captions]:::service
    physA --> servA
    servA --> relayA[Relay Operator or AI Mediation Engine]:::relay
    relayA --> atB[Output Modality Sign Video Text Audio Haptic]:::output
    atB --> userA
    classDef user fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#d2691e,color:#000
    classDef at fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef ua fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef api fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef content fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef telecom fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#5b2c6f,color:#000
    classDef channel fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000
    classDef service fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    classDef relay fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#424242,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#dcedc8,stroke:#33691e,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: How a VRS Call Flows Through the Model

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant D as Deaf User Sign Language
    participant V as VRS Terminal
    participant T as Telecom Network IP 4G 5G
    participant R as Relay Operator CA
    participant H as Hearing Party Voice
    D->>V: Captures sign language video CIF
    V->>T: Encodes H264 streams over RTP
    T->>R: Routes video to certified interpreter
    R->>H: Voices message in real time
    H->>R: Voice reply
    R->>T: Streams sign language response
    T->>V: Returns video frames
    V->>D: Displays signs
    Note over V,R: Round trip target 800 ms end to end
    Note over D,H: All exchanges comply with WCAG 1.2.6 and 2.2.1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: AT Interaction with Web Content via Accessibility API

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Loading: User navigates
    Loading --> Parsing: User agent receives HTML
    Parsing --> AccessibilityTree: WAI ARIA roles exposed
    AccessibilityTree --> ScreenReaderActive: AT queries API
    ScreenReaderActive --> TTSOutput: Screen reader narrates
    ScreenReaderActive --> BrailleOutput: Refreshable Braille display
    TTSOutput --> Idle
    BrailleOutput --> Idle
    AccessibilityTree --> ErrorState: Missing alt text detected
    ErrorState --> Logging: WCAG 1.1.1 violation recorded
    Logging --> Idle
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation for KTU:** The state diagram shows that the **Accessibility API** is the *single point of contact* between the dynamic web content and the user's assistive technology. If this layer is improperly implemented (e.g., missing ARIA roles), even a perfect telecommunications layer cannot deliver accessibility.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**Define the POUR principles of web accessibility as per WCAG 2.2. Why are they relevant to rehabilitation engineering?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The POUR principles are the four foundational pillars of the **Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.2**, published by the W3C:
- **P — Perceivable:** Information and UI components must be presentable in ways users can perceive (e.g., alt-text for images, captions for video).
- **O — Operable:** Interface components and navigation must be operable (e.g., keyboard access, no time-limits where avoidable).
- **U — Understandable:** Information and UI operation must be understandable (e.g., readable text, predictable behaviour).
- **R — Robust:** Content must be robust enough to be interpreted reliably by a wide range of user agents and assistive technologies.

In **rehabilitation engineering**, POUR provides the engineering specification for designing **assistive technology interfaces**, **accessible rehabilitation software**, and **telerehabilitation platforms** that allow persons with disabilities to participate independently in digital healthcare, education, and employment.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**
**What is a Video Relay Service (VRS)? How does it leverage telecommunications to provide accessibility?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Video Relay Service (VRS)** is a telecommunications-based accessibility service that enables communication between **deaf or hard-of-hearing individuals who use sign language** and **hearing individuals who use voice**.

**Working Principle:**
1. The sign language user transmits live video over a **broadband IP network (4G/5G/Wi-Fi)** to a **certified sign language interpreter** (the relay operator).
2. The interpreter **voices** the signed message in real time to the hearing party over a **voice telephony channel (PSTN/VoIP)**.
3. The hearing party's spoken reply is then **converted into sign language** by the interpreter and streamed back as video to the deaf user.

VRS leverages **high-bandwidth packet-switched telecommunications**, **low-latency codecs (H.264, VP8)**, and **real-time transport protocols (RTP, SIP)** to deliver near-synchronous (≤ 800 ms round-trip) interpretation, making it a textbook example of **telecommunications-enabled accessibility**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model Paper)]**

**(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, describe the **W3C Conceptual Model of Web Accessibility**, explicitly identifying the role of the **Telecommunications layer**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rehabilitation clinic deploys a **Real-Time Text (RTT)** service over a 4G LTE network. Each character is encoded using **UTF-8 (variable, average 2 bytes = 16 bits)** and transmitted over a channel with **S/N = 1000** and bandwidth **B = 200 kHz**. Compute:
- (i) The Shannon channel capacity.
- (ii) The maximum number of characters per second that can be transmitted if we require **0.5 bits/character of error-correction overhead**.
- (iii) Verify whether this satisfies the WCAG-aligned RTT benchmark of **≤ 300 ms per character**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The W3C Conceptual Model of Web Accessibility is a layered architecture. From top to bottom:

1. **User Layer:** End users with diverse abilities, including those using wheelchairs, screen readers, or sip-and-puff devices.
2. **Assistive Technology Layer:** Devices and software like NVDA, JAWS, Braille displays, head pointers, and eye-trackers.
3. **User Agent Layer:** Web browsers, media players, and email clients that retrieve and render content.
4. **Accessibility API Layer:** Standardised bridges (MSAA, UIA, AT-SPI, **WAI-ARIA**) that expose semantic information about UI elements to assistive technologies.
5. **Content Layer:** The actual web resources — HTML, CSS, JavaScript, multimedia, SVG.
6. **Telecommunications Layer:** The **transport and service mediation layer** that delivers content and bridges modalities. This includes physical channels (fiber, copper, 4G/5G, Wi-Fi, satellite), protocols (TCP/IP, SIP, RTP), and services (TTY, RTT, VRS, IP-Relay, captions).

> The **Telecommunications layer** is critical because it enables **remote accessibility services** (e.g., a deaf person in a rural area accessing captioned video via satellite) and provides the **quality of service (QoS)** guarantees (latency, jitter, packet loss) needed for real-time sign language and screen-reader interaction. Without adequate bandwidth and low latency, even a perfectly coded accessible webpage becomes unusable.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Shannon Channel Capacity:**

Given $B = 200$ kHz $= 2 \times 10^{5}$ Hz and $S/N = 1000$:
$$
C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)
$$

$$
C = 2 \times 10^{5} \cdot \log_2(1 + 1000) = 2 \times 10^{5} \cdot \log_2(1001)
$$

$$
\log_2(1001) = \dfrac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \dfrac{3.0004}{0.3010} \approx 9.967
$$

$$
C \approx 2 \times 10^{5} \times 9.967 = 1.9934 \times 10^{6} \text{ bps} \approx 1.99 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**(ii) Effective Bits per Character:**

UTF-8 average = 16 bits/char. Add error-correction overhead 0.5 bits/char:
$$
N_{\text{eff}} = 16 + 0.5 = 16.5 \text{ bits/char}
$$

Maximum characters per second:
$$
R_{\text{char}} = \dfrac{C}{N_{\text{eff}}} = \dfrac{1.9934 \times 10^{6}}{16.5} \approx 120{,}812 \text{ chars/sec}
$$

**(iii) WCAG-RTT Benchmark Verification:**

Time per character:
$$
t_{\text{per-char}} = \dfrac{1}{R_{\text{char}}} = \dfrac{1}{120{,}812} \approx 8.28 \text{ microseconds/char} = 0.00828 \text{ ms}
$$

Since $0.00828 \text{ ms} \ll 300 \text{ ms}$, the channel **vastly exceeds** the WCAG-aligned RTT benchmark. **Result: ✓ Fully compliant.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often compute $\log_2(1001) \approx 10$ (off by 0.3%), which is acceptable. However, they **frequently forget to convert 200 kHz to $2 \times 10^5$ Hz**, leading to a 1000× error. Always check unit conversions in numerical problems. Also, remember to include **error-correction overhead** when stated in the problem — omitting it costs **1 mark**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**

**(a)** Explain the roles of **TTY/TDD**, **RTT**, and **VRS** in accessible telecommunications. Compare their **bandwidth requirements, latency targets, and use-cases** in a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A deaf-blind user accesses web content via a **refreshable Braille display** over a PSTN-based TTY link at **45.45 baud**. The display refreshes 80 cells of Braille. Compute:
- (i) The total bits per refresh cycle.
- (ii) The maximum refresh rate (refreshes per second).
- (iii) The end-to-end text latency for a 200-character message. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

| Feature | **TTY / TDD** | **RTT (Real-Time Text)** | **VRS (Video Relay Service)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Network** | PSTN (analog) | 4G / 5G / VoIP (IP) | Broadband IP (4G/5G/Wi-Fi) |
| **Bandwidth** | 45.45 baud (very low) | ~64 kbps (VoIP-grade) | 384 kbps – 2 Mbps |
| **Latency Target** | ~165 ms/char (legacy) | ≤ 300 ms/char (ITU-T T.140) | ≤ 800 ms round-trip |
| **Modality** | Text ↔ Tones | Text ↔ Text (real-time) | Sign Language ↔ Voice |
| **Use Case** | Emergency calls, landline text telephony | Modern smartphone emergency texting (e.g., 988 in US, 112 in EU) | Direct sign-language communication |
| **Standard** | ITU-T V.18; EIA/TIA-825 | 3GPP TS 26.114; IETF RFC 4103 | FCC VRS Rules; WCAG 1.2.6 |

> **Role Summary:** TTY is the **legacy text-telephony baseline**; RTT is its **IP-native successor** mandated in modern 4G/5G networks; VRS is the **high-bandwidth video interpretation service** for sign language users. All three exemplify the **Telecommunications layer's mediating role** in the web accessibility conceptual model.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Bits per Refresh Cycle:**

Each Braille cell = 8 dots = 8 bits (one bit per dot). 80 cells:
$$
N_{\text{bits/cycle}} = 80 \times 8 = 640 \text{ bits/cycle}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Refresh Rate:**

At 45.45 baud (45.45 bps):
$$
f_{\text{refresh}} = \dfrac{45.45}{640} = 0.0710 \text{ refreshes/second} = 71 \text{ mHz}
$$

Equivalent: $1$ refresh every $\dfrac{1}{0.0710} \approx 14.08$ seconds.

**(iii) End-to-End Latency for 200-character message:**

Time per character:
$$
t_{\text{char}} = \dfrac{7.5 \text{ bits}}{45.45 \text{ bps}} \approx 0.165 \text{ s/char}
$$

Total latency:
$$
L = 200 \times 0.165 = 33.0 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Discussion:** This 33-second latency explains why **TTY is unsuitable for modern web browsing** for deaf-blind users and why **IP-based Braille-over-IP (BoIP)** protocols (e.g., **IETF RFC 8822 — Tactile Internet**) are being developed in 5G networks to reduce this to <100 ms.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A common error is treating the **baud rate as 45.45 bps without accounting for the 7.5-bit framing** (1 start + 5 data + 1.5 stop). This leads to an incorrect 14.08-second-per-character value, losing 2 marks. Always show the framing-bit calculation explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- [ ] **POUR Principles** — Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust. (WCAG 2.2)
- [ ] **W3C Conceptual Model Layers** — User → AT → UA → Accessibility API → Content → **Telecommunications**.
- [ ] **TTY Baud Rate** = **45.45 baud**; framing = 1 start + 5 data + 1.5 stop bits = **7.5 bits/char**.
- [ ] **RTT Latency Benchmark** ≤ **300 ms/char** (ITU-T T.140 / IETF RFC 4103).
- [ ] **VRS Minimum Specs** = CIF (352×288) @ **25 fps**; ~384 kbps – 2 Mbps.
- [ ] **Shannon–Hartley Theorem** — $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — use to verify channel adequacy.
- [ ] **Accessibility APIs** — MSAA, UIA, AT-SPI, **WAI-ARIA** (web-specific).
- [ ] **Key Standards** — WCAG 2.2, Section 508, EN 301 549, **RPwD Act 2016 (India)**, ITU-T V.18 (TTY), 3GPP TS 26.114 (RTT).
- [ ] **WebRTC** is the modern browser-native real-time communication stack (audio, video, data) used for in-browser VRS and captioned calls.
- [ ] **Modality Conversion** = the *core function* of the telecom-mediation layer (text↔voice↔sign↔haptic).
- [ ] For **deaf-blind users**, Braille-over-IP is the emerging 5G solution; legacy TTY is too slow (33 s for 200 chars).
- [ ] **TCP/IP efficiency factor** $\eta \approx 0.8$ must be applied to bandwidth calculations.
- [ ] Always **state units** (bps, kHz, ms) in your numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.

> **Final Note for Students:** The 2024 KTU scheme emphasises **applied engineering analysis**. Don't just memorise POUR — be prepared to **map each principle to a specific telecommunication technology and quantify its performance** using Shannon's theorem, baud-rate math, and latency budgets. That's the difference between a 7-mark answer and a 14-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computers and the Internet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT753 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Computers and the Internet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computers and the Internet in Rehabilitation Engineering** refers to the integrated application of digital computing hardware, specialized software, network connectivity, and web-based platforms to assess, assist, compensate for, and restore functional capabilities of individuals with physical, cognitive, sensory, or communication impairments.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme for **PEBMT753 - Rehabilitation Engineering**, this topic constitutes a foundational pillar of *Assistive Technology (AT)* — specifically the *information-age branch* of rehabilitation that leverages Information and Communication Technology (ICT) for disability mitigation.

The **WHO ICF (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health)** framework classifies computer-based interventions under **Environmental Factors (Chapter 1: Products and Technology)**, while their functional outcomes are measured under **Activities and Participation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module expects students to understand *how computing augments human ability*, *accessibility principles*, *telerehabilitation architectures*, and *future trends* such as Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI) and IoT-enabled smart rehabilitation environments.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Brain, the Body, and the Bridge"

Imagine a person with limited mobility wanting to send an email. Without assistive technology, this is like trying to cross a wide river with no bridge — the **intention (brain)** is clear, but the **execution (body)** is blocked. A computer, equipped with specialized input/output devices, acts as the **bridge**: it translates intention into action.

- The **CPU (Central Processing Unit)** is the *planner* of the bridge.
- The **Operating System** is the *bridge manager*.
- The **Internet** is the *highway system* that lets the user reach the world beyond.
- **Assistive software** (screen readers, voice recognition) is the *personal translator* that makes the bridge usable for each individual.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic Device — "I-CARE"** for remembering the four pillars of computer-aided rehabilitation:
> - **I** — Input adaptations (switches, eye-trackers, sip-and-puff)
> - **C** — Communication aids (AAC, text-to-speech)
> - **A** — Accessibility (WCAG, screen readers)
> - **R** — Remote rehabilitation (telerehabilitation, IoT)
> - **E** — Education & Employment access (e-learning, telework)

---

## 1.3 Core Components — High-Yield Snapshot

| Component | Role in Rehabilitation | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardware** | Physical access | Trackballs, Braille displays, eye-gaze cameras |
| **Software** | Functional augmentation | JAWS, Dragon NaturallySpeaking, Proloquo2Go |
| **Operating System** | Platform accessibility | Windows Narrator, macOS VoiceOver, Android TalkBack |
| **Network / Internet** | Remote access | Telerehabilitation, online therapy portals |
| **Cloud Computing** | Data persistence & AI | Cloud-based Electronic Medical Records (EMR) |
| **Web Standards** | Universal usability | WCAG 2.1/2.2 compliance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - **WCAG Conformance Levels:** **A** (lowest) → **AA** (legal standard in most countries) → **AAA** (gold standard).
> - **Refresh Rate for Display Comfort:** **≥ 75 Hz** (below this causes flicker-induced eye strain).
> - **Minimum Font Size for Low Vision:** **18 pt** sans-serif (per RNIB guidelines).
> - **Touch Target Minimum:** **44 × 44 pixels** (Apple HIG / WCAG 2.5.5).
> - **Color Contrast Ratio:** **4.5:1** for normal text, **3:1** for large text (WCAG AA).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of Computer-Assisted Rehabilitation

The application of computing in rehabilitation follows a **five-layer hierarchical model**, mirroring the classic OSI/ISO network reference model used in computing curricula.

### Layer 1 — The Human Layer (User)
The end user with a disability. Their **functional profile** (motor, sensory, cognitive) determines every downstream design choice.

- **Motor impairments** → need alternative input methods.
- **Visual impairments** → need screen readers, magnification, Braille.
- **Hearing impairments** → need captions, visual alerts.
- **Cognitive impairments** → need simplified UIs, memory aids.

### Layer 2 — The Interface Layer (I/O Adaptation)
This is the bridge between human intent and machine logic.

- **Alternative Input Devices:**
  - *Switches* (single or dual) — for users with severe motor limitations.
  - *Joysticks, trackballs* — for users unable to use a mouse.
  - *Eye-gaze trackers* — for users with locked-in syndrome.
  - *Sip-and-puff controllers* — for users with quadriplegia.
  - *Head-tracking cameras* — for users with limited hand function.
  - *Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)* — EEG-based direct neural control.

- **Alternative Output Devices:**
  - *Refreshable Braille displays* (typically **40 or 80 cells**).
  - *Screen magnification software* (1.5× to 36× zoom).
  - *Text-to-Speech (TTS) engines* — naturalness measured in **MOS (Mean Opinion Score)** on a 1–5 scale.
  - *Augmentative symbols* — Picture Exchange Communication System (PECS).

### Layer 3 — The Computing Layer (Hardware + OS)
Standard computing hardware running accessible operating systems. Key specifications for rehabilitation-grade hardware:

- **RAM:** Minimum **8 GB** (16 GB recommended for AI-driven therapy software).
- **Storage:** SSD ≥ **256 GB** for fast boot and patient data access.
- **Processor:** Multi-core ≥ **2.0 GHz** for real-time signal processing.
- **GPU:** Required for VR-based motor rehabilitation (> **4 GB VRAM**).

### Layer 4 — The Application Layer (Software)
Specialized rehabilitation software falls into three families:

1. **Cognitive Rehabilitation Software** — Lumosity, Cogmed, Parrot Software.
2. **Motor Rehabilitation Software** — SaeboRehab, Bioness H200, MIT-Manus.
3. **Communication Software (AAC)** — Proloquo2Go, TouchChat, Tobii Dynavox.

### Layer 5 — The Network Layer (Internet & Cloud)
Enables **telerehabilitation**, **remote monitoring**, **cloud-stored patient records**, and **online support communities**.

---

## 2.2 Web Accessibility — The WCAG Framework

The **Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG)** are the global standard for digital inclusion. The current operative version is **WCAG 2.2 (published October 2023)**, and **WCAG 3.0** is in draft.

### The Four POUR Principles:

| Principle | Meaning | Implementation Example |
|---|---|---|
| **P**erceivable | Information must be presentable in ways users can perceive | Alt-text for images, captions for video, color-independent cues |
| **O**perable | UI components must be operable by all | Keyboard navigation, sufficient time limits, no seizure-inducing flashes (< **3 flashes/sec**) |
| **U**nderstandable | Information and UI must be understandable | Reading level ≤ **Grade 8**, consistent navigation, error prevention |
| **R**obust | Content must work with assistive technologies | Valid HTML, ARIA (Accessible Rich Internet Applications) labels |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Takeaway:** WCAG **AA** is the *de facto* legal requirement worldwide (Section 508 in the USA, EN 301 549 in the EU, RPwD Act 2016 in India). Always mention "WCAG 2.1 AA" unless the question specifies otherwise.

---

## 2.3 Telerehabilitation — The Engineered Pipeline

**Telerehabilitation** is the delivery of rehabilitation services over telecommunication networks. It expanded massively post-COVID-19 and is now a **core KTU 2024 topic**.

### The Process Flow:
1. **Patient Assessment** → at home with wearable sensors.
2. **Data Transmission** → via secure HTTPS / HL7 / FHIR protocols.
3. **Clinician Review** → real-time video (≥ **720p**, ≥ **30 fps**) or asynchronous review.
4. **Therapy Delivery** → VR-based exercises, app-based cognitive drills.
5. **Progress Monitoring** → dashboard with KPIs (Range of Motion, adherence %).

### KTU Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Bandwidth requirement for video** | $\text{BW} = \text{Resolution} \times \text{FPS} \times \text{Compression Ratio}$ | Sizing network for telerehab |
| **End-to-End Latency** | $T_{\text{e2e}} = T_{\text{proc}} + T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{prop}}$ | Quality of experience |
| **Packet Loss Impact** | $\text{PSNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{MAX}^2}{\text{MSE}}\right)$ | Video quality metric |
| **Sample Rate (Nyquist)** | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{\max}$ | EMG / biosignal sampling |
| **WCAG Contrast Ratio** | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{L_1 + 0.05}{L_2 + 0.05}$ | UI accessibility check |
| **Relative Luminance (sRGB)** | $L = 0.2126R + 0.7152G + 0.0722B$ | After gamma correction |
| **BCI Information Transfer Rate** | $\text{ITR} = \dfrac{\log_2 N + P \log_2 P + (1-P)\log_2\!\left(\frac{1-P}{N-1}\right)}{T}$ | bits/min — accuracy × speed × classes |
| **MOS (Mean Opinion Score)** | $\text{MOS} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i$ | TTS / audio quality |
| **Hamming Distance (Error Detection)** | $d_{\min} = \min(d(x,y))$ | Error-correcting codes in BCI |

> [!WARNING]
> **Avoid markdown table breakage:** When writing the contrast ratio inline, I use $\dfrac{L_1 + 0.05}{L_2 + 0.05}$ — never with vertical bars like $\vert L_1 + 0.05 \vert$ which would break the markdown parser.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Wheelchairs with Computer Vision:** Autonomous navigation using SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) — depth cameras + LiDAR.
- **Cochlear Implants with Bluetooth:** Direct audio streaming from computers/phones.
- **Smart Prosthetics:** Microprocessor-controlled limbs with smartphone calibration apps.
- **VR Exposure Therapy for PTSD:** Computer-generated controlled environments.
- **AI-Powered Gait Analysis:** Markerless motion capture using standard webcams.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Web Content Accessibility Guideline (WCAG) Contrast Ratio

The contrast ratio between two colors is the foundation of perceptibility. Let us derive it step by step.

### Step 1: Define Relative Luminance
For each sRGB color channel, first linearize the 8-bit value.

$$C_{\text{linear}} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{C_{\text{sRGB}}}{12.92}, & C_{\text{sRGB}} \leq 0.03928 \\[6pt] \left(\dfrac{C_{\text{sRGB}} + 0.055}{1.055}\right)^{2.4}, & C_{\text{sRGB}} > 0.03928 \end{cases}$$

**Logic:** The sRGB transfer function is *piecewise* — a linear segment for very dark values and a power-law (gamma) segment for the rest. This matches how human eyes perceive brightness.

### Step 2: Compute Relative Luminance
Apply the **Rec. 709** weighting to the linearized channels:

$$L = 0.2126 \cdot R_{\text{linear}} + 0.7152 \cdot G_{\text{linear}} + 0.0722 \cdot B_{\text{linear}}$$

**Logic:** Human vision is most sensitive to green, then red, then blue. The coefficients sum to **1.0** (conservation of energy).

### Step 3: Calculate Contrast Ratio
Let $L_1$ be the lighter and $L_2$ the darker luminance. The contrast ratio is:

$$\text{CR} = \dfrac{L_1 + 0.05}{L_2 + 0.05}$$

**Logic:** The constant **0.05** accounts for ambient light and the minimum displayable black level. This ensures the formula models real-world viewing conditions.

### Worked Numerical Example
**Problem:** Check whether body text in color `#FFFFFF` (white) on background `#3366CC` passes WCAG AA.

**Step A — Normalize the hex to 0–1 sRGB:**
- White: $C_{\text{sRGB}} = 1.0$ → linear $= 1.0$ (uses power branch, $1.0^{2.4} \approx 1.0$).
- Blue (`#3366CC`): R=0x33/255=0.2, G=0x66/255=0.4, B=0xCC/255=0.8.

**Step B — Linearize each channel of `#3366CC`:**
- R: $(0.2 + 0.055)/1.055 = 0.2417$, then $0.2417^{2.4} = 0.0331$.
- G: $(0.4 + 0.055)/1.055 = 0.4313$, then $0.4313^{2.4} = 0.1329$.
- B: $(0.8 + 0.055)/1.055 = 0.8104$, then $0.8104^{2.4} = 0.6038$.

**Step C — Luminance of background:**
$$L_2 = (0.2126)(0.0331) + (0.7152)(0.1329) + (0.0722)(0.6038) \approx 0.0070 + 0.0950 + 0.0436 = 0.1456$$

**Step D — Luminance of white text:** $L_1 = 1.0$.

**Step E — Compute Contrast Ratio:**
$$\text{CR} = \dfrac{1.0 + 0.05}{0.1456 + 0.05} = \dfrac{1.05}{0.1956} \approx 5.37:1$$

**Step F — Verdict:** Since **5.37 ≥ 4.5**, this combination **passes WCAG AA for normal text**. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** If the question asks for AA *Large Text*, the threshold drops to **3:1** (where large text is defined as ≥ **18 pt** or ≥ **14 pt bold**).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Brain-Computer Interface Information Transfer Rate (ITR)

BCI performance is quantified by the **Wolpaw ITR formula**. Let us derive it from first principles.

### Step 1: Define the Decision Space
Suppose the BCI classifies user intent into **N** possible classes. Maximum entropy (uncertainty) in bits is:

$$H_{\max} = \log_2 N$$

**Logic:** Each class is equally probable in the worst case.

### Step 2: Account for Accuracy
If the BCI achieves a per-trial accuracy **P** (e.g., 0.85), the actual information conveyed is reduced. The corrected information is:

$$B = \log_2 N + P \log_2 P + (1-P) \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{1-P}{N-1}\right)$$

**Logic:**
- First term: max possible information.
- Second term: Shannon's entropy penalty for correct classifications being *probabilistic* rather than *certain*.
- Third term: penalty for misclassifications (all N−1 wrong classes are equally likely).

### Step 3: Convert to Rate
If each decision takes **T** minutes (typically 1–4 seconds), the ITR in **bits per minute** is:

$$\text{ITR} = \dfrac{\log_2 N + P \log_2 P + (1-P) \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{1-P}{N-1}\right)}{T}$$

### Worked Numerical Example
**Problem:** A P300 speller BCI has 36 characters, average accuracy 90%, and a trial duration of 2 seconds. Find ITR.

**Step A:** $N = 36$, $P = 0.90$, $T = 2 \text{ sec} = \dfrac{2}{60} = 0.0333 \text{ min}$.

**Step B — Compute $\log_2 36$:**
$$\log_2 36 = \log_2(4 \times 9) = 2 + 2\log_2 3 \approx 2 + 2(1.585) = 5.170 \text{ bits}$$

**Step C — Compute the penalty term:**
$$P \log_2 P = 0.90 \times \log_2 0.90 = 0.90 \times (-0.152) = -0.137$$

$$(1-P) \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{1-P}{N-1}\right) = 0.10 \times \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{0.10}{35}\right) = 0.10 \times \log_2(0.00286) = 0.10 \times (-8.452) = -0.845$$

**Step D — Effective bits per trial:**
$$B = 5.170 - 0.137 - 0.845 = 4.188 \text{ bits/trial}$$

**Step E — ITR in bits per minute:**
$$\text{ITR} = \dfrac{4.188}{0.0333} \approx 125.6 \text{ bits/min}$$

**Step F — Practical meaning:** This corresponds to roughly **23 characters per minute** (using 5.5 bits/char ASCII).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A healthy typist achieves ~**250 bits/min**. Cutting-edge BCIs (e.g., Synchron Stentrode, Neuralink) aim to approach this benchmark. The BCI is the *only* non-muscular communication channel for locked-in patients.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Assistive Computer Setup

| Impairment Type | Best Input | Best Output | Best Software | Internet Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Quadriplegia** | Sip-and-puff, eye-gaze, BCI | Speech synthesis | Tobii Dynavox, Grid 3 | Social media, email |
| **Blind / Low Vision** | Standard keyboard, Braille input | Refreshable Braille, screen reader | JAWS, NVDA, VoiceOver | Screen-reader-friendly websites |
| **Deaf / Hard of Hearing** | Standard input | Visual alerts, captions | Otter.ai, Live Transcribe | Video calls with captions |
| **Cognitive impairment** | Simplified touch UI | Pictograms, audio cues | Proloquo4Text, Choiceworks | Guided e-learning |
| **Amputation** | Voice, EMG-based | Haptic feedback, visual | Dragon, Myo scripts | Telehealth consultations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always justify technology selection using the **HAAT (Human Activity Assistive Technology)** model — Human, Activity, Assistive Technology, Context. This is the *gold standard* KTU expects for Part B answers.
