# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) and list three rehabilitation applications.
> `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set B]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) is a direct communication pathway between the brain's electrical activity and an external device, bypassing the normal neuromuscular output channels. **Rehabilitation applications include:**
1. **Communication** for locked-in syndrome patients (e.g., P300 speller).
2. **Motor restoration** — stroke rehabilitation via motor-imagery-driven robotic exoskeletons.
3. **Neurofeedback therapy** for ADHD, epilepsy, and anxiety disorders.
4. **Control of assistive devices** such as smart wheelchairs and prosthetic limbs.

---

### Question 2: What is WCAG? State the four guiding principles.
> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG)** are internationally recognized technical standards developed by the **W3C (World Wide Web Consortium)** to make web content accessible to people with disabilities. The four guiding principles are summarized as **POUR**:
1. **P**erceivable — Information is presentable in perceivable ways.
2. **O**perable — UI components and navigation must be operable.
3. **U**nderstandable — Information and UI operation must be understandable.
4. **R**obust — Content must be robust enough to be interpreted by a wide variety of user agents, including assistive technologies.
The current standard is **WCAG 2.2** (October 2023) with three conformance levels: **A**, **AA**, and **AAA**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A: Telerehabilitation Architecture
> `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set A]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **telerehabilitation system**. Discuss the roles of **HL7/FHIR standards** and **cloud computing** in such a system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A clinician conducts a 30-minute video consultation with a stroke patient. The video stream is 1280×720 pixels at 30 fps, compressed at a ratio of 50:1. Calculate the **bandwidth required** in Mbps. Also, list **four (4) challenges** of telerehabilitation in rural India. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Architecture (5 marks for diagram + explanation):**

1. **Patient Side (Home):** Wearable sensors (IMU, EMG, ECG), camera, microphone, tablet/PC. Patient performs prescribed exercises; data is captured locally.
2. **Communication Layer:** Encrypted transmission over broadband/4G/5G. Uses **TLS 1.3** for security, **RTP/RTSP** for real-time video, and **HL7 FHIR** for clinical data.
3. **Cloud Server:** Receives and stores data, runs AI analytics, hosts the Electronic Medical Record (EMR).
4. **Clinician Side (Hospital/Clinic):** Dashboard showing real-time vitals, exercise adherence, and video feed. Prescribes next session and writes back to the patient's record.

**Role of HL7/FHIR (1 mark):**
**HL7 FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources)** is the modern data-exchange standard. It uses RESTful APIs and JSON/XML, enabling different vendors' rehabilitation devices and EMR systems to interoperate seamlessly — critical for portable, lifelong patient records.

**Role of Cloud Computing (1 mark):**
Cloud computing provides **scalable storage**, **AI-driven analytics** (e.g., motion-quality scoring), **redundancy**, and **24/7 access** from any device. It eliminates the need for expensive on-premise servers in every clinic.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Bandwidth Calculation (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Uncompressed data per frame:**
$$\text{Size}_{\text{frame}} = 1280 \times 720 \times 3 \text{ bytes (RGB)} = 2,764,800 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Uncompressed data per second:**
$$\text{Size}_{\text{sec}} = 2,764,800 \times 30 = 82,944,000 \text{ bytes/s} = 82.944 \text{ MB/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to megabits per second (uncompressed):**
$$82.944 \text{ MB/s} \times 8 = 663.55 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 4 — Apply compression ratio of 50:1:**
$$\text{Bandwidth}_{\text{compressed}} = \dfrac{663.55}{50} \approx 13.27 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Verdict:** A minimum **15 Mbps** connection is recommended (with overhead buffer).

**Four Challenges in Rural India (3 marks — 0.75 each):**
1. **Poor internet connectivity** — low bandwidth, frequent dropouts in rural belts.
2. **Digital literacy gap** — elderly patients struggle with app-based interfaces.
3. **Power instability** — inconsistent electricity disrupts sessions and damages devices.
4. **Language and cultural barriers** — most apps are English-only, ignoring regional dialects.
5. *(Bonus)* Lack of **affordable devices** and **insurance coverage** for telerehab.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to multiply by **8** to convert bytes to bits, or skip the compression step. Either error costs 1–2 marks. Always **state the unit at every step** and **box the final answer**.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): Web Accessibility & Assistive Software
> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]`

**(a)** Explain the **WCAG 2.1 guidelines** in detail. Describe how a screen reader (e.g., JAWS/NVDA) renders a web page to a visually impaired user. List the keyboard shortcuts used for navigation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A college website uses body text in color `#000000` (pure black) on a background of `#F2F2F2` (very light gray). Calculate the **WCAG contrast ratio** and determine whether it passes **WCAG AA for normal text** and **WCAG AAA for normal text**. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**WCAG 2.1 Guidelines (3 marks):**
WCAG 2.1 has **three conformance levels** (A, AA, AAA) organized under **four principles (POUR)**:
- **Level A (Essential):** Alt-text for images, captions for prerecorded video, keyboard access, no keyboard traps.
- **Level AA (Legal Standard):** Color contrast ≥ 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text, resize up to 200%, multiple ways to find pages.
- **Level AAA (Gold):** Contrast ≥ 7:1, sign language for audio, no timing for reading.

**Screen Reader Working (3 marks):**
1. The browser exposes the **DOM (Document Object Model)** and **accessibility tree** (ARIA).
2. JAWS/NVDA reads the **semantic structure** (headings, lists, landmarks) and announces each element.
3. It uses the system's **Text-to-Speech (TTS)** engine (e.g., Microsoft OneCore voices) to vocalize text.
4. It supports **virtual buffers** for fast navigation.

**Keyboard Shortcuts (1 mark — list any 4):**
- `H` — next heading
- `Tab` — next focusable element
- `Insert + F7` (JAWS) — element list
- `K` (NVDA) — next link
- `Ctrl + Home` — top of page
- `Alt + Down` — open combobox

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Normalize hex values to 0–1 sRGB:**
- Text `#000000`: R=G=B=0.
- Background `#F2F2F2`: R=G=B=242/255 ≈ 0.9490.

**Step 2 — Linearize the background (text is black, no need to linearize):**
Since 0.9490 > 0.03928, use the power branch:
$$C_{\text{linear}} = \left(\dfrac{0.9490 + 0.055}{1.055}\right)^{2.4} = (0.9512)^{2.4} \approx 0.8885$$

**Step 3 — Background luminance (all three channels are equal):**
$$L_2 = 0.2126(0.8885) + 0.7152(0.8885) + 0.0722(0.8885) = 0.8885$$

**Step 4 — Text luminance (pure black):**
$$L_1 = 0$$

**Step 5 — Contrast Ratio:**
$$\text{CR} = \dfrac{L_1 + 0.05}{L_2 + 0.05} = \dfrac{0 + 0.05}{0.8885 + 0.05} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.9385} \approx 0.0533$$

**WAIT — this is less than 1! That's because the formula gives (lighter + 0.05) / (darker + 0.05), and we need to identify lighter vs. darker correctly.** Let me re-order:

$$L_{\text{lighter}} = 0.8885, \quad L_{\text{darker}} = 0$$
$$\text{CR} = \dfrac{0.8885 + 0.05}{0 + 0.05} = \dfrac{0.9385}{0.05} = 18.77:1$$

**Step 6 — Verdict (1 mark):**
- **WCAG AA for normal text (threshold 4.5:1):** 18.77 ≥ 4.5 → **PASSES** ✓
- **WCAG AAA for normal text (threshold 7:1):** 18.77 ≥ 7 → **PASSES** ✓
- Even **WCAG AAA Enhanced (threshold 4.5:1 for large text) → not applicable** for normal text.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often invert the numerator and denominator. **Rule of thumb:** Always place the *lighter* luminance in the numerator. Also, dark mode designs that look stylish to sighted users may fail accessibility for users with **photophobia** — keep this caveat in mind for design questions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU 2024 Valuation for "Computers and the Internet":**
> 1. **Confusing WCAG versions** — writing "WCAG 3.0" as the operative standard. *Correct answer: WCAG 2.2 is current; WCAG 3.0 is in draft.*
> 2. **Forgetting units** — bandwidth in MB/s vs Mbps; sample rate in Hz vs kHz.
> 3. **Omitting the linearization step** in contrast-ratio problems — a direct subtraction of hex values will yield wrong answers.
> 4. **No diagrams in 14-mark questions** — KTU mandates at least one block diagram for ≥ 7 marks; expect a **2-mark penalty** if missing.
> 5. **Using informal terms** like "screen reader app" — use precise terms like "JAWS (Job Access With Speech)" and "NVDA (NonVisual Desktop Access)".
> 6. **Skipping Indian context** — for 14-mark answers, always mention **RPwD Act 2016** and **Digital India initiatives** for a contextual edge.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and stick it on your study wall — these are the 25 high-yield facts that recur in KTU exams.**

- ✅ **WCAG** is by **W3C**; current version **2.2** (2023); levels **A, AA, AAA**; principles **POUR**.
- ✅ **WCAG AA** requires **4.5:1** contrast for normal text and **3:1** for large text.
- ✅ **JAWS** is paid (Freedom Scientific); **NVDA** is free and open-source.
- ✅ **VoiceOver** is built into macOS/iOS; **Narrator** into Windows; **TalkBack** into Android.
- ✅ **Refreshable Braille displays** typically have **40 or 80 cells**.
- ✅ **Bionic eyes** (e.g., Argus II) restore limited vision via retinal electrode arrays.
- ✅ **BCI ITR** formula accounts for **N classes, accuracy P, trial time T** — gives **bits/min**.
- ✅ **HL7 FHIR** is the modern healthcare data-exchange standard; uses **RESTful JSON/XML**.
- ✅ **P300 speller** lets users type by focusing on flashing letters; ~**20–30 char/min**.
- ✅ **Telerehabilitation** saw 60–80% adoption growth post-COVID-19.
- ✅ **RPwD Act 2016** (India) mandates accessibility in all ICT for persons with benchmark disabilities (≥ **40%**).
- ✅ **Section 508** (USA) and **EN 301 549** (EU) are equivalents of WCAG in law.
- ✅ **Touch target minimum** is **44 × 44 px** (Apple HIG) and **48 × 48 dp** (Material Design).
- ✅ **Flashing content** must flash **< 3 times/sec** to avoid photosensitive seizures.
- ✅ **Closed captions** must support **user-selectable color, size, and position** (WCAG 1.2.2, 1.4.7).
- ✅ **Sample rate for biosignals** must obey **Nyquist**: $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{\max}$.
- ✅ **Cognitive rehabilitation software**: Lumosity, Cogmed, Parrot Software.
- ✅ **AAC apps**: Proloquo2Go, TouchChat, Grid 3, CoughDrop.
- ✅ **Eye-gaze trackers** work at ~**60–120 Hz** with accuracy of **0.5–1°** of visual angle.
- ✅ **MOS (Mean Opinion Score)** for TTS: 1 (bad) to 5 (excellent); modern neural TTS achieves **> 4.0**.
- ✅ **Smart home IoT** uses **Zigbee, Z-Wave, BLE, Wi-Fi** for sensor interconnectivity.
- ✅ **Synchronous** telerehab = real-time video; **Asynchronous** = store-and-forward (e.g., recorded exercise videos).
- ✅ **VR rehabilitation** uses **Oculus Quest, HTC Vive, Microsoft HoloLens 2** for motor and cognitive therapy.
- ✅ **Cybersecurity** in telerehab must comply with **HIPAA** (USA) / **DISHA** (India proposed) / **GDPR** (EU).
- ✅ **Universal Design** (Ronald Mace, 1997) is the *philosophical foundation* of all computer accessibility work.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technology Solutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT753 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Technology Solutions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Technology Solutions (in Rehabilitation Engineering)** refers to the integrated application of engineered devices, software systems, and adaptive hardware designed to **restore, augment, or substitute** functional capabilities of individuals with physical, sensory, cognitive, or neurological impairments. As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme** and aligned with the **WHO ICF (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health)** framework, technology solutions span the entire spectrum of **Assistive Technology (AT)**, **Rehabilitation Robotics**, **Sensory Substitution Systems**, **Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC)**, and **Universal Design implementations**.

In the context of the **PEBMT753 syllabus (Module 4)**, technology solutions are categorized along two primary axes:

1. **Functional Domain Axis** — Mobility, Manipulation, Communication, Cognition, and Sensory rehabilitation.
2. **Engineering Architecture Axis** — Mechanical, Electronic, Bioelectrical, Computer-based, and Hybrid systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome (CO) Mapping for KTU 2024:**
> - **CO4 (Module 4):** *"Apply engineering principles to design and evaluate technology-based rehabilitation solutions for persons with disabilities."*
> - **Bloom's Level:** Apply (Level 3) → Analyze (Level 4) → Evaluate (Level 5).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Body as a Smart City"**
>
> Imagine the human body as a **smart city** with a power grid (nervous system), road network (musculoskeletal system), communication towers (sensory organs), and service infrastructure (organs). When an **earthquake (disability/impairment)** damages a part of this city — say, a collapsed bridge (amputation) or a damaged power line (spinal cord injury) — the city cannot simply be abandoned. Instead, **engineers step in to build detours, backup generators, and new communication relays**.
>
> **Technology solutions are these engineering interventions**:
> - A **prosthetic leg** is a detoured bridge.
> - A **powered wheelchair** is a backup vehicle.
> - A **Cochlear implant** is a backup communication tower.
> - A **screen reader** is a translated road sign.
>
> The goal is **not to "fix" the person**, but to **engineer an environment + device ecosystem** that allows the person to fully participate in society — this is the philosophical core of the **Social Model of Disability**, which underpins modern rehabilitation engineering.

---

## 1.3 Core Categories of Technology Solutions (KTU Module 4 Syllabus)

> [!NOTE]
> The following taxonomy is **directly derived from the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 syllabus** and is the **standard classification** used in KTU question papers.

| # | Category | Target Impairment | Engineering Sub-Discipline |
|---|----------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Mobility Aids** | Locomotor disability | Biomechanics, Mechatronics |
| 2 | **Prosthetics** | Amputation (limb loss) | Materials, CAD/CAM |
| 3 | **Orthotics** | Musculoskeletal weakness/deformity | Biomechanics, Polymer Engg. |
| 4 | **Sensory Aids** | Visual/Hearing impairment | Signal Processing, Electronics |
| 5 | **AAC Devices** | Speech/language impairment | HCI, Linguistics |
| 6 | **Rehabilitation Robotics** | Paralysis, Stroke, CP | Robotics, Control Systems |
| 7 | **Environmental Control Systems (ECS)** | Severe physical disability | Embedded Systems, IoT |
| 8 | **Computer Access Tech** | Motor/Sensory disability | Software Engineering, HCI |

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants and metrics are **frequently tested** in KTU examinations and must be memorized:

- **Standard human body mass (adult, for prosthesis design):** **70 kg (154 lbs)**
- **Normal walking cadence:** **~100 steps/min** (range: 90–120)
- **Gait cycle duration:** **~1.0 s** (two strides)
- **Average step length:** **0.6–0.8 m**
- **Maximum voluntary grip force (adult male):** **~400 N**
- **Maximum bite force:** **~700 N** (molars)
- **Visual acuity standard (normal):** **6/6 (20/20)** — corresponds to a **resolving angle of 1 arc-minute (1/60° ≈ 0.00029 rad)**
- **Audible frequency range (human ear):** **20 Hz – 20,000 Hz**
- **Speech frequency range (intelligibility band):** **300 Hz – 3,400 Hz**
- **Skin-electrode impedance (standard ECG/EMG):** **1–5 kΩ** (target after gel preparation)
- **Standard battery voltage for assistive devices:** **12 V DC, 24 V DC** (lead-acid/lithium)
- **Wheelchair standard (ISO 7176):** **Max slope: 8° (1:7 gradient)** for manual; **10°** for powered
- **Standard wheelchair seat width:** **16, 18, 20 inches** (406, 457, 508 mm)

---

## 1.5 The ICF Framework Alignment

> [!NOTE]
> The **WHO ICF Model** is the **theoretical backbone** of Module 4. Every technology solution must be evaluated against this framework:

$$\text{Disability (per ICF)} = f(\text{Body Functions}, \text{Activities}, \text{Participation}, \text{Environmental Factors}, \text{Personal Factors})$$

The **engineering goal** is to **modify Environmental Factors (EF)** using technology, which in turn enables **Activities and Participation** despite the **Body Function** impairment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Technology Solution (5-Layer Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every assistive technology system, regardless of complexity, can be decomposed into **5 engineering layers**. This decomposition is a **favourite 7-mark KTU question** pattern.

### Layer 1 — **Input / Sensing Layer**
Captures the user's intent (biological signal) or environmental state.

- **Biological inputs:** EMG (electromyography), EEG (electroencephalography), EOG (electrooculography), ECG, force/pressure.
- **Physical inputs:** Joystick, sip-and-puff, switch, head-tracking, eye-tracking, voice.
- **Sensors:** Strain gauge, accelerometer, gyroscope, encoder, force-sensing resistor (FSR).

### Layer 2 — **Signal Conditioning & Processing Layer**
Amplifies, filters, and digitizes the raw sensor input.

- **Amplification:** Differential amplifier (CMRR > 80 dB).
- **Filtering:** Bandpass (e.g., 20–500 Hz for EMG).
- **ADC:** Sampling rate (Nyquist) ≥ **2 × highest signal frequency**.

$$\boxed{f_{\text{Nyquist}} \geq 2 \cdot f_{\text{max signal}}}$$

- **Feature extraction:** RMS, MAV (Mean Absolute Value), zero-crossing rate (for EMG).

### Layer 3 — **Decision / Control Layer**
Translates processed signals into commands.

- **Control strategies:** Threshold-based, PID, Fuzzy logic, Neural Network-based (ML), Adaptive control.
- **State machine logic** for switching between modes.

### Layer 4 — **Actuation / Output Layer**
Executes the commanded action.

- **Actuators:** DC motor, servo, stepper, pneumatic muscle, SMA (Shape Memory Alloy), functional electrical stimulator (FES).
- **Display/Audio outputs:** LCD, Braille display, speech synthesizer.

### Layer 5 — **Feedback & Safety Layer**
Closes the loop and ensures user protection.

- **Sensors:** Position, force, current, temperature.
- **Safety mechanisms:** Limit switches, emergency stop, software watchdog, current cutoffs.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for ESE)

> [!TIP]
> This table summarizes every critical formula in Module 4. **Memorize the variable definitions, not just the symbols** — KTU examiners frequently test definitions.

| # | Formula | Description / Variable Definitions | Engineering Application |
|---|---------|--------------------------------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$ | **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** — minimum sampling frequency to avoid aliasing | EMG/EEG signal digitization |
| 2 | $V_{\text{out}} = G \cdot (V_+ - V_-)$ | **Differential amplifier gain** — $G$ = gain, $V_+, V_-$ = inputs | EMG/ECG amplification |
| 3 | $P_{\text{out}} = \tau \cdot \omega$ | **Mechanical power** — $\tau$ = torque (Nm), $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s) | Motor sizing for wheelchair/prosthesis |
| 4 | $\tau = F \cdot r \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | **Torque from force** — $F$ = force (N), $r$ = lever arm (m), $\theta$ = angle | Joint torque in prosthetic knee |
| 5 | $E_{\text{spring}} = \frac{1}{2} k x^2$ | **Elastic PE** — $k$ = stiffness (N/m), $x$ = displacement (m) | Energy-storing prosthetic feet |
| 6 | $f_{\text{res}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$ | **Natural frequency** of spring-mass system | Prosthetic foot resonance tuning |
| 7 | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | **Common Mode Rejection Ratio (dB)** — $A_d$ = differential gain, $A_c$ = common-mode gain | Bio-signal amplifier quality |
| 8 | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (dB)** | Cochlear implant speech processing |
| 9 | $V = IR$ | **Ohm's Law** — for electrode-skin interface | Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES) |
| 10 | $I_{\text{stim}} = \frac{V_{\text{stim}}}{R_{\text{skin}} + R_{\text{electrode}}}$ | **Stimulation current** — bounded to < 20 mA for safety | FES / TENS device design |
| 11 | $E = mc^2$ (not used) | — | — |
| 12 | $P_{\text{peak}} = \frac{W_{\text{work}}}{\Delta t}$ | **Peak power during gait** — typically 2–3× average | Battery sizing for powered wheelchair |
| 13 | $\text{Cadence} = \frac{60}{T_{\text{stride}}}$ (steps/min) | **Cadence formula** — $T_{\text{stride}}$ = stride period (s) | Gait analysis |
| 14 | $\eta = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | **Efficiency** of actuator system | Battery life estimation |
| 15 | $R_{\text{ext}} = R_0 \cdot (1 + \alpha \Delta T)$ | **Strain gauge resistance change** — $\alpha$ = gauge factor | Force/pressure sensing in FSR |
| 16 | $F_{\text{grasp}} = \mu \cdot F_{\text{normal}}$ | **Friction-based grip** — $\mu$ = coefficient of friction | Prosthetic hand design |
| 17 | $\text{ROM} = \theta_{\text{max}} - \theta_{\text{min}}$ | **Range of Motion** of a joint | Orthosis prescription |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes application-level questions (Bloom's Level 3-4).** You must understand **why** these formulas are used, not just memorize them.

- **Nyquist Theorem** → directly determines the ADC chip selection cost. For a surface EMG (500 Hz bandwidth), a 1 kHz ADC suffices. For EEG (clinical, 100 Hz), 200 Hz is fine. Choosing unnecessarily high rates wastes power — critical in **wearable assistive devices**.
- **Mechanical Power** → a powered wheelchair must overcome rolling resistance, gradient, and acceleration. Sizing the motor for **peak power (2× normal)** is non-negotiable.
- **Energy-storing feet (Formula 5)** → the Seattle Foot and Flex-Foot use carbon-fiber springs; the **stored elastic energy** is released during toe-off, mimicking the gastrocnemius-soleus push-off.
- **CMRR** → bio-signals are buried in 50/60 Hz mains noise. A high CMRR (>80 dB) is the **single most important spec** for EMG/ECG amplifiers.
- **FES current bound (<20 mA)** → above this, ventricular fibrillation risk increases. This is a **safety-critical** engineering constraint.

---

## 2.4 Theoretical Principles Behind Each Technology Category

### 2.4.1 Mobility Aids — Wheelchair Engineering
- **Manual wheelchair propulsion biomechanics:** Effective force is applied at the pushrim.
- **Rolling resistance:** $F_{\text{rr}} = \mu_{\text{rr}} \cdot W$ where $W$ = weight on wheel.
- **Static stability condition** (anti-tip): $h < \frac{b}{2} \cdot \tan(\theta_{\text{slope}})$.

### 2.4.2 Prosthetics — Lower Limb
- **Sach Foot** (Solid Ankle Cushion Heel): Uses compressed rubber heel wedge for energy absorption.
- **Energy-storing feet**: Carbon fiber E-glass composite; tuned spring constant.
- **Prosthetic knee** classification: Mechanical (single-axis, polycentric), pneumatic, hydraulic, microprocessor-controlled (e.g., C-Leg, Genium).

### 2.4.3 Sensory Aids
- **Cochlear implant:** Converts acoustic signal → electrical stimulation of auditory nerve.
- **Braille display:** Pin-array refreshable (8-dot standard cell).
- **Screen reader (JAWS, NVDA):** TTS (Text-to-Speech) engine.

### 2.4.4 Rehabilitation Robotics
- **LOKOMAT, EKSO, ReWalk:** Gait training exoskeletons.
- **End-effector vs. Exoskeleton:** Different kinematic architectures.
- **Control modes:** Assist-as-needed, impedance control, admittance control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Nyquist Sampling Theorem for EMG Signal Acquisition

> [!NOTE]
> **Context:** This is a frequently asked 3–4 mark derivation in KTU. The student is expected to start from the aliasing condition and arrive at the sampling inequality.

### Step 1: Define the highest frequency component present in the EMG signal
The surface EMG (sEMG) signal has useful information in the frequency band of **20 Hz to 500 Hz**. Therefore, the maximum significant frequency is:

$$f_{\text{max}} = 500 \text{ Hz}$$

### Step 2: State the aliasing condition
When a continuous-time signal is sampled, frequencies above half the sampling rate will "fold back" into the lower spectrum, corrupting the original signal. To prevent this **aliasing**, the sampling frequency $f_s$ must satisfy:

$$f_s > 2 \cdot f_{\text{max}}$$

### Step 3: Substitute the EMG maximum frequency

$$f_s > 2 \times 500 \text{ Hz} = 1000 \text{ Hz}$$

### Step 4: Apply the engineering safety margin
In practice, engineers use a sampling rate **3 to 5 times** the maximum frequency to allow for the **roll-off of the anti-aliasing filter**. Therefore:

$$f_s = 3 \times f_{\text{max}} = 3 \times 500 = 1500 \text{ Hz} \quad (\text{typical})$$

### Step 5: Final selection and justification
Commercial EMG systems (e.g., Delsys Trigno, MyoWare) use sampling rates of **1000 Hz to 2000 Hz** to satisfy the Nyquist criterion with adequate margin.

$$\boxed{f_s^{\text{EMG}} \approx 1000 \text{ Hz to } 2000 \text{ Hz}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Stored and Returned by a Prosthetic Carbon-Foot

> [!NOTE]
> **Context:** Frequently tested as a 7-mark numerical problem in KTU 2024 scheme. The student must show units clearly and explain the physical meaning of every step.

### Step 1: Identify the spring constant of the carbon-fiber keel
A typical **Flex-Foot** style prosthetic foot has a keel stiffness of approximately:

$$k = 35{,}000 \text{ N/m}$$

(This is for a 70 kg adult; scaled for other masses.)

### Step 2: Determine the maximum vertical compression during mid-stance
During gait, the maximum vertical deflection (compression) of the keel is approximately:

$$x_{\text{max}} = 10 \text{ mm} = 0.010 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3: Compute the maximum elastic potential energy stored
Using the formula for elastic potential energy in a linear spring:

$$E_{\text{stored}} = \frac{1}{2} k x_{\text{max}}^2$$

$$E_{\text{stored}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 35{,}000 \times (0.010)^2$$

$$E_{\text{stored}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 35{,}000 \times 0.0001$$

$$E_{\text{stored}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 3.5$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{stored}} = 1.75 \text{ J}}$$

### Step 4: Interpret the result physically
This 1.75 J of energy is **returned** during toe-off, augmenting push-off. Compared to a SACH foot (which absorbs and dissipates this energy as heat), the energy-storing foot offers approximately **15–25% metabolic energy savings** to the amputee — a **measurable clinical benefit**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Battery Sizing for a Powered Wheelchair

> [!NOTE]
> **Context:** A common ESE numerical problem. The student must convert units carefully and apply safety/derating factors.

### Step 1: Define the design requirements
- Total mass of wheelchair + user: $M = 100 \text{ kg}$ (wheelchair ~30 kg, user ~70 kg).
- Required range on a single charge: $d = 30 \text{ km}$.
- Average speed: $v = 6 \text{ km/h}$.
- Rolling resistance coefficient: $\mu_{\text{rr}} = 0.015$ (hard tire on smooth surface).
- Drivetrain efficiency: $\eta = 0.75$ (75%).

### Step 2: Compute the rolling resistance force
The total weight on the wheels:

$$W = M \cdot g = 100 \times 9.81 = 981 \text{ N}$$

The rolling resistance force:

$$F_{\text{rr}} = \mu_{\text{rr}} \cdot W = 0.015 \times 981 = 14.715 \text{ N}$$

### Step 3: Compute the mechanical power required
Power required to overcome rolling resistance at cruise speed:

$$P_{\text{mech}} = F_{\text{rr}} \cdot v$$

Convert speed: $v = 6 \text{ km/h} = 6 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 1.667 \text{ m/s}$

$$P_{\text{mech}} = 14.715 \times 1.667 = 24.53 \text{ W}$$

### Step 4: Account for drivetrain efficiency
The electrical power drawn from the battery:

$$P_{\text{elec}} = \frac{P_{\text{mech}}}{\eta} = \frac{24.53}{0.75} = 32.7 \text{ W}$$

### Step 5: Compute total travel time
$$t = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{30{,}000 \text{ m}}{1.667 \text{ m/s}} = 18{,}000 \text{ s} = 5 \text{ hours}$$

### Step 6: Compute total energy required
$$E_{\text{bat}} = P_{\text{elec}} \times t = 32.7 \times 18{,}000 = 588{,}600 \text{ J}$$

Convert to Watt-hours:
$$E_{\text{bat}} = \frac{588{,}600}{3600} = 163.5 \text{ Wh}$$

### Step 7: Apply a derating factor for hills, acceleration, and battery aging
A standard derating factor of **2×** is used:

$$E_{\text{battery, rated}} = 2 \times 163.5 = 327 \text{ Wh}$$

### Step 8: Convert to Ampere-hours at 24 V system
$$\text{Capacity (Ah)} = \frac{327 \text{ Wh}}{24 \text{ V}} = 13.6 \text{ Ah}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Recommended battery: } 24 \text{ V}, \sim 15 \text{ Ah deep-cycle lead-acid or LiFePO}_4}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: FES Stimulation Current Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Context:** Safety-critical calculation. KTU examiners specifically test this.

### Step 1: Define the FES circuit parameters
- Stimulation voltage (constant voltage stimulator): $V_{\text{stim}} = 50 \text{ V}$ (typical).
- Skin-electrode impedance (after gel preparation): $R_{\text{skin}} = 1 \text{ k}\Omega = 1000 \text{ }\Omega$.
- Electrode-tissue impedance: $R_{\text{electrode}} = 500 \text{ }\Omega$.

### Step 2: Compute total series resistance
$$R_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{skin}} + R_{\text{electrode}} = 1000 + 500 = 1500 \text{ }\Omega$$

### Step 3: Compute the resulting current using Ohm's law
$$I_{\text{stim}} = \frac{V_{\text{stim}}}{R_{\text{total}}} = \frac{50}{1500}$$

$$\boxed{I_{\text{stim}} = 0.0333 \text{ A} = 33.3 \text{ mA}}$$

### Step 4: Safety evaluation
The result **33.3 mA > 20 mA threshold** for ventricular fibrillation risk. Therefore, a **constant-current stimulator** must be used instead, with the current capped at **20 mA** (or below) for transthoracic applications. For limb muscle stimulation, currents up to **100 mA** are acceptable, but the **stimulation waveform** must be **biphasic** to prevent DC accumulation.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Analysis of Lower Limb Prosthetic Knees

> [!NOTE]
> **Context:** This is a classic "compare and contrast" 7-mark question in KTU.

| Feature | Single-Axis Mechanical | Polycentric (4-bar) | Pneumatic | Hydraulic | Microprocessor (MPK) |
|---------|------------------------|---------------------|-----------|-----------|----------------------|
| **Mechanism** | Pivoting hinge | 4-bar linkage | Air piston | Oil cylinder | Sensor + processor + valve |
| **Swing Phase Control** | Friction brake | Geometric | Pneumatic damping | Hydraulic damping | Adaptive (real-time) |
| **Stance Phase Stability** | Manual lock | Geometric lock | None | Yes (yield) | Yes (terrain sensing) |
| **Cadence Range** | Single | 1–2 | Variable | Wide | Wide + automatic |
| **Weight (kg)** | 0.3–0.5 | 0.5–0.7 | 0.6–0.8 | 0.8–1.0 | 1.0–1.3 |
| **Cost (USD)** | 200–500 | 500–1500 | 1000–3000 | 3000–6000 | 8000–30000 |
| **Indication** | Slow walkers, geriatric | Moderate activity | Active walkers | Active, varied terrain | All, esp. transfemoral |
| **Examples** | Ottobock 3R15 | Ottobock 3R80 | Mauch SNS | Mauch Hydraulik | C-Leg, Genium, Rheo |
| **KTU-Mandated Discussion** | Mechanical principles | Kinematic analysis | Thermodynamics | Fluid mechanics | Control systems |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Rehabilitation Robots

| Robot | Type | Actuation | Control | Indication |
|-------|------|-----------|---------|------------|
| **LOKOMAT** | Exoskeleton (gait) | DC servo drives at hip & knee | Impedance / position control | Spinal cord injury, stroke |
| **EKSO GT** | Wearable exoskeleton | Electric motors at hip, knee, ankle | Pre-defined gait trajectories | SCI, paraplegia |
| **ReWalk** | Wearable exoskeleton | Battery-powered motors | Tilt sensor + motors at hip & knee | SCI (T7–L5) |
| **ARMEO Power** | Arm exoskeleton (end-effector) | Cable-driven | Gravity compensation | Stroke, MS |
| **InMotion ARM** | End-effector planar robot | Servo motors | Adaptive assist | Stroke rehabilitation |
| **HAL (Cyberdyne)** | Cyborg-type exoskeleton | Bio-electrical (EMG) sensing + motors | Voluntary intent-based | SCI, stroke |

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Screen Readers & AAC Software

| Software | Platform | TTS Engine | Language Support | Cost |
|----------|----------|------------|------------------|------|
| **JAWS** | Windows | Eloquence / Vocalizer | 30+ | Commercial |
| **NVDA** | Windows | eSpeak | 50+ | Free (open source) |
| **VoiceOver** | macOS / iOS | Apple TTS | 30+ | Bundled |
| **TalkBack** | Android | Google TTS | 50+ | Bundled |
| **Orca** | Linux | eSpeak / Festival | 30+ | Free |
| **Proloquo2Go** | iOS | Acapela | English, Spanish | Commercial (AAC) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: 5-Layer Architecture of an Assistive Technology System

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** showing how a user's intent becomes a device action through 5 distinct engineering layers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Biological Signal EMG EEG EOG] --> B[Layer 1 Input Sensing]
    A2[User Physical Input Joystick Sip Puff Switch] --> B
    B --> C[Layer 2 Signal Conditioning Amplify Filter ADC]
    C --> D[Layer 3 Decision Control]
    D --> E[Layer 4 Actuation Output]
    E --> F[Layer 5 Feedback and Safety]
    F --> D
    D --> G[User Receives Action Movement Sound Display]
    B --> H[Noise and Artifact Source 50Hz Mains EMG Crosstalk]
    H --> C
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: EMG-Controlled Prosthetic Hand — Block Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> Maps the signal path from muscle contraction to finger movement of a myoelectric prosthetic hand.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Residual Limb Muscles] --> P2[Surface EMG Electrodes]
    P2 --> P3[Differential Amplifier CMRR 80dB]
    P3 --> P4[Bandpass Filter 20 to 500 Hz]
    P4 --> P5[Rectifier and Smoothing]
    P5 --> P6[Microcontroller ADC and Feature Extract]
    P6 --> P7[Threshold and Pattern Classifier]
    P7 --> P8[Motor Driver H Bridge]
    P8 --> P9[DC Motor in Prosthetic Hand]
    P9 --> P10[Finger Movement Flexion Extension]
    P6 --> P11[Feedback Encoder and Current Sensor]
    P11 --> P6
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Cochlear Implant — Audio-to-Nerve Stimulation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Microphone Pickup] --> Q2[Sound Processor Behind Ear]
    Q2 --> Q3[Bandpass Filter Bank 22 Channels]
    Q3 --> Q4[Strategy Selection ACE CIS SPEAK]
    Q4 --> Q5[RF Transmission Link Coil to Coil]
    Q5 --> Q6[Implanted Receiver Stimulator]
    Q6 --> Q7[Electrode Array 22 Contacts in Cochlea]
    Q7 --> Q8[Auditory Nerve Stimulation]
    Q8 --> Q9[Brain Auditory Cortex Perception]
    Q2 --> Q1
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES) Cycle for Gait

> [!NOTE]
> Shows the closed-loop sequence from heel-strike detection to muscle activation in a drop-foot stimulator.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define "Assistive Technology" as per the WHO ICF framework and list any four categories of assistive technology devices used in rehabilitation.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Assistive Technology (AT) is any product, device, equipment, or software that is used to **maintain, increase, or improve the functional capabilities** of individuals with disabilities. As per WHO, AT enables **participation** in education, employment, and social life, and is a key **Environmental Factor** in the ICF model.

> **Four categories (1 mark — 0.25 each):**
> 1. **Mobility aids** — wheelchairs, crutches, walking frames.
> 2. **Prosthetics & Orthotics** — artificial limbs, braces.
> 3. **Sensory aids** — hearing aids, cochlear implants, Braille displays, screen readers.
> 4. **Communication aids (AAC)** — speech-generating devices, picture boards.
> 5. *(Alternative)* Cognitive aids, Environmental Control Systems.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a Prosthesis and an Orthosis with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Prosthesis | Orthosis |
|--------|------------|----------|
| **Purpose** | Replaces a **missing** body part | Supports/controls a **present but impaired** body part |
| **Example** | Below-knee artificial leg | Knee-ankle-foot orthosis (KAFO) |
| **User population** | Amputees | Persons with CP, polio, stroke, scoliosis |
| **Attachment** | Interfaces with residual limb via socket | Worn externally over the joint/limb |

> **Key distinction:** Prosthesis **replaces**, Orthosis **supports** (memory hook: **"P**rosthesis = **P**art missing; **O**rthosis = **O**riginal part weakened").

## 5.2 PART B Question A (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

### **Question A**
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **working principle of a myoelectrically controlled prosthetic hand**. Explain the role of:
- (i) Surface EMG electrodes
- (ii) Differential amplifier and bandpass filter
- **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **minimum sampling frequency** required to digitize a surface EMG signal whose useful bandwidth is 20 Hz to 500 Hz. State the Nyquist theorem and explain what happens if the sampling rate is insufficient. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A (a):**

#### **Working Principle of a Myoelectric Prosthetic Hand**

A myoelectric prosthetic hand is a **multi-layer technology solution** that translates the **electrical activity of residual muscles** into **prosthetic finger movement**.

#### **Step 1 — Bioelectric Signal Generation (1 mark)**
When the user attempts to **contract** a residual muscle (e.g., flexor digitorum in a below-elbow amputee), the muscle fibres generate action potentials. The **summated electrical activity** is known as the **electromyogram (EMG)**. This EMG signal has:
- Amplitude: **50 µV to 5 mV**
- Bandwidth: **20 Hz to 500 Hz**

#### **Step 2 — Surface Electrode Pickup (1 mark)**
**Surface EMG electrodes** (typically Ag/AgCl) are placed on the skin over the residual muscle. Two **active electrodes** (differential pair) and one **reference electrode** form the standard configuration. The electrode-skin interface uses conductive gel to **lower impedance** from ~50 kΩ to ~1–5 kΩ.

#### **Step 3 — Differential Amplification (1.5 marks)**
The EMG signal is amplified using an **instrumentation differential amplifier** with:
- **Gain (G):** 1000 to 10,000
- **CMRR > 80 dB** to reject 50/60 Hz mains interference

The output is:

$$V_{\text{out}} = G \cdot (V_+ - V_-)$$

#### **Step 4 — Bandpass Filtering (1.5 marks)**
A **bandpass filter (20 Hz – 500 Hz)** removes:
- Low-frequency baseline wander (motion artifact)
- High-frequency noise (electronic)

#### **Step 5 — Rectification, Smoothing & Feature Extraction (1 mark)**
The signal is full-wave rectified, smoothed (low-pass filter), and the **envelope (RMS or MAV)** is computed. This envelope represents muscle activation level.

#### **Step 6 — Microcontroller Decision (0.5 mark)**
The envelope is compared against a **threshold**. If above, the controller commands motor activation. **Pattern recognition** (with multiple electrode sites) can classify **multiple grip types** (e.g., pinch, lateral, power grip).

#### **Step 7 — Motor Actuation (0.5 mark)**
A **DC gear motor** in the prosthetic hand drives a **cable-tendon mechanism** or **direct-drive gear train** to flex/extend the fingers. The thumb may be opposable.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A (b):**

#### **Step 1: State the Nyquist Sampling Theorem (2 marks)**
> *"A bandlimited continuous-time signal can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency $f_s$ is greater than twice the highest frequency component $f_{\text{max}}$ present in the signal."*

Mathematically:

$$f_s > 2 \cdot f_{\text{max}}$$

#### **Step 2: Identify the maximum frequency (1 mark)**
For surface EMG, $f_{\text{max}} = 500 \text{ Hz}$.

#### **Step 3: Apply the theorem (2 marks)**

$$f_s > 2 \times 500 = 1000 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\boxed{f_{s,\text{min}} = 1000 \text{ Hz}}$$

In practice, a **3×–5× safety margin** is used, so $f_s = 1500$–$2500$ Hz is chosen.

#### **Step 4: Explain the consequence of insufficient sampling (2 marks)**
If $f_s < 2 f_{\text{max}}$, the phenomenon of **aliasing** occurs. High-frequency components **fold back** into the lower spectrum, causing **irreversible distortion** of the EMG signal. The prosthetic hand will then produce **erratic, unpredictable, and dangerous** movements.

**Example:** If $f_s = 800$ Hz, a 600 Hz EMG component appears as a **200 Hz false signal** ($f_{\text{alias}} = f_s - (f - f_s) = 800 - (600 - 800) = 200$ Hz), which the controller will misinterpret as a low-level muscle contraction.

---

## 5.3 PART B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

### **Question B**
**(a)** Explain the **classification of lower-limb prosthetic knees** with a comparison table. Discuss the working of a **microprocessor-controlled knee (MPK)** with a suitable block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **powered wheelchair** must carry a 100 kg user up an **8° slope** at **4 km/h** continuously for 1 hour. Calculate the **battery capacity (in Wh)** required if the overall drivetrain efficiency is 70%. State all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B (a):**

#### **Classification of Prosthetic Knees (4 marks)**

| Type | Principle | Pros | Cons |
|------|-----------|------|------|
| **Single-axis mechanical** | Hinge + friction brake | Simple, cheap, durable | Single cadence only |
| **Polycentric (4-bar)** | Instant Centre of Rotation | Stable stance, sit easily | Limited swing control |
| **Pneumatic** | Air piston damping | Variable cadence | Bulky, leaks possible |
| **Hydraulic** | Oil cylinder damping | Variable cadence + stance yield | Heavier, expensive |
| **Microprocessor (MPK)** | Sensors + processor + actuator | Real-time adaptive, stumble recovery, multi-mode | High cost, requires charging |

#### **MPK Block Diagram (2 marks) — describe verbally**

A microprocessor knee uses:
1. **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** — accelerometer + gyroscope at the knee axis.
2. **Strain gauges** on the shank pylon — measure load.
3. **Angle encoder** — measures knee flexion angle.
4. **Microprocessor** — runs real-time control algorithm (PID / state machine).
5. **Servo-valve** — controls hydraulic (or magneto-rheological fluid) flow.
6. **Battery** — typically Li-ion, lasts 24–48 hours per charge.

#### **Working Principle (1 mark)**
The IMU detects gait phase (stance/swing) and terrain. The microprocessor adjusts the **servo-valve opening**, which modulates the **hydraulic resistance** at the knee joint. On stairs/ramps, **higher resistance** is applied for stability; during swing, **lower resistance** for free movement.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B (b):**

#### **Step 1: List the given data (1 mark)**
- Total mass (user + wheelchair): $M = 100$ kg
- Slope angle: $\theta = 8°$
- Speed: $v = 4$ km/h
- Duration: $t = 1$ h = 3600 s
- Efficiency: $\eta = 0.70$
- Rolling resistance coefficient (assumed): $\mu_{\text{rr}} = 0.015$
- Gradient resistance: full gravity component (component along slope)
- Assume steady speed, no acceleration

#### **Step 2: Compute slope component of weight (1 mark)**

$$F_{\text{slope}} = M g \sin(\theta) = 100 \times 9.81 \times \sin(8°)$$

$$\sin(8°) \approx 0.1392$$

$$F_{\text{slope}} = 100 \times 9.81 \times 0.1392 = 136.5 \text{ N}$$

#### **Step 3: Compute rolling resistance (0.5 mark)**

$$F_{\text{rr}} = \mu_{\text{rr}} \cdot M g \cos(\theta)$$

$$\cos(8°) \approx 0.9903$$

$$F_{\text{rr}} = 0.015 \times 100 \times 9.81 \times 0.9903 = 14.57 \text{ N}$$

#### **Step 4: Total mechanical force (0.5 mark)**

$$F_{\text{total, mech}} = 136.5 + 14.57 = 151.07 \text{ N}$$

#### **Step 5: Convert speed to SI units (0.5 mark)**

$$v = 4 \text{ km/h} = 4 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 1.111 \text{ m/s}$$

#### **Step 6: Mechanical power output (1 mark)**

$$P_{\text{mech}} = F_{\text{total, mech}} \times v = 151.07 \times 1.111 = 167.8 \text{ W}$$

#### **Step 7: Electrical power required (1 mark)**

$$P_{\text{elec}} = \frac{P_{\text{mech}}}{\eta} = \frac{167.8}{0.70} = 239.7 \text{ W}$$

#### **Step 8: Total energy over 1 hour (1 mark)**

$$E_{\text{bat}} = P_{\text{elec}} \times t = 239.7 \times 1 = 239.7 \text{ Wh}$$

#### **Step 9: Apply a 25% derating margin for battery aging (0.5 mark)**

$$E_{\text{rated}} = 1.25 \times 239.7 = 299.6 \text{ Wh}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Battery capacity required} \approx 300 \text{ Wh} \quad (\text{e.g., 24 V, 12.5 Ah LiFePO}_4)}$$

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 (Technology Solutions):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Prosthesis with Orthosis** (–2 marks) — remember **P**rosthesis = **P**art missing; **O**rthosis = **O**riginal weak.
> 2. **Forgetting to state the Nyquist safety margin** (–1 mark) — always say *"in practice, 3× to 5× is used"*.
> 3. **Writing "voltage" instead of "current"** in FES calculations (–2 marks) — FES is **current-controlled**, and the safety limit is **20 mA** for cardiac applications.
> 4. **Skipping units in derivations** (–1 to –2 marks) — KTU examiners explicitly deduct for missing **N, m, J, W, Hz, A** units.
> 5. **Confusing dB formulas** — remember:
>    - **CMRR & SNR** use **20 log₁₀** for amplitude ratios and **10 log₁₀** for power ratios.
> 6. **Not mentioning efficiency ($\eta$) in battery/motor problems** (–2 marks) — every power question requires dividing by $\eta$.
> 7. **Calling a screen reader a "Braille device"** — they are different. Screen reader = audio output; Braille display = tactile pins.
> 8. **Writing "myoelectric" as "myoelectrical"** — both are accepted, but the standard term is **myoelectric**.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Technology Solutions**

- [x] **AT Definition** — WHO: *"Any device that maintains, increases, or improves functional capability of a person with disability."*
- [x] **ICF Model** — Disability = interaction of Body Functions, Activities, Participation, Environment, Personal Factors.
- [x] **Prosthesis vs Orthosis** — P = Part missing; O = Original weak.
- [x] **Nyquist** — $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$; in practice, **3–5×** margin.
- [x] **EMG bandwidth** — 20 Hz – 500 Hz → minimum $f_s = 1$ kHz, typically 1.5–2 kHz.
- [x] **EMG amplitude** — 50 µV to 5 mV; needs gain of 1000–10,000.
- [x] **CMRR** — should be > 80 dB for bio-amplifiers.
- [x] **FES safety current** — < 20 mA for transthoracic; biphasic waveform mandatory.
- [x] **Cochlear implant** — 22 electrodes, ACE/CIS/SPEAK strategies, RF transcutaneous link.
- [x] **SACH foot** — Solid Ankle Cushion Heel; energy-absorbing, not energy-returning.
- [x] **Energy-storing foot** — carbon fiber; returns ~1.75 J per step (for a 70 kg user, 10 mm deflection).
- [x] **Wheelchair battery** — typically 24 V; size in Wh = Power (W) × time (h) / efficiency.
- [x] **MPK** — C-Leg, Genium, Rheo; uses IMU + strain gauges + microprocessor + hydraulic/magneto-rheological actuator.
- [x] **AAC** — Augmentative (adds to existing speech) vs Alternative (replaces speech).
- [x] **Screen readers** — JAWS, NVDA, VoiceOver, TalkBack, Orca.
- [x] **Universal Design principles** (7 principles by Ron Mace) — Equitable use, Flexibility, Simple, Perceptible info, Tolerance for error, Low effort, Size/space.
- [x] **Standard wheelchair slope** — 8° manual, 10° powered (ISO 7176).
- [x] **Normal cadence** — ~100 steps/min; **stride time** = 1.0 s.
- [x] **Visual acuity standard** — 6/6 (20/20); resolving angle = 1 arc-minute.
- [x] **Audible range** — 20 Hz – 20 kHz; **speech band** — 300 Hz – 3.4 kHz.
- [x] **ECS** — Environmental Control System; single-switch / sip-and-puff controlled.
- [x] **LOKOMAT, EKSO, ReWalk** — names of common gait rehabilitation robots.
